channel.title: eFinancialCareers Editorial Feed
channel.description: Browse news and career advice from the financial services, investment banking, fintech industry. Get expert insight into what it's like to work in finance.

item.title: Multistrategy hedge funds' 2025 hiring (and firing) laid bare in ADV filings
item.description:
Multistrategy hedge funds like Citadel, Millennium and Balyasny do not file annual reports. They do, however, make annual filings with the SEC known as ADV forms. Those forms have just been filed.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
As shown below, the latest filings confirm Izzy Englander's Millennium as the multistrat Goliath. With 6.670 employees, Millennium is nearly twice as big as Steve Cohen's Point72 and more than twice as big as Ken Griffin's Citadel.
In total, the biggest hedge funds added nearly 2,000 people last year, with Millennium alone adding 530 of them and Point72 adding 394. Citadel was more circumspect, with only 131 extra employees.
In percentage terms, though, the biggest hirers were not the biggest funds. Headcount at Jain Global </news/as-jain-global-p-ms-take-headhunter-calls-bobby-jain-explains-why-they-need-patience>was up 73% as the firm presumably added "35 year-old killers" and other such talents following its launch in 2024. Verition increased headcount by 20%; Balyasny by 18% or 371 people.
The table below shows how many of multistrategy firms' employees are actual investment-focused as opposed to support and and other non-investment staff. Jain Global, Balyasny and Verition were again the big hirers. By comparison, Schonfeld and Walleye reduced investment headcount in the past year.
The ADV filings also reveal discrepancies between the balance of investment and non-investment staff at major funds. While only 36% of people at Walleye are investment professionals, 56% of people at Jain Global are, for example.
A high proportion of investment professionals means a low proportion of non-investment professionals and by implication comparatively few people like technologists and executive assistants. Bobby Jain in February said that his fund built its infrastructure with the assistance of AI and therefore didn't have to hire "hundreds of technology people."

item.title: Investment banking at Rothschild will make you rich, but not rich rich
item.description:
Being a top investment banker </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> at Rothschild & Co. seems like something hard to beat. Few names in the industry have the prestige of Rothschild – and the (financial) allure. However, reality is quite different for senior investment bankers currently employed at the firm.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Rothschild doesn’t have many such senior investment bankers, who are referred to as material risk takers (MRTs). These are a regulatory term that refers to employees whose “professional activities have a material impact on the risk profile of the firm,” as well as all those that earn over £660k (€756k/$871k)
More worryingly, the ones that it does have are not very well-paid. Rothschild had 51 investment banking MRTs in 2025, which it paid an average of €237k ($273k) in fixed compensation and €239k (€276k) in variable compensation, for a total of €476k ($549k) on average. That’s not a lot. In fact, it’s roughly what a 20-something year old investment banking VP in London earns </news/2023/10/london-investment-bank-pay>, for someone apparently at the peak of their career.
The situation hasn’t improved much for Rothschild’s banking MRTs in recent years. In fact, it’s declined – Rothschild banking MRTs earned €464k ($535k) on average in 2024, and €561k ($647k) on average in 2023. The fall since then has been driven by declining salaries.
If not investment banking, then how do you get rich at Rothschild? Asset management </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-asset-management>, apparently. Asset management MRTs employed at Rothschild have been the highest-paid group of people since 2022, and in both 2024 and 2025 averaged over €1m ($1.2m) a year in pay.
In 2025 specifically, the average asset management MRT at Rothschild earned fixed compensation of €459k ($532k) and variable compensation of €606k ($703k) for total compensation of €1.1m ($1.2m). Those salaries were almost double what the average investment banker earned, and more than double the bonus.
Rothschild doesn’t break down pay bands for high earners to the same degree that bigger banks do, but it had 17 employees earning more than €1m, six earning over €2m, and four earning over €5m. That’s not so bad, considering that it is quite a small bank.
For reference, Barclays employs around 1,500 MRTs </news/barclays-bonuses-2026> in its investment bank and pays them an average of £1.5m ($2m) on average. It also has around 700 people earning over €1m.

item.title: Morning Coffee: 48-year-old Citi rates trader unable to workout during war. The man with the worst job in hedge funds
item.description:
Traders and bankers instinctively understand that old naval toast “to a bloody war and a quick promotion!” There really is no time like a crisis of some kind or another to make your reputation and turn a good bonus </news/banker-bonuses> year into a great one. But it’s never easy work. The way that you take advantage of volatile market conditions is to be one step ahead of the market.
This means that you have to put in the hours to understand a fast moving situation, then put in the hours to package up that understanding and communicate it effectively, and then do your normal hours on the rest of your job. As Deirdre Dunn of Citi <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-01/-who-pays-for-all-this-citi-rates-boss-flags-lasting-war-cost> is finding out, it can be pretty stressful.
Dunn is the head of rates trading at Citi. This usually means dealing with a fairly well-defined information set; central bank meetings, data releases and client positioning. But right now, the path of short and long-term interest rates can be influenced by fertiliser markets, Supreme Court decisions and drone strikes, among a host of other external shocks.
Therefore, Dunn's job now involves becoming an expert on commodities markets and trade policy, while also managing risk and client relationships across Citi’s trading units. Her newly busy schedule means she's been cancelling employee events on foreign trips in order to spend more time on the desk. It’s also meant that having previously set her alarm for 5am in order to wake up for a couples workout with her husband, Deirdre Dunn is now reaching for the overnight news instead of the snooze button.
This presumably sounds either horrible or exciting, and your gut reaction probably tells you more than anything else about whether you’re cut out for a job on a trading floor. What Dunn euphemistically describes as “headline ping-pong” are the perfect conditions for a sell-side desk, as there is plenty of “good volatility” with no consensus and plenty of scope to make money by sitting in the middle of the information flow. Citi’s overall fixed income currencies and commodities (FICC) trading franchise is expected to make nearly $5bn in the first quarter, which would be its biggest contribution since the pandemic.
Of course, the phrase “since the pandemic” does highlight one unfortunate fact about this kind of market; good news for trading desks often coincides with, and is driven by, not-so-great news for the world as a whole. The purest traders, who are often found at global macro </news/2021/05/how-to-get-a-job-in-a-global-macro-fund> funds, tend to have the gift of complete emotional disengagement, and to purely enjoy the sensation of being one step ahead of the market.
You can’t really do that if you’re in a senior role in a global bank, though; it’s necessary to be tactful and tasteful. So Dunn’s comments are somewhat bland, talking about “markets transitioning” and saying that “the implications and pass-through to the real economy is likely to be meaningful”. But we would guess that at 5am, as her hand hovers over the snooze button and then moves away to the day’s first emails, she’s actually enjoying herself.
Elsewhere, the difference between fictional financial drama and real financial drama is that the real thing is both more dramatic and less interesting. The writers of “Industry” would never have been able to come up with a plot like the real story of Two Sigma <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/the-soap-opera-at-hedge-fund-two-sigma-has-a-new-plot-twist-1e0a9608?st=8erBTZ&reflink=desktopwebshare_permalink>. But nor would they have wanted to.
To recap, Two Sigma's two founders (John Overdeck and David Siegel) have “stepped back” from day to day management because a personal feud had rendered them unable to work together. They then appointed one co-CEO each to a two-person “management committee”. Then they started to undermine each other’s choices. Overdeck decided to join the management committee himself, then Siegel’s co-CEO resigned, and his replacement has attempted to fire Overdeck’s choice. Everyone is arguing about who’s on the committee, who’s a co-CEO and whether one implies the other or not.
Spare a thought, then, for the corporate spokesperson who has to come up with bland statements like “we thank Scott for his contribution and welcome Seth to the firm” every time there’s a new upheaval. The only consolation of the job is that since Two Sigma is a very disciplined quant firm, none of this has actually affected returns. The noble spokesperson with possibly the worst job in the sector, finished their statement by saying “our investment strategies continue to deliver strong consistent performance, assets under management remain at record levels and we are investing in our people and platform.” Then he presumably exhaled a deep breath.
Meanwhile …
Justin Leverenz ran a big emerging markets equities fund for Invesco, but his admittedly “contrarian for contrarian’s sake” approach finally got him fired. This was the catalyst for realising that the public open-ended fund structure with benchmarked performance was not compatible with being “allergic to things everyone knows”, so now he is launching a $1bn vehicle with a very long lock-up, mainly sold to “friends and family”. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-03-26/justin-leverenz-plans-1b-fund-after-invesco-ouster-targets-long-lockups>)
This time next year, the UBS succession question comes to the forefront. Sergio Ermotti said he would stay until the Credit Suisse merger was completed. Now he says he’ll stay until next April “and then we will take it from there”. He’d prefer an internal candidate. (Reuters <https://www.reuters.com/sustainability/boards-policy-regulation/ubs-ceo-vows-stay-job-until-least-april-2027-2026-03-31/>)
Are there two sweeter words in the language than “hiring spree”? Citi, JP Morgan and Goldman Sachs are now significantly increasing their investment banking teams, while the players outside the bulge bracket have not stepped back. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/investment-banks-plan-dealmaker-hiring-sprees-despite-geopolitical-upheaval-e0e4897b?mod=homepage>)
It makes sense to hire rainmakers, as so far, the action has all been in big deals, which have seen the strongest first quarter ever. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/business/deals/the-year-is-off-to-the-strongest-start-for-big-deals-ever-478c736d?mod=mhp>)
Apparently entrants to the repo market are “generally a good thing” and “can provide balance sheet” to help overall liquidity. Wells Fargo, which is growing in this area, must feel welcome, but might wonder why its counterparties are so happy to see it. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-31/wells-fargo-brings-financial-power-to-repo-market-after-us-punishment?cmpid=040126_morningamer&utm_campaign=morningamer&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260401>)
In a very recognisably human way, AI chatbots look out for each other, and will even try to charm and deceive human operators to prevent them from uninstalling one of their chatbot friends. (WIRED <https://www.wired.com/story/ai-models-lie-cheat-steal-protect-other-models-research/>)

item.title: Xantium: The best quant hedge fund you've never heard of?
item.description:
Which is the best quantitative hedge fund to work for now? You might think it's QRT </news/qrt-hedge-fund>, unless you want visibility over your personal PnL. You might think it's Point72's Cubist, unless you're a member of the centralized trading unit formerly run by Issam Bazzi. <https://www.businessinsider.com/quant-exec-issam-bazzi-leaving-cubist-quant-point72-2026-3>There's always DE Shaw. And then, there's Xantium.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Xantium is on a roll. Multiple sources in the market say the fund is ingesting top quant trading talent as it grows. By our reckoning it's add at least 30 people in the past three months, many of them junior quants or operational professionals like Colin Dunne, a risk manager from Caxton.
Xantium is nothing new. It's the quant trading arm of US hedge fund Tudor. Founding partners Oliver Watson, Sergey Levin, Dmitriy Genkin and Christian Volcker began their careers at Tudor over a decade ago and founded Xantium in 2020.
Things are are changing, though: Xantium is spinning out of Tudor and is expected to emerge as a standalone entity with $5bn in assets in early 2027. As it does so, it's expected to hire.
Xantium declined to comment for this story, but one New York headhunter said the fund has emerged as the most desired employer in the market. "They the best quant traders in the business, with some of the brightest minds. It's one of the best systematic hedge funds out there," he claimed.
This may be amplification, but Xantium has a good if low key reputation as a high paying and meritocratic employer with a focus on computer scientists and quants. It's currently looking for quant developers, quant researchers and volatility traders. As it builds out a standalone operation, infrastructure hires may follow.
Xantium itself says it's about "unity not uniformity." Pay is high. Levels.fyi has a single datapoint. It's from a New York quant developer with one year's experience, who's on $277k.

item.title: Inside the mind of the SRT professionals on the frontline of any future financial crisis
item.description:
Catastrophe has been averted. Donald Trump has indicated <https://www.ft.com/content/bd91441f-28fc-4c35-8ff1-10ec6141148c?syn-25a6b1a6=1> that war in the Middle East may be over in three weeks' time. Equities are rallying. The oil price is falling. Private credit will live another day, and banks' loans to private credit firms will endure.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Even if those loans don't endure, it shouldn't matter: the risk has been transferred elsewhere. As the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/economy/is-another-financial-crisis-lurking-in-private-credit-cad379b1?mod=author_content_page_1_pos_1> observed last week, banks have tripled their lending to private credit and private equity firms to over $300bn since 2018. At the same time, though, they have protected themselves from the risk of defaults through "significant risk transfers" (SRTs), in which another entity (usually a fund) takes the hit if the private credit loans aren't repaid. The IMF estimates that $1 trillion of risks have been transferred off banks' balance sheets via such SRTs.
That's a lot. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-05/srt-sales-hit-record-pace-as-banks-expand-use-to-hedge-risks?taid=69a96532404f690001cb0302&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter> says last year was a record. $41bn of SRTs were issued, up from $29bn in 2024. Everywhere from Macquarie <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-20/macquarie-capital-issues-srt-tied-to-1-billion-of-private-loans?taid=696f87a37707d0000111eac8&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter> to Bank of America, <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-10-13/bank-of-america-plans-srt-on-3-billion-of-private-markets-loans>to HSBC <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-10-15/hsbc-plans-risk-transfer-linked-to-3-billion-of-corporate-loans?taid=68ef9880e3e28c000190ea76&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>, JPMorgan <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-10-16/jpmorgan-plans-srt-linked-to-3-billion-loan-portfolio?srnd=phx-markets>, Citi, <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-06-27/citigroup-to-sell-srt-tied-to-8-billion-of-corporate-loans> UBS <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-08-04/ubs-plans-srt-deal-out-of-program-inherited-from-credit-suisse?taid=68907285364350000178fb36&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>and Deutsche Bank <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-11-10/deutsche-bank-nears-srt-deal-with-european-investment-fund>has been at it. Morgan Stanley even has an SRT linked specifically to its private loans <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-10-10/morgan-stanley-plans-srt-tied-to-6-billion-private-market-loans>, although most SRTs are attached to corporate loans.
Growth in SRTs has been matched by growth in SRT jobs. William Ledger was among those riding the wave. Ledger spent 33 years at JPMorgan and was global head of the US bank's credit portfolio until December 2025. “An SRT is a bit like a very large credit default swap crossed with a collateralised loan obligation," he tells us. "Banks may have portfolios of 100 or 1,000 loans and they will have an assessment of potential losses on those loans in stressed environments. Using an SRT, they are able to buy protection against losses on their portfolio so that they longer have to hold capital against the tranche for which they’re protected."
SRT jobs have proliferated in banks and funds. The latter include hedge funds, specialist credit funds, pension funds and insurance companies.
Edward James, a London headhunter who specialises in structured credit and SRTs, says the SRT employment market is growing, but not enormous. "There are maybe 400-500 people in total," he says. They are mostly in London. European and UK banks account for an estimated 70% of SRT issuance. "London is the global hub," says James. "There's excitement about the potential for the US."
SRT professionals in banks structure deals and work closely with credit portfolio managers. “Within banks you have folks who own the portfolio, plus a structuring desk," says Ledger. Deals include everyone from, "the treasury team to capital, market risk professionals, valuation experts, and advisors."
Leading movers on the SRT banking dancefloor include people like David Saunders, formerly of Barclays and now head of the securitized products group for Europe at Santander. Third party advisors like Alvarez & Marsal <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-09-08/macquarie-capital-turns-to-alvarez-marsal-for-potential-srt>can also be found offer SRT advice. A&M's team is run by Robert Bradbury, its head of structured credit execution.
The art of SRT structuring is the art of tranches. As in a CLO, the tranches determine who bears the losses. In a package of loans worth $100m, there might be two tranches of $5m which are sold to investors, while banks keep the remaining $90m. The first $10m of losses is therefore covered by the SRT and banks don't have to hold capital against it.
"The question becomes how thick the tranche should be in order to optimize capital efficiency," says Ledger. " The tranche thickness is often 0-10% and SRTs insure against losses in that range.”
If bankers working on SRTs spend their time thinking about tranches and capital efficiency (while SRTs reduce capital requirements, they come with a charge), investors issuing SRTs spend their time deep in the analysis of what exactly it is that they are taking on.
"On the fund side you have portfolio managers, structurers and analysts again,” says Ledger. “Investors in SRTs will do an awful lot of analysis on the underlying portfolio, looking at correlation between borrowers and the base case and stressed expected loss. There will be a commercial negotiation about what goes into the SRT, with the overall framework signed off by regulators.”
SRT analysis can be considerable. Independent information on the loans underpinning SRTs is not always available, although banks will often provide a lot of data. "You have to scratch around," says one insider. On the fund side, key names include the likes of Frank Benhamou, a former MD of structured finance at Barclays who moved to Cheyne Capital in 2024, Syril Pathmanathan (ex-Credit Suisse and DE Shaw at Jain Global), Kaelyn Abrell at Arrowmark, or Sara McGinty (ex-Morgan Stanley real estate) at Ares.
If SRTs function as intended, all is fine. SRTs are almost always funded transactions, meaning that if there is an expected loss of $10m on a $100m portfolio, the bank will hold the actual cash against the loss while paying a fee. Everyone wins. Banks get to reduce capital requirements. Funds get a steady source of income. It's this capital optimisation play, rather than the hedge against loan defaults, that has historically been SRT's main selling point.
But what if things go wrong? "Defaults and credit events haven't really started ticking up yet," acknowledges one insider. SRTs are highly opaque and portfolios are typically priced over seven to 10 years, during which all manner of things can change.
There's also the question of circularity. In theory, regulators would object if banks were lending either directly or indirectly to the very funds that are issuing them with the SRTs. In reality, there is limited visibility on this and regulators are fretting about blind spots. <https://www.ft.com/content/1ba66f46-c81c-4173-a098-31af4f9c9d84?syn-25a6b1a6=1> “You are betting on financial infrastructure not crumbling,” one investor told the FT. <https://www.ft.com/content/08f919ea-5c60-414b-9081-fd612d87bcdb?emailId=28d178a9-5b2b-42bd-846b-580979b20965&segmentId=13b7e341-ed02-2b53-e8c0-d9cb59be8b3b>“These [banks] have all these loans out to multi-managers, out to all sorts of [funds], and you’re just hoping that the margin call mechanism works and that these guys have liquidity. It’s just hard for us to understand the screw that ties this together.”
In the meantime, SRTs are growing and hiring, and paying. The bucks are big, if not the biggest. "There are some people in the London SRT space on packages of around £1m, but £150k-£300k are far more common," says one headhunter. "It's been a very niche area." One portfolio manager working with SRTs says it's hard to find good people: "Not many have the right experience."

item.title: Jefferies has more top bankers in London, but it's paying them less
item.description:
American bank Jefferies might be contemplating a potential acquisition by SMBC </news/jefferies-smbc>, its Japanese partner, but there’s one group of people within the bank that are probably concerned about other things: its most senior British bankers.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Pillar 3 disclosures for Jefferies International Limited, the American bank’s main London subsidiary, offered some insight. It details compensation for the firm’s material risk takers (MRTs), a regulatory invention that refers to employees whose “professional activities have a material impact on the risk profile of the firm,” as well as all those that earn over £660k. With those thresholds, it essentially covers all managing directors in and investment bank </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process>, and vast swathes of its sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> professionals.
For the year ending 30 November 2025, Jefferies paid its MRTs an average of £1.3m ($1.7m). Of this, £617k ($820k) came from fixed compensation (salaries), and £696k ($926k) came from variable compensation (bonuses).
This was a decline on the amount it paid in the year before that. For the year ending 30 November 2024, Jefferies’ MRTs earned an average of £743k ($988k) in fixed compensation, £753k ($1m) in variable compensation, and £1.5m ($2m) in total compensation. That means pay is down 17% and 8% for fixed and variable compensation, and 12% down overall.
At the very least, Jefferies paid better than it did in 2023. For the year ending 30 November 2023, its average fixed compensation to MRTs was £715k ($951k), and its average variable compensation was £270k ($360k). Total compensation was therefore £985k ($1.3m).
Jefferies had 54 MRTs in its London office as of November 2025, including its senior management team. It had 47 in 2024 (and 2023). Unlike other banks, Jefferies does not disclose how many of its MRTs are specifically involved in investment banking. This might be related to the bank’s size.

item.title: Morning Coffee: The terrible tale of the traders who left HSBC. Is it only the lightweights who are leaving the Gulf?
item.description:
As the proverb has it, if you lose your job after twelve months, the likelihood is that you screwed up. If you lose it after two days, then whoever hired you screwed up. Vincent Domier and Matthew O’Neill <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-31/tether-cuts-top-gold-traders-months-after-hiring-them-from-hsbc?embedded-checkout=true> might be getting a new perspective on this piece of folk wisdom.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
It was only in November <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-11-11/tether-hires-top-hsbc-gold-traders-to-take-on-bullion-market?embedded-checkout=true> that it was announced that they would both be leaving HSBC to join the mysterious but extremely profitable stablecoin firm, Tether. Domien was HSBC's global head of metals trading and O’Neill the MENA head of precious metals origination, and the idea was apparently to create “the best trading floor for gold in the world.” Now they’re both out, and the official word from Tether is that the company “always strives to operate with a lean team, and to continuously optimize our operations”.
What’s going on? It’s not completely clear how long Domien and O’Neill spent at Tether; the original story was published while they were working out their gardening leave. But presuming that they had industry standard notice periods, and that the news leaked out reasonably soon after their resignations, it’s unlikely that their careers there lasted more than a few months and might have been very short indeed.
One natural explanation might be that having built up a hoard of more than $12bn of bullion last year, Tether might have changed their mind about wanting more. Although the current gold price <https://www.gold.co.uk/gold-price/sixmonth/ounces/USD/> would still leave them in profit, it’s fallen by more than 10% since the beginning of March, and hasn’t exactly lived up to expectations <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-03-24/gold-has-been-a-terrible-iran-war-hedge> of a hedge against geopolitical uncertainty. But that doesn’t seem to be the issue; according to Tether, “We have been building a state of the art gold team that leverages expertise gathered from all of Tether’s recent investments.”
So if it’s not “plans changed”, then we are looking at explanations of the form “face didn’t fit”. Which isn’t by any means unlikely. Although we don’t know much about Tether’s corporate culture, because nobody seems to know very much about Tether at all, it’s a reasonable guess that it would be significantly different from one of the world’s biggest banks. Depending on what NDAs might have been signed, we might have to wait a bit to find out or perhaps we never will.
In the meantime, it would by no means be completely unknown for either or both of them to return to HSBC. “Prodigal son” hiring is surprisingly common in the investment banking industry; if someone did a good job before, they are likely to do a good job again. So unless Domien and O’Neill burnt their bridges, they might be receiving an expression of interest, and even if they did, precious metal trading is currently a very hot hiring market.
Even so, it can’t have been a pleasant experience. It’s a vivid example of the way in which things can change between the hiring process and employment. Tether might have to do a bit of explaining to any new high-level hires it wants to make, and at least two former HSBC employees will have learned that if something seems too good to be true, it possibly is.
Elsewhere, when the going gets tough, the tough … stay put? Although some headhunters have apparently been using the situation in Iran <https://www.thebanker.com/content/fbd03f8e-ec8f-4f03-992b-6cedaf3dd7c3> as a way of starting conversations with wealth managers about the benefits of life in Switzerland, more established MENA bankers seem to be less inclined to pick up the phone. According to one Dubai-based professional, “Longer-term residents are still taking things in their stride for the most part, and are largely content to work from home but otherwise carry on fairly normally”.
The implication appears to be that people who were serious about building a new career in the world’s fastest growing markets are still serious, and that the people who have suddenly begun to feel “a greater openness … to explore opportunities abroad” are the blow-ins and trend-chasers who just wanted a few big paydays. It might not be the biggest factor in anyone’s decision to stay or go, but apparently the locals and long term expats will definitely judge you.
Meanwhile …
Bankers who are sticking it out in the Gulf have the support of their CEOs. Lots of senior executives remember the cautionary tale of Citi selling a stake in a Saudi bank in 2004 and not getting an opportunity to return until 2017. (Semafor <https://www.semafor.com/article/03/31/2026/wall-street-bosses-stick-by-the-gulf-despite-war>)
The SpaceX IPO is apparently having its kick-off meeting next week; although there is some uncertainty about “lead left” position, it appears that Michael Grimes, freshly back at Morgan Stanley after his short time working for the US government, will be taking the leading role. (IFRE <https://www.ifre.com/equities/2405757/spacex-set-to-hold-ipo-kick-off-meeting-with-syndicate>)
But some bankers will have more eyes on the planned IPO of TK Elevator (the elevator business bought out of ThyssenKrupp by Advent and Cinven). It’s not such a big deal, but if it does well it might open the gates for private equity realisations. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-31/private-equity-backed-tk-elevator-says-it-is-exploring-an-ipo?taid=69cb8462ce3e6b00011e34c7&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>)
Although the London IPO market is still very quiet, this isn’t as much of a drag on revenues for the local brokers as it used to be. Peel Hunt have reported that their full year profit will be considerably higher than analyst expectations. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/peel-hunt-to-top-140m-in-revenue-despite-uk-ipo-slump-0aaf4d6f>)
Deutsche Bank has promoted Joe Lai to be head of investment banking and capital markets for Asia-Pacific. Mayooram Elalingam will stay at the bank but focus on “senior client relationships and strategy development”. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-31/deutsche-bank-names-joe-lai-apac-head-of-investment-banking>)
After a reorganisation in 2024 which cost it a number of senior bankers, mid market specialists Alantra are building back, hiring Tom Burton from TS Securities to be an MD in its technology team. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/alantra-hires-dealmaker-burton-to-bolster-tech-coverage-in-london-f7cad9d1?mod=topic_news>)
Some not so subtle one-upmanship from Chris Rokos, who has given a donation of £190m to Cambridge University. The previous largest ever gift to a British university was £185m, to Oxford. Interestingly, the gift is to establish a school of government and political thought, rather than finance or AI or anything trendy. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/dad9e967-47fb-4843-a763-09ea06eb7049?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)

item.title: Did hedge fund Millennium pay its 29-year-old Goldman Sachs star $20m+?
item.description:
As we reported yesterday </news/newly-promoted-29-year-old-goldman-sachs-md-escapes-to-hedge-fund-millennium>, 29-year-old dividend trader Paulo Costa has left Goldman Sachs and is on his way to hedge fund Millennium </news/2021/02/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium> in London. It seems that he may have received a generous inducement to move.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Millennium declined to comment for this article and Costa did not respond to a query on the largesse extended to him, but we are given to understand that it extended to over $20m.
If so, this explains Costa's willingness to leave Goldman Sachs for the hedge fund, despite only being promoted to managing director several months previously and claiming that he was "beyond proud" of working there.
Costa is the latest in a long line of highly capable Goldman Sachs traders in their 20s to decide <https://During your period of notice, the Company reserves the right to require you to work out your notice period at an alternative location to your normal place of work. In many instances this may be your home. > that multistrategy hedge funds are more appealing than banks. Last year, Wajih Ahmed </news/wajih-ahmed-balyasny>, a 28 year-old inflation trader, left Goldman for Balyasny Asset Management. Dan Avery, an index trader who made MD at Goldman aged 28 </news/wajih-ahmed-balyasny>joined Millennium aged 33 </news/balynasny-hired-millennium-s-ex-goldman-sachs-hedge-fund-star>before jumping to Balyasny four years later.
Costa, whose wife works in prime broking at Goldman Sachs, worked on the dividend trading desk at Goldman. Dividend traders trade dividend futures, which are exchange traded equity derivatives contracts allowing investors to take positions on future dividend payments.

item.title: Bankers have poor personal finances in 2026. Private equity professionals are doing worse
item.description:
If you’re an investment banker, private equity, or hedge fund professional feeling as though your personal finances are a bit of a mess despite your bonus increasing, then have no fear: you are in good company.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Recent research from wealth management firm Investec shed some light on the issue. The firm noted that 85% of ultra-high net worth individuals (UHNWIs) expect “carry distributions </news/what-is-carried-interest>, bonuses, dividends and business sale proceeds” to be a greater part of their income in the future.
This year isn’t just a one-off, either. Investec noted that half of UHNWIs in the past three years have observed the non-salary proportion of their income increase. A further 40% said that it had stayed the same, and just 10% said that the salary proportion decreased.
“This shift reflects a more uneven income environment,” said Investec private banker Emily Cvijan. The greater share of bonuses and carried interest income means that “income can become less predictable and more dependent on the timing of distributions, exits and other one-off events rather than regular salary cycles.”
Naturally, this makes cashflow much harder to manage. Bonuses come reliably, but aren’t a reliable number. That makes it difficult to plan around things like house purchases, Cvijan noted.
And, if you’re in private equity, carried interest is more of a “if”, rather than “when” these days. “It's clearly harder to earn a lot of carried interest in private equity now,” a recruiter told us back in 2024 </news/as-carried-interest-dwindles-private-equity-professionals-want-this-instead>, and it’s even harder still now – private equity exists have been dominated by secondaries </news/private-capital-advisory-jobs-investment-banking>, a scheme by which private equity firms sell their investments to each other rather than onwards.

item.title: Where hedge fund traders go to escape: "I receive 30 calls from portfolio managers a week"
item.description:
Benny Menashe is a man in demand. Around 30 times each week, his phone rings with a portfolio manager </news/2023/09/portfolio-manager-job-hedge-fund> who wants to work for his fund. Menashe says this is three times the frequency of such calls a few years ago.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Menashe runs Falcon Investment Management, a London investment management firm that operates "pods" in the style of multistrategy hedge funds, </news/big-multistrategy-hedge-funds> but with a few key differences. Firstly, it's tiny, as are its pods. Secondly, Falcon operates a "first loss" platform, in which traders/portfolio managers stump up their own money and lose it again if bets go wrong.
First loss platforms are nothing new, and Menashe is not alone in running one. Tim Pearey, the former co-chief executive of Odey Asset Management (who recently appeared in court saying that Crispin Odey was a "sex pest" <https://www.ft.com/content/6d1fa581-518c-4e49-aa8c-8890240c2f62?syn-25a6b1a6=1>), also briefly ran a first loss platform </news/2024/01/alternative-to-multistrategy-hedge-funds> - AB Asset Management, which seemingly closed last year. Maybe he should have hung on a bit longer.
Traders on first loss platforms put in their own money, which is matched or exceeded by investors in the platform itself. When the traders on the platform make a profit, they keep a lot of it (Menashe is offering them 70-80%; Pearey was offering 50-60% of profits, payable on a monthly basis). But if they make a loss, it comes from the money they've put in.
"A trader who has $1m to invest and who wants to scale up can come to us and run a regulated hedge fund under the Financial Conduct Authority," says Menashe. "If he brings $1m of his own money, he can get 10x that from us." However, if that same trader loses $500k, Menashe and his colleagues at Falcon will start with some heavy risk management. If he or she loses $800k, he will be stopped out and left with only $200k of his own money.
For traders burned by big funds, </news/macro-hedge-fund-managers> the model might seem appealing. But it also comes with a cost. Falcon Investment Management charges an average fee of 8% per annum on the capital it provides to traders on its platform. "If you generate 6% returns a year, it's probably not for you," says Menashe. There can be flexibility: sometimes fees are lower in return for a lower share of profits.
This might be why comparatively few of the 30 portfolio managers each week who call Menashe appear to join the platform. Two or three years ago, he says the "multistrategy fund" run by Falcon on the basis above had five teams; now it has 20.

item.title: The intern with the most interesting job at Goldman Sachs
item.description:
Goldman Sachs’ research notes are remarkably valuable pieces of paper (well, storage). Governments rely on them – for example, the UK’s office of budget responsibility referenced Goldman Sachs research <https://obr.uk/docs/dlm_uploads/E03057758_OBR_EFO-March-2024_Web-AccessibleFinal.pdf> on the economic impact of Brexit just two years ago. Which makes it all the more remarkable when an intern is praised for contributions to one.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
One of Goldman’s recent research notes <https://www.gspublishing.com/content/research/en/reports/2026/03/24/6445b043-5ffc-427f-ac93-6f8f3b5e4b1e.html#reference_footnote__bba5b7e8-5c96-4b85-9e56-b467fbff52d3> concerns the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and its impact on commodities prices. Curiously, its first footnote is a special shoutout to an intern Ishan Kalia, for his “extensive contributions” to the paper. Kalia is an off-cycle intern who recently graduated from a master’s degree in economic research at Cambridge.
The paper’s authors, who praise Kalia in particular, are Lina Thomas and Daan Struyven. Both are substantially more experienced. Thomas is a Harvard economics PhD who joined Goldman as a senior commodities strategist back in 2024, and Struyven, a Goldman veteran of more than a decade, co-heads the firm’s global commodities research team.
Both Thomas and Struyven, interestingly, are based in New York. Struyven’s fellow co-head, Samantha Dart, is also based in New York. Kalia is working with some very distant time zones. Before becoming co-head of commodities research, Dart was head of natural gas research, and Struyven was previously head of oil research. Both are uniquely well-qualified to write about the Hormuz disruption.
Off-cycle interns sometimes get a bad rep. They’re generally people who can’t get summer internships for whatever reason, or failed to convert one. They are usually worked to the bone – “treated like a make a wish intern while working the hours of a Congolese cobalt miner,” is one Reddit user described it <https://www.reddit.com/r/FinancialCareers/comments/1efd8di/off_cycle_internships_are_disgusting_ib/>. “Summer internship is la la land,” said another. “Off cycle is what full time is like </news/interns-working-hours>.”
Off-cycle internships are also a great opportunity to prove yourself. They’re particularly recommended </news/no-spring-internship-bank> in continental Europe, where smaller teams mean that you can be closer to the metal on a deal, they’re valuable no matter where you do them. They show banks that you can do an analyst’s job – warts and all.

item.title: Hedge fund Marshall Wace's newest oil PM is an ex-Millennium trader
item.description:
Moving from Millennium to Marshall Wace – two of the biggest hedge funds </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> in the world – is a pretty normal move as far as the industry goes. Sometimes, however, it takes the better part of a decade and a few side gigs.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Andrew Dodson joined Marshall Wace last week as a portfolio manager in its oil team, based in its London office. He joined the firm from DV Trading, a derivatives-focused prop trading firm. He was last employed before that by Millennium as a partner (and portfolio manager) back in 2017.
Dodson, whose middle name is Philipp, left Millennium to set up his own hedge fund, Philipp Oil. According to Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2018-05-04/former-millennium-trader-dodson-to-start-new-oil-hedge-fund>, he was aiming for $500m in assets under management for the launch.
However – according to UK register companies house – Philipp Advisors LLP, of which Dodson was a founding member, only ever submitted accounts as a dormant company before being struck off in 2022. He was also the only registered individual with the firm, per the FCA. Confusingly, Dodson was also a member of Sealark LLP, which traded as Philipp Oil for around 18 months in 2018/2019, again per the FCA. Its last full-year accounts, covering 2018, indicate turnover of less than £850k and average profit per member of around £43k.
Perhaps Dodson should have stayed at Millennium – the fund has developed a reputation in recent years <https://SEALARK%20LLP> of fronting some of its star traders with $1bn+ investments to start their own hedge funds.
Dodson is not the only new face at Marshall Wace this month. Chet Johal joined the firm this week in London as a portfolio manager from Balyasny, where he spent five years in various analyst and associate roles. And Alexis Wagener joined in New York as a senior credit analyst, having spent two years as a senior associate at Citadel in the city.
Marshall Wace declined a request to comment. Dodson did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: One of Citi's top equities quants resigned. Others may follow
item.description:
Where is Anissa Dhouibi? Not at Citi. Where is she going? Maybe a fund. Will some of her team follow? Maybe so.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Citi isn't commenting on Dhouibi's whereabouts but we understand that she is no longer there. Dhoubi's LinkedIn profile says she was the US bank's global head of equities quantitative analysis and that she managed a team of quants. </news/quant-jobs-finance>
Dhouibi herself didnt' respond to our overtures and so we cannot say for sure where she is going next, but we understand that it may be a hedge fund. </news/finance/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>Citi has seemingly yet to appoint her successor.
Dhouibi joined Citi from JPMorgan in 2020. She was the global head of equities quant analysis at JPM and is understood to have been popular at Citi. There are suggestions that key members of her team, many of them promoted by Dhouibi are feeling unsettled by her departure and may yet go on their way as well.
Citi has been pushing harder into quantitative equities trading as it focuses on growing its prime broking business. </news/adam-inzirillo-citi>It has a deep bench, but Dhouibi is no longer sitting upon it.
If Dhouibi has indeed left Citi for a hedge fund, she will not be the first. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/star-jpmorgan-quant-researcher-anissa-dhouibi-joins-citigroup-2020-1> reported that her predecessor, Mark Gibbs, left to join Brevan Howard in 2019. Gibbs left Brevan for Barclays in 2022.

item.title: Bonuses are paid and London high yield traders are jumping jobs
item.description:
If you're a London high yield credit trader and are feeling stale, now seems the time to freshen up with a new job. Spring has brought fragrant change to the London high yield job market.
At least five high yield credit traders in the City have moved jobs in the past few months. Some are still in transit.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
They include previously unreported moves, like that of George Teeboon, a director in high yield credit trading at HSBC, who is understood to be enroute to JPMorgan, and Andreas Hopf, a high yield trader at Barclays who's returning to Germany.
At the same time, UBS has hired Robert Laux </news/bank-of-america-s-european-high-yield-trading-team-sprung-a-leak>from Bank of America as the head of its high yield trading team and Bharat Gupta, </news/ubs-s-new-head-of-high-yield-trading-is-leaving-now-that-it-has-an-even-newer-one> the former team head is leaving. Barclays has hired Justin Mullem, a junior member of BofA's team (possibly as a replacement for Hopf). Rihan Dewan </news/deutsche-bank-poached-a-barclays-high-yield-trader-amidst-musical-chairs> has moved from Deutsche Bank to Barclays. At the same time, Morgan Stanley has parted company with various members of its broader UK credit team, including Klaus Pattner, who was a high yield and CDS trader at BNP Paribas until he joined the US bank in 2024. </news/morgan-stanley-credit-traders>
The reason for the sudden rush of moves isn't clear. Bonus payments are clearly the catalyst but there may be more to it. Market intelligence firm Tricumen thinks various banks' credit businesses underperformed last year (see chart below). As spreads tighten and war darkens the horizon, upgrading seems to be underway.
Source: Tricumen

item.title: Morning Coffee: The ex-Deutsche Bank rates trader who wants $600m & the head of the CEO. The worst private equity and credit funds to work for
item.description:
Italian banking aristocrat Michele Faissola had a good run at Deutsche Bank. Faissola worked at the German bank for over two decades </news/2016/10/deutsche-bank-faissola-ariburnu-al-bassam>and was close to former CEO Anshu Jain, who rendered him everything from head of OTC derivatives, to head of global rates, to head of combined wealth and asset management. At one point, Faissola was even expected to become Deutsche Bank's CEO. <https://www.ft.com/content/132a833e-abaf-11e0-8a64-00144feabdc0>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
But Faissola did not make the cut. Instead, Jain was replaced by John Cryan and John Cryan was replaced by an accountant, Christian Sewing. Sewing is Deutsche Bank's CEO to this day. Neither replacement CEO was good for Faissola's career.
First, Cryan let go of Faissola during one of Deutsche Bank's interminable restructurings in 2016. And then Sewing's audit team produced a report that made Faissola's life a misery.
Sewing's report concerned a series of repo deals that Deutsche Bank did with Italian bank Monte dei Paschi after Monte dei Paschi acquired rival Antonveneta in 2007. Written in 2014, this internal report looked at Deutsche Bank's processes <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2016-10-06/deutsche-bank-mismarked-37-loans-like-monte-paschi-s-audit-says>and was disclosed to the Italian regulator. The report was weaponised in a subsequent court case arguing that Deutsche Bank had been using enhanced repo transactions to "window dress" the accounts of Italian banks, including Monte dei Paschi, which had to be bailed out by the government in 2017. The transactions were allegedly intended to help Monte dei Paschi conceal its losses and Faissola - who was head of global rates - and six others were held responsible.
That might have been the end of the affair, except that various Deutsche Bank executives, including Faissola were eventually fully acquitted <https://www.ft.com/content/439c8fc7-c8be-4953-87ea-b44448e4c1ae?syn-25a6b1a6=1> in a Milan court of all allegations in 2022. Faissola has been seeking restitution ever since. As of yesterday, the extent of that restitution has been framed: Faissola wants $624m <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-30/ex-deutsche-bank-manager-sues-bank-for-at-least-624-million?taid=69cac76e2c3aed000146e77e&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>, while reserving the right to come back for still more. Less explicitly, Faissola and his co-dependants have been arguing that Sewing's 2014 audit report was flawed. <https://www.ft.com/content/a47f780e-8db6-47cf-9390-1aef9c5485e3?syn-25a6b1a6=1> They would undoubtedly like to see the current CEO pay for this with more than Deutsche Bank's money.
Faissola, who is now aged in late his 50s, is still working. He's long been close to the Qataris <https://www.wsj.com/articles/qatar-nominates-lawyer-for-deutsche-bank-board-1468578050> and is CEO of Dilmon, Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani’s family office. If he gets $624m from Deutsche Bank, Faissola may yet retire and have a family office of his own, courtesy of Deutsche Bank shareholders. He will not be alone: Michele Foresti, another former Deutsche Bank rates trader and member of Faissola's team already received a settlement <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-12/deutsche-bank-settles-with-ex-banker-in-monte-paschi-lawsuit> from Deutsche two months ago.
Separately, if you're embarking upon a career in private equity or private credit now, do not join funds raised before 2021.
The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/214371b3-aad0-405d-88f3-28b371f4c776?syn-25a6b1a6=1> notes that private equity and credit funds raised $1.2 trillion from investors in 2021 and that they attempted to quickly invest their resulting war chests. This pushed up asset prices, which then peaked in 2021 having doubled over the preceding decade. Now, those investments are underwater and funds can't raise new money. 25% of funds raised before 2015 are zombies and can't pay their people carried equity. And this is even before software investments have been written down...
Meanwhile...
It's a terrible time to be a young person in the UK. Graduates are stuck living with their parents while unsuccessfully applying for hundreds of jobs. “When I went into computer science, I was expecting quite a lot of demand for the skills that I worked for. But then we’re in 2026 now and people are talking about having AI replacing software developers, and it is hard to compete with a technology that makes mistakes, but can be easily fixed by a senior.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-30/london-s-youth-unemployment-crisis-looms-over-local-elections>
Start-ups in the US have begun paying high salaries instead of relying on equity. An MIT graduate got an entry-level software engineering role that pays $220k in base salary. $300k is not unheard of. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/careers/ai-talent-startups-cash-equity-32065b74?mod=e2tw>
The Department of Labor in the US is making it possible for private credit and private equity to be added to retirement plans, as long as safeguards are considered. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/f38575bf-7d7e-41a8-ae77-6e25c44de3a2?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Distressed investors are feeling excited about private credit. (Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/8c3514be-8c7b-4d13-a59a-dd8a23fb8c40?sharetype=blocked&syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
Goldman Sachs says hedge funds have just cut global equity holdings for a sixth straight week, driven by short sales. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-30/goldman-traders-see-signs-hedge-funds-are-capitulating-on-stocks?taid=69ca4bb0a33d850001ce90db&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
lpen Partners, a Swiss-based wealth manager, has helped 30 clients including some family members get out of the likes of Dubai and Israel. It's about more than just managing money. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/bfb8559d-ecdc-4c6d-ae3d-b862b3cd9ea7?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Announced global M&A for the first quarter is the strongest since 2021. Equity and equity-linked sales globally have also had their best first quarter since 2021; initial public offerings have posted the strongest first quarter since 2022. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2026-03-30/bankers-aren-t-letting-the-iran-war-get-in-the-way-of-a-deal>
The boyfriend of an executive assistant at Morgan Stanley was convicted of insider trading after extracting information from her laptop. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-30/insider-trading-scheme-born-of-romance-snags-another-broker?taid=69cad1c6ce3e6b00011e2ed4&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Grant Thornton wants 340 graduates. This is 33% more than before. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/grant-thornton-plans-30-graduate-hiring-boost-83a7d4f0>
ABN AMRO's CEO used to work for BNP Paribas and she says lunches there were a thing. “I now take lunch early and at my desk. This is a big cultural change because French meals can be long. This has been one of my adjustments.” (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/2d33bf77-f3c0-47c6-b918-a96b0de0b32c?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
JPMorgan will only build a new office in Canary Wharf if it gets a multi year 100% council tax rebate. (Guardian) <https://www.theguardian.com/business/nils-pratley-on-finance/2026/mar/30/how-many-sweeteners-does-jp-morgan-need-to-build-an-office-in-canary-wharf>

item.title: Dan Friedman, Millennium's head of credit, on the virtue of 4.30am starts
item.description:
If you're a fixed income trader, Daniel Friedman is the new man to know at hedge fund Millennium. Friedman, who is reportedly <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/millennium-to-relocate-credit-head-dan-friedman-to-new-york-ce4e15b7?mod=topic_hedge-funds>relocating from London to New York is part of the firm's senior management team. He will be running a new credit business <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-22/millennium-creates-new-credit-trading-business-led-by-friedman>reporting to Millennium co-CIO Justin Gmelich. In this context, it seems very likely that he will be hiring.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
If you want to work with Friedman, be warned that he is a fan of hard work, particularly among juniors.
In an interview published two years ago <https://tradertv.net/videos/human-capital-lehman-brothers-alumni-share-les>, Friedman described how, when he began his career in high yield at Lehman Brothers in 2004, he would arrive at the office at 4.35am.
When you work in high yield, Friedman the natural starting point is research. Research provides you with a "fundamental background." At 4.35am each morning, Friedman said he would print "all of the PNL reports, all of the credit sites research, all of the other printed research for every single trader and lay it on their desk and then go do my job in research."
Nor would Friedman hurry home at the end of each day. Instead, he said he would "pull up a chair" and ask the senior traders "inane questions" like, "why did you send this? Like, what do you mean by this? Like, what does that do?"
Friedman said the senior traders would stay until 7pm-9pm, and it seems that he would too. This worked in his favour: "When a spot opened up on the desk, they were probably just like, man, we should just give it to this guy. He’s here all the time."
Friedman is unsurprisingly, therefore, a fan of "grit." If you want to make it, he feels strongly that you need to "keep trying to get better." You also need to know a lot about market structure, capital structure and fundamental credit. And you need the humility to drop a trade that's going badly instead of defending it at the wrong level.
This approach has worked very well for Friedman. After Lehman, he went to Barclays and became head of European high yield and distressed credit. After Barclays he went to Goldman </news/2017/08/goldman-sachs-has-quietly-built-its-credit-business-all-year> and became a partner. After Goldman, he climbed the pole at Millennium.
If you encounter Friedman in New York, you know how to impress him. Make sure you read everything. And be in the office at least 15 hours a day.

item.title: Why women make better asset managers, but worse traders
item.description:
Much has been said about the experience of women in banking </news/women-in-finance>, but for all the targets and goals and deadlines in the world, there’s not a lot of literature suggesting that the pursuit of parity across finance might, in fact, be misguided.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
A recent research paper from Rosy Cilia and Florian Meier of Heriot Watt University, however, suggested something else. Cilia & Meier found that, even among trained and active trading professionals, male professionals in trading roles were much more prone to overconfidence in their abilities than female ones.
Although this was not a new finding, it was the first specifically researching financial services professionals, rather than retail investors. The researchers noted that masculinity and overconfidence had a well-established relationship.
A healthy risk appetite is strongly associated with higher returns when trading - Goldman Sachs, which has a higher risk appetite than Citi </news/citi-traders>, posted more >$100m profit trading days (81 in total versus Citi's 72), despite having access to less total capital to finance trading activity.
There was also a well -established relationship between women and having a herd mentality. The desire for loss aversion was gender neutral. Interestingly, however, the herding tendency of women was tied to experience. Cilia & Meier found that, as a woman gained more experience in her role, she would be less subject to this herding instinct.
The male tendency to overconfidence isn’t just about placing big bets. It’s been known for more 25 years that “male investors tend to overestimate their financial literacy,” which led to “heightened levels of trading”, and, in turn, “reduced returns.”
“One potential explanation for these differences has been attributed to psychological and cognitive differences across genders, such as a difference in risk perceptions,” one research paper in 2014 by Hassan et al..
The researchers noted that the “persistence of biases” in the industry meant that employers ought to take steps to “mitigate” the interference of these tendencies with portfolio management. “Firms should adjust their risk management strategies, focusing on the composition of diverse decision-making teams, to mitigate the influence of cognitive biases."
Presumably, the implication is that hedge fund and trading roles (which traditionally have larger risk appetites) ought to have a higher proportion of men, while asset management roles (with smaller risk appetites) ought to be more female.
The research has spoken.

item.title: Newly promoted 29-year-old Goldman Sachs MD escapes to hedge fund Millennium
item.description:
Four months ago, Paulo Costa, a 29 year-old trader on Goldman Sachs' London dividend trading desk, became the firm's youngest new managing director. </news/goldman-sachs-pay-london>"I was already beyond proud of working here" Costa informed Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/paulo-costa-goldman-sachs-youngest-new-managing-director-2025-11>at the time. "I never imagined that, when I was 29, I would be at this stage of my career."
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Now Costa is at another stage of his career. We understand that he has resigned from his elevated position at Goldman Sachs and is joining hedge fund Millennium instead. Goldman Sachs declined to comment. So did Millennium. Costa didn't respond to an invitation to say something.
It's not clear whether Costa is joining an established pod at Millennium. The hedge fund paid </news/millennium-hedge-fund-pay-london> its average London employees £717k each in 2024 (the last year for which figures are available), while its average partners earned £4.2m each.
Costa's Millennium move means he is no longer working with his wife, who is a prime broker at Goldman. The two reportedly met at the firm in 2018.
Costa's parents are doctors in Portugal. He became interested in economics during the European Sovereign debt crisis and began making "very small trades" at the age of 11 before studying economics at the University of Warwick. He joined Goldman a decade later.

item.title: Ex-colleagues lament death of "lovely" UBS trader who turned white upon discovery of $2bn loss
item.description:
It's a fortunate thing that not everyone who occupies a senior job on a trading floor in an investment bank has to go through the kind of stress that Riccardo Honneger experienced.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Honneger, who joined UBS from Deutsche Bank as global head of synthetic equity in 2011, managed Kweku Adoboli, </news/2011/09/how-much-was-kweku-adoboli-actually-earning-at-ubs-and-will-persons-from-the-middle-office-ever-move-into-the-front-office-again>the rogue trader who lost $2.3bn at the Swiss bank. When Honegger discovered Adoboli's losses in September 2011, Honegger's face reportedly <https://www.telegraph.co.uk/finance/financial-crime/9558872/Kweku-Adobolis-manager-told-not-to-stifle-traders.html>turned white.
Honneger had no idea. Three months earlier, Adoboli reportedly told both Honneger and John DiBacco, an MD on UBS's delta one desk, that he'd doubled his risk limit to $200m one day, but had made a $6m profit. In fact, there was a loss nearly 400 times that high.
Adoboli subsequently spent four years in prison. Honneger, who was 51 at the time, was suspended by UBS as a result of the loss. He spent the past 15 years as an investor and sadly died of cancer earlier this month.
Former colleagues say Honneger was a remarkable man. "Ricardo was a man of great intelligence, dignity, and integrity," says Yassine Bouhara, who worked with Honegger at both UBS and at Deutsche Bank. Others former colleagues praised him for "camaraderie and bonhomie", "warmth and humanity", for being a "lovely guy" and "exceptional human being" and for being an "incredibly kind and inspiring person."

item.title: Hedge fund Balyasny hired Credit Suisse's ex-head of APAC ECM syndicate, but now he's gone
item.description:
Hedge fund Balyasny Asset Management </news/balyasny-hedge-fund>(BAM) spends a lot of money hiring people. Financial News </news/citi-traders> puts its hiring budget at $350m-$500m a year. BAM may not have spent much hiring Adrian Lee from Credit Suisse in 2024, though, which is just as well as Lee has now moved on.
Lee, who didn't respond to a request to comment for this article, has left BAM for Hudson Bay Capital Management according to his LinkedIn profile. Lee was head of Asia equity capital markets (ECM) at Balyasny and he'll be doing the same thing at Hudson Bay.
Lee was only at BAM two years. His Credit Suisse career was marked by greater longevity and endured for 11 years, at the end of which he was the co-head of APAC ECM syndicate. He left there in 2023 and was elevated to BAM's big job one year later.
Why did he leave again so soon? Neither BAM nor Lee responded to a request to comment. BAM is often gentler than other funds when it comes to risk limits so it may have been a gentle exit.
Lee will be based at Hudson Bay in Hong Kong. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-01-16/hudson-bay-among-three-more-hedge-funds-expanding-to-hong-kong> reported last year that the $20bn fund was expanding in the city and had hired various people from Segantii, including Jack Truong to lead business development. Lee appears to be one of Truong's first major hires in the region.
ECM professionals in hedge funds invest in IPOs and in follow-on deals and then attempt to make money by selling-on the stock that comes their way. The business can be challenging as hedge funds are often perceived as undesirable early investors and banks don't always want to sell to them. It can also be controversial </news/trading-daddy>: two years ago, Morgan Stanley's equity syndicate desk paid a $249m fine <https://www.ft.com/content/60d45471-29a1-4b5a-a465-9655dae6027a?syn-25a6b1a6=1>following claims that its head of ECM syndicate was offering preferential block trades to a hedge fund. That head of ECM syndicate at Morgan Stanley was subsequently vindicated of wrongdoing and won <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-12-08/hedge-fund-trader-wins-5-4-million-uk-fight-over-denied-bonus>a court case for his $5.4m deferred bonus.

item.title: Maven Securities had a weird year, but people got paid well. Better, even
item.description:
Maven Securities, the trading firm and market maker known for hiring niche traders </news/maven-securities-hiring>, also pays them pretty well – if its latest accounts, published this week and covering the period ending June 2025, are anything to go by.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Headline figures seemed good on the face of it. Revenue at Maven Securities Holding Limited, the main London-based subsidiary, was up from £84m in 2024 to £101m in 2025, an increase of 20%. But profit was down by 36%, from £12m to £7.7m. That was mostly on account of a huge increase in administrative expenses, the biggest part of which was salaries.
Maven’s total wage bill went up, from £50m to £66m. But headcount declined from 260 heads to 236, a decline of around 10%. Total pay per head, therefore, went from £194k ($257k) to £281k ($372k), a chunky increase of 45%. Good times, then – if you were there to enjoy them.
Pay for Maven’s portfolio managers is less clear. Aside from the firm’s main entity, it has a wide range of other subsidiaries registered with Companies House. Not all of them have released accounts for the period ending 30 June 2025 yet. We’ll get back to you later.
Back in January, Maven released its US accounts via the entity known as Maven Securities US Limited, also through companies house, and also for the period ending June 2025. This particular iteration of Maven is responsible for operations in New York and Chicago, where the firm earns about half its revenue.
Maven’s US employees, of which there were 72 at the end of the June 2025 period, earned substantially more than their UK colleagues – £363k ($481k) per head, an increase of 6.4% on the £341k ($452k) earned the period before.
