item.title: FICC traders with disappointing bonuses have this to blame
item.description:
If you’re a fixed income, currencies, and commodities (FICC) salesperson or trader </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> looking at their bonus announcement email in disbelief this year, rest assured: it was probably your fault.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Data from compensation consultancy Johnson Associates can shed some light on the matter. The firm, which estimates bonus changes, revised its estimation for last year's FICC trading bonuses in a recent presentation.
In November last year, Johnson Associates predicated that bonuses in FICC would rise by up to 15%. Alas, it wasn't meant to be: it now estimates that bonuses were up by 10% compared to last year.
The reason for that is that, quite simply, FICC traders did not have a good end to 2025. Of the banks we track, only two managed to post Q4 FICC revenue that was more than 7.5% higher in 2025 than it was in 2024: Goldman Sachs, which was up 12.5%, and UBS, which was up a more spectacular 46% on its Q4 2024 result.
Most banks ranged between 8.7% down (Morgan Stanley) and 7.5% up (JPMorgan). This included Bank of America, Citi, Deutsche Bank, BNP Paribas, and Nomura. Considering how those same banks performed across 2025 as a whole, expectations for big bonus increases had to be tempered.
Bonuses predictions were more optimistic for some investment bankers – specifically, those working in M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs> and Debt Capital Markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm>. Johnson Associates revised their predictions for these two fields from 10% to 15% and 5% to 15% increase on last year respectively to a 10% to 20% and 10% to 20% increase, respectively.
But predictions are just predictions. How did FICC bonuses really change, seeing as most of them have been announced now? You’ll have to tune in to our annual compensation report to find out. Expect to see articles from that published on our site starting later this month.

item.title: "As an ex-Goldman Sachs banker in Dubai, I would never give up this quality of life"
item.description:
I am a British citizen and I live in Dubai. I moved here three years ago after a career in banking at Goldman Sachs and I have absolutely no intention of leaving again, irrespective of the current situation with Iran.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
If you're not in Dubai, you won't get it. Live and work here for a year and you'll wonder how you ever thought Dubai could be over.
When you ask a British resident why someone would move to Dubai they typically say something along the lines of "weather and tax". This is revealing only in so far as it holds a mirror up to their own lives. Weather and tax are what people (justifiably) dislike about their own lives in the UK.
However, if you ask a British person who has lived in Dubai for a few years why they live in Dubai, they don't say weather and tax. They invariably say something related to quality of life. Sure, weather and tax play a part, but the reality is vastly different - it is so so easy to have a far higher quality of life here, across multiple domains.
The reasons are too numerous to mention, but holistically they add up to a feeling of "my goodness, the quality of life for me, my partner and my family is wildly better than I thought possible in our lifetimes - or would have been possible if I had stayed in the UK. My only regret is not moving sooner." I have heard versions of this refrain literally hundreds of times.
The vast majority of things that make life in Dubai so great are structural. They will far outlast any short term geopolitical flare up. This is why I am still investing in Dubai property </news/dubai-property-investments-bankers>and don't believe the hype about falling prices. Life is great here. The reality on the ground and the perception of it overseas are worlds apart.
Percy Hunter is a pseudonym

item.title: Jane Street has 160 PhDs. Academia alone won't get you a job there.
item.description:
In general the new breed of electronic trading firms </news/difference-between-electronic-trading-firms>focus their extensive campus recruiting </news/jane-street-target-universities>efforts on undergraduates. Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> employees have said that PhDs are a minority there </news/jane-street-employees-reveal-what-degrees-matter-when-applying-for-quant-jobs#:~:text=%22I%20think%20the,in%20machine%20learning.> in the past; the firm's CTO (who does have a PhD) explained why relying on just your degree to get a job in the space might be misguided.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Ron Minsky, speaking in his podcast Signals and Threads <https://signalsandthreads.com/> this week, said "the world is actually very bad at teaching people how to program. Universities, I feel, are especially bad at it." He said that when he first started at Jane Street he was "super incompetent... I had a PhD </news/do-you-need-a-phd-for-a-quant-job-in-finance> in Computer Science, which doesn't tell you how to be a software engineer." Indeed, PhDs we've spoken to in the past have said they struggled to get a quant job based solely on their academic record "because you don't have the experience to justify the seniority." </news/the-cheap-course-getting-students-hedge-fund-quant-jobs-might-be-in-peril#:~:text=%22My%20PhD%20was%20very%20theoretical.%22%20He%20said.%20%22When%20I%20applied%20for%20jobs%2C%20I%20didn%27t%20know%20how%20to%20program%3B%20it%20wasn%27t%20easy%20to%20find%20one.%22%20Having%20a%20PhD%20can%20sometimes%20be%20a%20hindrance%20for%20jobs%20in%20finance%20because%20%22because%20you%20don%27t%20have%20the%20experience%20to%20justify%20the%20seniority.%22>
What is it that universities do so wrong? Minsky said programming in academia is a "weird form of performance art," in which assignments are given and resolved with no feedback or conversation, so that it "vanishes like a puff of smoke." Minsky said software development in the real world is "nothing like that... the permanent evolving state of the software is part of what's important about it."
For example, Minsky says academia focuses more on high-level technical concepts instead of "low status" tasks like testing your code. He said he "didn't know anything about testing" when he started at Jane Street but has "come to realizing that testing is not just important, but it's super interesting and fun." Of course, this problem isn't unique to PhDs, but those studying doctorates spend an extra four years in school that they could have otherwise spent learning on the job. Internships </news/this-is-what-it-takes-to-get-a-64k-jane-street-internship> in the sector may offset this disparity.
There are exceptions; Jane Street currently employees hold roughly 160 PhDs globally. Citadel Securities has 260. Highly technical or theoretical skillsets like machine learning or hardware engineering, for example benefit from the extra years of study. In Jane Street's internship listings, it says it is "more interested in how you think and learn than what you currently know."
Jane Street has also been running a graduate research fellowship for PhD students since 2023; recipients of the fellowship this year <https://www.janestreet.com/join-jane-street/programs-and-events/grf-profiles-2026/> include Gefen Baranes, an MIT student working on quantum computing, and Jared Siegel, a Princeton astrophysics PhD investigating Dark Matter.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: WhatsApp: http://wa.me/442079977910 <http://wa.me/442079977910> (+44 20 7997 7910), Telegram: @AlexMcMurray, Signal: @AlexMcMurrayEFC.88 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. item.title: Morning Coffee: 22-year-old JPMorgan bankers on $160k are wild for overwork. Goldman Sachs' newly perpetual job cuts
item.description:
If you work for JPMorgan, you're supposed to work no more than 80 hours a week. </news/jpmorgan-restrict-working-hours-banking>There are exceptions, like times of live deals, but 80 hours are the mandatory norm. And yet junior bankers seem to be abusing JPMorgan's trust.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The bank has mandated 80 hours, but JPMorgan's 22 year-old bankers earning circa $160k (as per the Prospect Rock Compensation Report <https://prospectrockpartners.com/prospect-rock-partners-2025-ib-compensation-report/>) cannot be trusted to abide by its edict. They want to work more. Therefore, the Financial Times <https://prospectrockpartners.com/prospect-rock-partners-2025-ib-compensation-report/>says JPMorgan has stepped up its scrutiny of what its junior bankers are doing and will no longer rely upon the old self-reported time sheets as a measure of hours worked, but examine what they're up to on their computers. "Digital footprints" don't lie; if a 22 year-old analyst spends 100 hours making "keyboard strokes," video calls and attending "scheduled meetings", JPMorgan will know. JPMorgan will command that less work be done.
Given that in many walks of life people attempt to evade work, the situation at JPMorgan might seem freaky. But junior bankers are well documented </news/2020/05/why-bankers-overwork>as having a tendency towards working so hard that their hair falls out. This can be self-imposed. "Their sense of self-esteem is constructed around being an elite individual," said Alexandra Michel, the ex-Goldman Sachs associate who's built a career studying the phenomenon. </news/2020/05/why-bankers-overwork>
Not all young bankers would agree that they're wild for overwork though. Nor would they agree that they're intentionally misleading employers by marking down the hours they work on self-reported time sheets. In many cases, senior bankers at all banks are said to encourage juniors to understate their hours so that they can keep on working. Bank of America, for example, denied </news/2020/05/why-bankers-overwork> that Leo Lukenas worked 120 hour weeks before the associate died in 2024, but the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/podcasts/the-journal/outcry-at-bank-of-america-over-dangerous-workloads/55c88dae-09ca-44c3-9e65-ed17451ebae5?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqf_8GvGA1V9FkJT5jsKaWJDAhip0rWq6U17cWont-YbDTBHUZsipO7ISQp2_i0%3D&gaa_ts=69bd1342&gaa_sig=jU56fcUudLCCvX8ZQKdm2wgqdeHYDnoxgFKIY_z6L6bDuMFDTy9h79-Rg55cXp7xmamM4pmxJ9UKYVF-XPRZ4A%3D%3D> subsequently discovered that managers had been asking juniors to under-record banking hours to avoid HR intervention and keep working.
There will no more of this at JPMorgan, and this be welcome. From now on, every keyboard stroke at the bank must matter.
Separately, as we reported yesterday </news/hsbc-ai-job-cuts>, Goldman Sachs is job cuts. Insiders at the bank tell us that lists have been drawn up over the past month. The bank had been hoping to avoid layoffs through natural post-bonus attrition but this hasn't come to pass.
Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/goldman-sachs-layoffs-cuts-underperformers-sra-spring-summer-2026-3> confirms that cuts are coming. They will start in April, but they will not stop then. Instead of a single round or rounds Goldman is reportedly planning for a series of "ongoing, smaller rolling cuts" that may never end.
The new approach is intended to "give divisional leaders more control over timing than waiting months for the next firmwide review," says BI. Goldman Sachs says there is nothing to see here. "Regular, consistent head count management is nothing out of the ordinary for a public company," a spokesman declares.
Meanwhile...
Point72 and Balyasny recently banned employees from trading on prediction markets in their personal accounts. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/point72-balyasny-ban-staff-bets-in-prediction-market-crackdown?taid=69bc56e4a072250001e9aa3f&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Being a trader is an exhausting business now, but AI is helping. “I was up for almost 48 hours straight, monitoring the interceptions in the United Arab Emirates while simultaneously running scenarios and preparing for the market open. That’s precisely the kind of moment where AI becomes indispensable." (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/traders-overwhelmed-by-iran-news-are-turning-to-ai-for-help>
Traders have been pouring over real time alt data </news/hedge-funds-and-market-makers-need-data-strategists-as-war-heats-up>. This week, the key data point was whether Ras Laffan had gone into “cold shutdown" (refrigeration is still ongoing), or “warm,” which would take much longer to recover from. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/commodities-futures/frantic-pace-of-mideast-energy-strikes-leaves-markets-trading-blind-e2b67532?mod=finance_lead_story&mod=djemHeardEUH>
Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan have assembled baskets of listed companies with exposure to private credit and are creating products for hedge funds to trade against. Goldman's index includes European financial institutions. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/jpmorgan-goldman-offer-hedge-funds-way-to-short-private-credit?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260319&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
Bank of America suggested that clients bet against companies exposed to European private credit, including Deutsche Bank, but now it says this was all a terrible mistake. “Those views do not reflect those of BofA Research or the broader organisation. We apologise to the companies that were inaccurately referenced.” (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/363a2bc3-566a-4cc2-b69c-28db1782816e?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Morgan Stanley hired James Machin, a managing director at Bank of America in London for its healthcare team. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/morgan-stanley-hires-bank-of-americas-machin-to-bolster-london-healthcare-team-79becd70?mod=homepage>
Fabrizio Campelli who runs they investment bank at Deutsche Bank might replace Christian Sewing. But so might Stefan Hoops, who's just been promoted to the management board and who runs the asset management arm. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/bdf1851f-8c9a-4aaf-a49a-178b8a3c6808?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Michele Faissola says Deutsche Bank caused irreparable harm to his career and wants £500m in damages. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/ex-trader-suing-deutsche-bank-wants-500-million-in-paschi-case?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260319&utm_campaign=author_23851719>
Ex-Point 72 guy says you need to work weekends at the start of your career to compounds knowledge. "What I learned was if you work six hours a Sunday, 50 Sundays a year, right? That's 300 hours." (YouTube) <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=byEXabVAxus>
Starting next month, bankers may not be able to make long haul flights to clients due to a shortage of jet fuel. (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/uk/transport/article/long-haul-flights-easter-holiday-travel-g2rfsn623?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqdcqFxc5bGL46euQvzckQHFkLh6_AwsFkJQHwm3WUZK7xYzZmewI64DfPJePow%3D&gaa_ts=69bbbf75&gaa_sig=u_zWaEPiuL5fZ0jFlVuLQ9aNhNnGg_iBxnJijuw_wRi_3EkYFbh4tQVkjmpcBzEuyHxHmAc4QxSFvzC-neFXbA%3D%3D>
Jes Staley's wife wants a divorce. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/jes-staley-s-wife-files-for-divorce-after-epstein-fallout?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260319&utm_campaign=author_3487187>

item.title: UBS's "brief" tech outage may have lasted three hours and been caused by an oversight
item.description:
What happened at UBS on Tuesday? As we reported </news/ubs-technology-outage>on Wednesday, the bank was said to have experienced a "brief" technology outage that impacted "some" of its trading business. The incident was resolved within the day.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
All is now back to normal, but the outage came at a challenging time amidst the volatility imposed by war in the Middle East and there are suggestions that it was not all that brief. Multiple sources at the Swiss bank say it lasted for up to three hours and affected trading on different desks, including FX and futures.
As such, it's thought that the failure was caused by infrastructure rather than a specific trading application. "If it's transversal and cross-business, it's due to infrastructure," observed one insider.
It's thought that UBS employees were unable to login to internal messaging systems like Teams as a result of the outage. One insider suggested it was due to Citrix, which provides software that UBS uses to validate people logging into its systems. "We had no access," he says. "Even Neo [UBS's cross asset trading platform] was down." The problem may have been a security certificate that was noted updated or corrupted.
UBS's outage came as the bank rang ritual cowbells <https://www.ft.com/content/c7c9e78b-e007-4ec6-a4b4-9a559e931fe3?syn-25a6b1a6=1> to celebrate the integration of most of Credit Suisse's computer systems. In December it parted company with Mike Dargan </news/first-ubs-s-head-of-technology-left-now-tech-people-are-being-cut>, its former CTO. Thousands of technology jobs are expected to go at the bank this year now that work on the integration is done.
One senior insider said the outage had nothing to do with the integration or the coming job cuts. However, they also observed that UBS has not has these kind of non-brief outages much before. Hopefully it will not have them much again.

item.title: HSBC's 20,000 potential AI job cuts could be duplicated at Goldman Sachs & Citi
item.description:
HSBC is not unfamiliar with the concept of cutting tens of thousands of jobs. In 2015, then-CEO Stuart Gulliver promised <https://www.ft.com/content/2fddac06-0e4b-11e5-9a65-00144feabdc0?syn-25a6b1a6=1>to cut 50,000 of the bank's 266,000 workforce by closing some operations and pivoting to Asia. In early 2020, Noel Quinn, Gulliver's successor, produced another plan </news/2020/02/hsbc-results-2019-cost-cutting> to cut 35,000 jobs by simplifying the bank's management "matrix" and further pivoting to Asia. And then last January, Georges Elhedery </news/hsbc-s-self-proclaimed-ruthless-ceo-is-not-its-most-feared-person>, Quinn's bold successor, announced a plan to close the bank's equity capital markets (ECM) and M&A businesses in both Europe and the US, and to focus more intently on Asia, plus the Middle East.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Despite all this, HSBC's headcount at the end of last year stood at 210,000 people. Even without Elhedery's efforts, it should now be 181,000 or below.
AI may help the bank to make the final push.
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/hsbc-mulls-deep-job-cuts-from-multiyear-ai-fueled-overhaul>reported today that under Elhedery, HSBC is hoping to cut 20,000 jobs in the next three to five years by automating jobs in the middle and back office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> using AI, and maybe also exiting some more businesses. It's not clear exactly which jobs would be automated, but the favourites are reportedly "non-client facing roles in global service centers," which usually means operations, </news/operations-jobs-banking> compliance <https://efinancialcareers.com/news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance#:~:text=If%20you%20want%20a%20job,the%20overall%20financial%20services%20industry.>, finance, and IT roles away from major financial hubs.
Speaking yesterday <https://seekingalpha.com/article/4883769-hsbc-holdings-plc-hsbc-presents-at-european-financials-conference-2026-transcript> before Bloomberg published its revelations at the Morgan Stanley Financial Conference, HSBC CFO Manveen Kaur, said HSBC has been using AI at its Hang Seng subsidiary for KYC onboarding, small ticket credit lending, transaction monitoring, and other "volume-driven activities" that require "very basic binary decision-making."
HSBC's endeavours with AI at Heng Seng are not a "severance story," said Kaur. Instead the bank will be "upskilling colleagues" and "doing more with the same number of people," she explained.
For the bank as a whole, Bloomberg suggests this may not be so. HSBC didn't respond to a request to comment.
While HSBC's binary decision makers brace for pain, they may not be alone. Off the record, insiders at Goldman Sachs and Citi suggest that similar intentions are in the works there.
At Goldman Sachs, a memo from CEO David Solomon in October last year described plans to introduce a new AI driven "operating system" for the bank titled "OneGS 3.0." <https://finance.yahoo.com/news/david-solomon-says-theres-key-215243387.html?guccounter=1&guce_referrer=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZ29vZ2xlLmNvbS8&guce_referrer_sig=AQAAAHJsokWNWZ7XpKguB7yoY05U2ov6SDWL8ZLXG2GEPbfcJ1c8YO78JtorLirnrqg7tc6tEsR4E0DTsOCHrymLOCwuDqiSXCLia7gg0K4YxJkTNVCLC9Xc0QYn2u3gNWtiZoa_S1r06t4H9wp-GtTPgCQyg3K15hO4GS0kQwTnmDGp>In January, Solomon said he was "excited" <https://seekingalpha.com/article/4860209-the-goldman-sachs-group-inc-gs-q4-2025-earnings-call-transcript>about this effort and that Goldman had already begun looking at "six work streams," where it will "invest to reengineer these processes from the ground up." Solomon didn't say what these six work streams are, but he promised news "over the course of the next quarters."
Speaking off the record, one Goldman Sachs insider says this news could come as soon as April. The firm is allegdly planning to make another round of job cuts focused on underperformers and has tightened its performance criteria in light of AI. There are suggestions this will just the start. A spokesman for Goldman Sachs said: “As always, Goldman Sachs is focused on supporting our people through our talent management, development and engagement efforts. We do not comment on speculation or rumors.”
While there are rumblings at Goldman Sachs, there are rumblings at Citi too. The US bank announced a small number of job cuts in January </news/citi-job-cuts-2026-bonuses>, but there are suggestions that a considerably larger round may be coming. Roles across compliance, risk, controls, and data are likely to bear the brunt of future cuts. Citi added thousands of jobs in this areas as a result of its 2020 consent order. </news/citi-is-still-out-there-hiring-m-ds-to-work-on-its-consent-order>Citi CEO Jane Fraser said in January that resulting transformation work is nearly done. AI is now being used by the bank for "over 50 processes," added Fraser.
A spokesperson for Citi said: “As we said previously, we will continue to reduce our headcount in 2026. These changes reflect adjustments we’re making to ensure our staffing levels, locations and expertise align with current business needs; efficiencies we have gained through technology; and progress against our Transformation work, which is nearing Citi’s Target State. We are grateful for the contributions these colleagues have made to Citi.”
As AI bites, some roles are likely to be savaged more comprehensively than others. Relationship-focused front office jobs could be comparatively immune. In December, David Solomon at Goldman Sachs even suggested </news/goldman-sachs-ai-hiring> that the firm might reinvest the money it saves from process-roles in new bankers to talk to clients. Humans can but hope.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: Coinbase fired an engineer after they refused to use AI coding tools
item.description:
AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job>coding tools are all the rage in fintech </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-fintech>. Last summer, Robinhood </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-robinhood-culture-fintech> CEO Vlad Tenev said his engineers were writing half their code with AI. </news/robinhood-ai-coding> Coinbase </news/coinbase-pay-hiring> CEO Brian Armstrong has just admitted the same, having gone to slightly more extreme lengths to tout that fact.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Speaking on a podcast by Norges Bank <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jmdf35zSdA8>, Armstrong said, "on our software engineering team, more than 50% of the code is being written by AI." Armstrong said that about a year ago, he pushed for Coinbase's engineers to start using tools like Cursor and Claude; he said people were "a little hesitant... now almost all the engineers are using it every day."
This didn't come about without some nudging. When around 70% of Coinbase engineers had experimented with AI, Armstrong said he got "impatient" and sent a note out to staff saying that, if they hadn't signed up to try an AI tool by the end of the week, they'd be put in a meeting on Saturday with Armstrong so they could tell him why they hadn't.
Armstrong said one employee got fired because of this; they'd refused to use AI tools and didn't show up to the meeting. He said that the engineer didn't have a "good reason" like vacation or parental leave, so they were told they were "not a good fit for the company," and let go. Armstrong said the ordeal created a "very clear tone from the top that we need to stay on top of these things to stay relevant as a company."
It's hard not to place some blame on the developer in that instance, having ghosted their CEO, but it's not the first time Coinbase has taken an unconventional approach to its engineering culture. At the start of last year, the firm initiated a 'new norm' </news/coinbase-s-300k-software-engineers-aren-t-happy-with-the-firm-s-new-norm> in which projects could have no more than one bug per developer working on it.
Armstrong also noted that, thanks to AI coding, Coinbase has "started to see some really impressive potential cost savings." Given that the firm made a $667m loss </news/coinbase-pay-hiring>last year, it will be keen to realize as many cost savings as it can in 2026.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? item.title: Financial Services is using AI more than other industries but seeing a fraction of the benefits
item.description:
Like most industries, financial services is bullish on the potential of AI. </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job> It's in a unique position, however, in that teams have adopted AI far more than the average but seen far fewer benefits. </news/ai-projects-failing>
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
In a new survey from product development firm Studio Graphene, 500 managers and executives in UK businesses were asked about the usage of AI within their teams. 78% of respondents said they used AI in some capacity but, for financial services professionals, the usage rate was 91%.
Despite this, fewer financial services professionals were able to clearly define what 'successful' AI implementation was. While 31% of all respondents using AI said they'd seen a return on investment from their AI usage, just 13% of managers in finance were able to justify the cost.
There are many reasons for this. For one, finance and banking in particular are highly regulated industries full of publicly traded companies. They need to be able to justify their actions to investors and some AI tools are either hard to explain or prone to hallucinations.
There's also the fact that the tools today simply aren't good enough for industries prone to perfectionism. Claude for Excel, for example has received various complaints from people in finance; the tried and tested method of having your analysts work 100-hour weeks yields more consistent results. Mo Achour, a Goldman Sachs executive director who left to build an AI startup, told us last week </news/ex-goldman-sachs-banker-that-worked-on-thousands-of-financial-models-wants-ai-to-build-it-for-you>that "there’s no such thing as good enough" in financial services. Goldman CIO Marco Argenti, another AI advocate, told Bloomberg in 2024 that even "0.1% inaccuracy is totally unacceptable." </news/the-other-problem-goldman-sachs-faces-with-ll-ms>
Despite all this, it's no surprise that financial services firms are trying to make AI work for them. A report from Anthropic </news/ai-hedge-fund-jobs-vs-banking> earlier this month said that business and finance was one of (if not the) most exposed sector to AI in theory. The bank that is able to realize that potential while 87% see it as a money suck will be very happy indeed.

item.title: Citi's head of culture and behavioural insights has left for HSBC
item.description:
Citi wants its culture to foster champions </news/andy-sieg-women#:~:text=%E2%80%9CWhat%20champions%20do%2C%20when%20their%20backs%20are%20to%20the%20wall%2C%20they%20find%20a%20way%20to%20win.%20They%20know%20they%E2%80%99ve%20got%20to%20change%2C%20take%20risks%2C%20play%20outside%20the%20box.%22>. It'll have to do so without one of the senior women </news/citi-parted-company-with-the-nice-woman-leading-its-unpopular-internal-hit-squad>tasked with improving that culture.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Anna Koczwara, an MD at Citi and its head of cultural and behavioural insights, has left to join HSBC </news/hsbc-s-md-promotions-are-back-to-normal-but-never-simple> in London. She arrived this week as its global head of behavioural science and research. Now-closed job listings in Citi's behavioural insights team say the division is tasked with "improv[ing Citi's] working environment, engagement levels, satisfaction, and intent to stay."
Koczwara, a psychological safety advocate, </news/citi-s-latest-effort-to-rectify-its-culture-hiring-a-ph-d-psychologist> was only at Citi for a year and a half. Before that, she spent seven years at RBS in London, where she was head of strategic capability and assessment.
HSBC's behavioural science team was first led by David Grosse, who joined HSBC in 2017 and left in 2022. He led a team of four according to Reuters <https://www.thomsonreuters.com/en-us/posts/investigation-fraud-and-risk/behavioral-science-bank-risk/>, but the team appears to have shown little growth since he left; Koczwara's arrival may be an attempt to revitalize it. The bank was also seen hiring for a behavioural scientist to work with its employee listening and dialogue team last month.
Grosse was different to other behavioural science leaders in that he had a banking risk background. Koczwara has a more standard background with a PhD </news/do-you-need-a-phd-for-a-quant-job-in-finance>in psychology. For her thesis <https://research.gold.ac.uk/id/eprint/28520/1/PSY_thesis_KoczwaraA_2007.pdf>, she interviewed 40 managers in banking and finance to assess their bias in evaluating the potential of male and female talent. </news/gender-pay-gap-investment-banking-goldman-sachs-jpmorgan>
HSBC did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Hong Kong’s global talent push gains pace
item.description:
As global talent hubs grapple with economic uncertainty, Hong Kong stands out. This resilient international business centre has both regulatory clarity and long‑term stability.
One of the latest initiatives supporting this is the Innovating Hong Kong Global Online TalentX Carnival <https://recruiterhub.efinancialcareers.com/InnovatingHK_Global_Online_TalentX_Carnival_Partner.html>. The careers event will take place online on 26 and 27 March 2026. Ambitious finance, technology, and innovation professionals will have access to employers, career insights, and relocation guidance.
TalentX Carnival: More than a traditional job fair
Innovating Hong Kong TalentX Carnival goes beyond the format of a recruitment fair. It brings together talent, enterprise, policy, and technology within one integrated platform. This reflects Hong Kong’s ambition to position itself as an innovation hub and a high-value professional services centre.
The Carnival features cloud-based interview functionality, creating a hybrid recruitment experience that bridges online and offline engagement.
Event highlights
Over two days, candidates can connect with employers and get visa policy guidance, career support and practical relocation tools.
Insight-sharing sessions and seminars on career development, industry growth and Hong Kong’s evolving economic landscape. Direct access to 3,000+ roles across financial services, technology, and property. AI-powered matching: The platform features an enhanced AI suite, including mock interview practice and CV analysis, to provide feedback before speaking with employers.
The Carnival is the official designated recruitment event of the Global Talent Summit (GTS) <https://gts.hkengage.gov.hk/en/> and a strategic partner of Hong Kong Talent Engage <https://www.hkengage.gov.hk/en>, underscoring its central role in Hong Kong’s global talent strategy.
AsiaWorld-Expo in Hong Kong
Participants will be invited to engage further at the physical career fair on 28 and 29 March <https://www.innovatinghk.com/> 2026 at AsiaWorld-Expo in Hong Kong. The in-person fair will host more than 400 employers and 10,000 open roles, offering face-to-face interaction, in-depth interviews, and extra seminars. Featured employers will include FUTU, Hang Seng Bank, MOUTAI, UNITREE and Liepin, alongside established institutions and emerging companies.
Past offline events have attracted over 10,000 participants, with 30% coming from outside the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area. The average interview-to-offer conversion rate for companies reached 25%.
The online event serves as a warm-up, offering company previews, early job applications, and AI tool experiences. The offline event focuses on in-depth interviews, seminars, and face-to-face interactions.
Sector-wide opportunities
This year’s online Carnival features roles across financial services, technology, property and other growth sectors. Roles are available for graduates, early-career professionals and experienced specialists. Candidates can upload their CVs in advance, speak to recruiters pre-application, and engage in live chat conversations.
Why Hong Kong?
The recruitment market is pivoting from ‘backfilling’ to value creation; 34% of employers <https://hongkongbusiness.hk/hr-education/in-focus/whats-driving-hong-kongs-hiring-rebound> plan to increase headcount this year. Hong Kong offers proximity to fast-growing regional markets and excellent career progression.
Its location and role as a bridge between mainland China and the West continue to drive demand for experienced professionals.
The city is also home to five universities ranked among the world’s top 100, reinforcing its depth of academic and research capability. In the International Institute for Management Development World Talent Ranking 2025, Hong Kong rose to fourth globally. Its highest position to date and first in Asia.
Hong Kong’s reach extends beyond the city. For those looking beyond the city limits, the event highlights GBA talent mobility. This includes cross-border career consultation, GBA policy interpretation sessions, and access to joint recruitment channels for enterprises operating across Guangdong, Hong Kong, and Macao.
Working and living in Hong Kong
Understanding that a move to Hong Kong is about more than a job, the Carnival is a one-stop support system for relocation logistics and long-term career planning.
The government encourages talent from beyond the region. This includes programmes such as the Quality Migrant Admission Scheme. There is also an Admission Scheme for Mainland Talents and Professionals, as well as the General Employment Policy.
The Carnival provides authoritative guidance on Hong Kong’s key talent admission schemes. This includes the Top Talent Pass Scheme (TTPS) and the Immigration Arrangements for Non-local Graduates (IANG), including all recent updates.
Besides visa guidance, participants can access:
Career support through official job databases Advice on visa renewal pathways Education consulting and school information for families Broader relocation resources
Taking the next step
With competition for skilled professionals intensifying worldwide, Hong Kong is open, competitive, and globally connected. For international candidates, the online Carnival is the perfect warm-up to explore the market and secure early interviews.
If you're considering new horizons in Asia, the online Carnival offers a practical first step towards building a long-term career in Hong Kong.
Registration for the online event is now open. Sign up here: https://recruiterhub.efinancialcareers.com/InnovatingHK_Global_Online_TalentX_Carnival_Partner.html <https://recruiterhub.efinancialcareers.com/InnovatingHK_Global_Online_TalentX_Carnival_Partner.html>
item.author: editor@efinancialcareers.com (Innovating Hong Kong)

item.title: Morning Coffee: The new power move if you’re leaving Citi and want a better job. Last year’s superstar bankers are getting worried
item.description:
If you’re frustrated in your banking career – perhaps you’ve reached a point at which the only job you can be promoted into is occupied by someone two years older than you who seems to be doing well – then you might start looking for ways to get a promotion by moving to the competition. If there are no vacancies there, then you could consider a lateral move, but if there aren’t any empty seats at your level either, you have two choices.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
One is to stick around getting progressively more and more bitter, and the other is to resign, take a sabbatical and write a social media post titled something like “Stepping Out Of My Comfort Zone”. Or if you’re Mark Mason and up until recently the Chief Financial Officer of Citigroup, you don’t even need to fire up ChatGPT or break out the book of inspirational quotes, as the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/hes-leaving-citigroup-to-be-a-ceo-can-he-land-the-big-job-79148099?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqcF8CG7Rx-pk4aACWDhKGizq0SoO43V9tUznaW1MdcocDLMiQsuOU9trP1Us3A%3D&gaa_ts=69bbbd47&gaa_sig=iX8riNL4nyDABfaaxjwDRCmMXluQl0vZrsrG7lM30BFdxuBh4T9qezqAmoYYVJ9KO6WR-DMl12ysiqyiiwACgw%3D%3D>might do a profile which allows you to announce your availability for bigger and better roles.
Mason does not waste the opportunity, making it clear that he now wants his next job to be as a CEO. Some recruiters are quoted as saying that he will probably have to “go down the food chain in terms of the size of enterprise” to achieve this, but he was probably aware of that risk; there are, after all, fewer than half a dozen banks which aren’t smaller than Citi.
Mason has the right resume for quite a lot of possible roles; he came to prominence in a wealth management business unit, but he also managed a “bad bank” winding down problem assets in the aftermath of the last financial crisis, and has had a key role in trying to sort out the back-office problems which once caused Citi to accidentally pay $900m </news/2020/12/jpmorgan-forecast-2021> to the creditors of Revlon. If you’re interested in shrinking something, he’s closed down over 60 business lines and cut around 60,000 jobs.
So why’s he leaving? Well, he isn’t, not immediately; until the end of the year, he’s going to be an Executive Vice-Chair and a senior advisor to Jane Fraser. (Although, since Citi have presumably signed off on the interview, they probably wouldn’t make him work out the whole contract if something big came up). But it seems pretty clear, reading between the lines, that this was a matter of “up or out”. Mason was offered the top job at Carlyle Group in 2023 and turned it down, something which really only makes sense if he had ambitions to be CEO of Citi.
And he might have been, if things had turned out differently; while the big restructuring project was going on, he would have been the natural choice if the board had decided that things weren’t working out and Citi needed a course correction. But things didn’t turn out differently, the board have demonstrated confidence in Jane Fraser by making her Chair as well as CEO. In that sort of situation, it’s best for all concerned to make a fresh start.
Elsewhere, European private credit was one of the hottest labour markets of 2024 and 2025. But the latest news from that market segment feels rather like the scene in Jurassic Park <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LFLB68JalpI> where the characters are wondering “why is the water in that glass rippling?” It’s not just software company loans any more; even private credit funds based on consumer and SME <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/private-credits-investor-exodus-spreads-to-consumer-loans-de2507d7?st=eCQuPD&reflink=desktopwebshare_permalink> loans are seeing heightened redemptions. And the problems might not be confined to North America.
At industry conferences this week, banks like Deutsche, SocGen and UBS <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-17/socgen-s-krupa-sees-private-credit-clean-up-as-investors-exit?embedded-checkout=true> have been emphasising their sensible scale, cautious underwriting and long experience in the market, to make the case that they don’t have a private credit problem. But Bank of America <https://www.ft.com/content/9719a659-083d-4605-9238-4292b851439a?syn-25a6b1a6=1> doesn’t believe it, and is pitching a “medium-size short” basket trade of European stocks with private credit exposure which they believe to have 30% downside or more, since they haven’t sold off as much as US peers.
It can’t help that some LBO debt deals <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-18/wall-street-buckles-up-for-white-knuckle-ride-on-lbo-debt-sales?srnd=homepage-uk> are in danger of getting “hung” <https://www.ft.com/content/ce9a67da-62df-484e-9f50-075d4be7817f?syn-25a6b1a6=1>, and IPOs postponed <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/companies-trim-delay-ipos-2026-volatility-tests-valuations-2026-02-20/>. This was meant to be a “top decile” year for investment banking revenue, and that may yet turn out to be the case. But it seems that, like a good dinosaur movie, it’s going to give us a few jump-scares on the way to the happy ending.
Meanwhile …
It might be a sign of the times that 51% of buy-side traders are complaining that their biggest source of stress, fatigue and burnout is the internal technology that they have to work with. That’s almost twice as stressful as compliance (27%), and much worse than annoying brokers and counterparties (17%) or even career uncertainty (25%). But an ice-cool 11% of traders claim that they don’t feel any stress at all. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-18/stock-traders-are-facing-burnout-from-tech-angst-study-shows>)
UBS has completed the transfer of Credit Suisse clients in Switzerland onto their own IT systems, so far without major issues. The integration team had apparently got into the habit of ringing cowbells to celebrate major goalposts. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/c7c9e78b-e007-4ec6-a4b4-9a559e931fe3?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
One of the main reasons to have co-heads is that if one goes, you still have the other. So it’s potentially quite interesting that both of the “co-managing directors of real assets” responsible for private markets at the Australian sovereign wealth fund are leaving simultaneously. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-18/private-markets-chiefs-quit-australia-s-sovereign-wealth-fund>)
Charlotte Nutting has joined Capula to be its new head of central execution trading, after six years at Millennium. Capula is apparently beefing up its execution team; it’s got a vacancy advertised for a trader in New York. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/capula-hires-senior-millennium-trader-nutting-b7e1c0e7?mod=topic_news>)
JPMorgan is setting up an “athletes’ council” with some of the biggest names from professional sport, to help it create products that take sports stars from college to retirement and beyond. (Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/jpmorgan-partners-athletes-brady-wade-wealth-management-2026-3>)
While some Asian firms are reinstating work-from-home arrangements in order to save money on rising energy prices, bankers in Bangladesh are even being asked to take the bus rather than drive private cars into work. (Daily Star <https://www.thedailystar.net/business/news/bangladesh-bank-asks-bankers-avoid-cars-use-public-transport-4126286>)

item.title: The star trader UBS won in a bidding war has gone on his way
item.description:
Jonas Klink has left UBS. It's not clear why, but some new people are doing his job in London.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
UBS declined to comment on Klink's whereabouts. It's understood that Adam Krawiec is the bank's new head of SSA and covered bond trading, effective immediately and that Kilian Frensch is its new head of EUR & GBP flow rates trading, effectively immediately.
Both roles were formerly the domain of Klink, who was UBS's head of EUR and GBP linear rates trading. He did not respond to a request to comment.
We don't know whether UBS's intended Klink's departure. It wasn't so long ago that the Swiss bank was said to pay $2m for him in a frenzy of bidding to entice </news/jonas-klink> him away from Citi. It's also not so long ago that it's thought to have paid Klink and his team handsomely for generating $80m in profits last year.
Klink is not today's only exit. We understand that Jess Eagel, the head of US flow rates trading also took flight.
UBS's rates team is in a period of flux. At least eleven people </news/ubs-markets-bonuses> have now left in the past year and risk takers are thought to be thin on the ground. Shane O’Cuinn, who once ran Credit Suisse's small macro business before running Credit Suisse's non-core unit, is now in charge of the UBS rates business. O’Cuinn is thought to have hired Frensch, who also once worked for Credit Suisse.
Many of those exiting UBS are thought to have done so by choice.

item.title: "Electronic trading internships are an eight-week job interview. This is how you convert one."
item.description:
I’ve secured multiple internships at electronic trading firms </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> and am returning to one full-time this year. In my prior internships outside the sector, I was essentially handed a problem and left alone to work on it. In quant trading, </news/quant-jobs-finance> internships </news/2025-quant-interns-are-earning-5k-per-week-at-citadel-d-e-shaw-and-point72>are more like an 8-week interview. </news/quant-interview-questions-finance>
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
You’ll usually do a project during your internship in which you’re solving some kind of problem, and you’ll have an hour a day at least to spend with some traders </news/four-habits-of-highly-successful-quant-traders> and talk about what you’re doing. There’s a sort of cold dance through which work is performed. It can feel quite dramatic, and I feel like some people go into it wrongheadedly. Those people are a bit too keen on being correct and winning arguments, flailing about to prove their point. In their eyes they’re doing better than everyone else but they’re missing the point.
It’s not fundamentally about being correct but working together to find a solution through a Socratic process. The dynamics of this can be quite varied. Sometimes, you and the traders will come up with ideas for a problem and work through it together, but there’s also a more destructive side.
Once you have a theory for your problem, someone may look at what you’ve done and rattle questions for your ideas until you can’t answer any more. They’ll ask you ‘why did you do it this way and not that way?’ They'll find a reason why your argument doesn’t work and see how you respond to that. They’ll also ask you to explain a technique or technology they don’t recognize and teach it to them.
Depending on the personality of the trader, this can feel very harsh. You’re not arguing with them, but the psychology of it makes you feel like you’re in a pressure cooker; the temperature is slowly increasing and you’re inside trying to bash it open.
There are happier stories too. You’ll give a trader your narrative and they’ll say they had the same idea. Even then though, ideas aren’t treated as precious. They’re taken seriously, but they’re also thrown around.
A discussion ends one of three ways. You might convince them of your idea, but if you fail to do so, it will take the form of either admitting a mistake or agreeing to disagree. These discussions are fundamentally a good thing, and responding to them well is important. It’s an effective way of internalizing basic principles, and if you did get something wrong, it’s good to have it isolated and excavated so you understand why it’s wrong.
Even if you do things right, you might not get in; </news/balyasny-s-quant-research-head-to-interns-with-no-return-offer-learn-to-suffer-successfully>some people in my cohort who I was very confident in did not get a return offer. I thought I only had a 30% chance and was very surprised I received mine.
Samuel King is a pseudonym

item.title: Hedge fund Balyasny's Abu Dhabi office has its inaugural member
item.description:
Last December, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-12-08/uae-hedge-fund-balyasny-joins-hudson-bay-in-expansion-to-abu-dhabi-from-dubai> reported that Balyasny Asset Management (BAM) was opening an office in Abu Dhabi to accompany its existing office in Dubai. Three months later, this has come to pass.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Yash Agarwal, an ex-ExodusPoint portfolio manager who went on gardening leave last May, has arrived at BAM. Agarwal is based neither in London nor New York as we anticipated, </news/hedge-fund-balasny-hired-one-of-exodus-point-s-top-london-portfolio-managers-to-build-a-team>but is understood to be in Abu Dhabi. It is thought that he is BAM's first employee on the ground in the emirate.
BAM declined to comment and Agarwal didn't respond to a request to comment for this article.
BAM is expected to begin operations in Abu Dhabi during the first half of this year. It's still awaiting authorisation from the regulator.
Agarwal is a global equities portfolio manager and was understood to be one of ExodusPoint's top PMs in London. He is expected to build a new team at BAM.
Agarwal's arrival in Abu Dhabi comes amidst war in the Middle East and looks like a vote for the emirate's ongoing success. Tens of hedge funds have arrived in Abu Dhabi in recent years. Late last year, the emirate was planning a $16bn expansion of Al Maryah Island, where the hedge funds hang out.

item.title: Optiver hired a quant trader from Millennium's central execution book
item.description:
Optiver, the high frequency trading firm </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> that's based in Amsterdam has made a hire from hedge fund Millennium.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Lloyd Satchwell, a London-based trader who was a quant trader on Millennium's central execution book, has joined Optiver to run the central risk book according to his LinkedIn profile.
Satchwell didn't respond to an overture that may have resulted in a comment.
Satchwell described himself as working on the central execution book at Millennium. Speaking last year </news/what-is-a-quant-researcher-hedge-fund>, Giuseppe Paleologo, the head of quantitative research at Balyasny Asset Management (BAM), said the purpose of a central liquidity book at a multistrategy hedge fund is to allocate risk to individual portfolio managers and strategies at appropriate moments. Quant researchers often occupy such roles: last year, Thomas Yang, a former quant researcher at Citadel turned head of the central liquidity book at BAM left for North Rock Capital Management.
Last April, Millennium appointed Mark Holder as its global head of central liquidity strategies. Holder was a former CTO at Citadel in Chicago.

item.title: A Citi banking MD with two MBAs left of his own volition, as did a director
item.description:
How many MBAs </news/best-mba-for-finance-banking> are required to do a technology investment banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank>deal? If you're Davide Russo, a senior technology banker, two are advisable: one from IESE in Spain, focused on broad corporate strategy; one from the London business school, focused on venture capital </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-venture-capital> and entrepreneurship.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Three years ago, Russo took his MBAs to Citi, </news/2023/02/family-office-jobs-singapore> where he became the head of EMEA technology services investment banking. Now he has taken his MBAs off again.
Citi isn't commenting and Russo didn't respond to a request to comment, but multiple sources say Russo has handed in his resignation. It's not clear what he intends to do next, but the possibilities are manifold. Immediately before he joined Citi, Russo was head of strategy and investments for ByteDance, which owns TikTok. He is thus equipped for both banking or corporate development roles.
Russo's apparent resignation now is not unsurprising. Bonuses </news/banker-bonuses>have been paid for last year and bankers everywhere are moving on.
Russo's is not the only exit at Citi. It's understood that Andrea Canavesio has also resigned as a director in the financial sponsor group.
Citi's financial sponsors group is now run by Sidharth Punshi, the former co-head of the financial sponsor group at JPMorgan. Punshi joined Citi in late 2024 and is said to be a dominant force in both the financial sponsors and tech teams.
Earlier this month, Ashu Khullar, Citi's head of global asset managers, told Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/citi-braces-for-busy-spell-for-financial-sponsors-deals-in-asia?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260310&utm_campaign=author_16891741>this will likely be a busy year for financial sponsors deals. “From private equity to infrastructure to credit, alternative asset managers are once again more actively participating in big transactions.”
Sources at Citi in London say the European team may leak more people before the year is out.

item.title: Jane Street is hiring "rigorously" to maintain "intellectual density"
item.description:
Electronic trading firm </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> is the talk of the town, for good reasons (like its $10bn trading haul </news/jane-street-profit-margin> in Q2 last year) and for bad (like its alleged involvement in the crash of TerraUSD </news/terraform-labs-jane-street-bryce-pratt>). The firm's headcount has ballooned, putting more eyes on it than ever, but CTO Ron Minsky doesn't expect the growth to be too wild going forward.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Speaking on his podcast, Signals and Threads <https://signalsandthreads.com/>, Minsky said the firm has grown by 10-30% in each of the last few years and said "when we’ve been on the upper range of that, we’re like, wow, this is really uncomfortable." Jane Street had just under 3,000 staff </news/jane-street-s-rush-of-recruiter-hiring-suggests-headcount-will-follow-revenues#:~:text=Jane%20Street%20employed%202%2C960%20people%2C%20up%20from%202%2C631%20at%20the%20end%20of%202023.> at the end of 2024 and (according to LinkedIn) had around 3,300 at the end of 2025. Last year was presumably a comfortable year for hiring.
Minsky said that Jane Street doesn't want to grow too fast so that it can "take the time to absorb people into the organization." It's also about taking the time to hire the right people in the first place. Minsky said Jane Street is "very rigorous about hiring" and wants to bring in people who are both technically proficient, and "nice and humble." Jane Street wants people who "aren’t made super uncomfortable about being wrong because we’re all wrong a lot." Minsky also said that not-so-nice hires who make work less fun for everyone else "lowers the intellectual density."
Jane Street doesn't tend to hire many experienced candidates for its trading </news/jane-street-s-traders-on-1-4m-have-fighter-pilot-eyes-high-pain-thresholds>and engineering </news/jane-street-entry-level-pay>roles, instead preferring campus recruiting. </news/jane-street-target-universities> Minsky said Jane Street's lack of formal titles </news/working-for-jane-street>is a significant barrier to entry for many of these experienced candidates but that the firm will make exceptions for elite talent; he said Jane Street will be "more intentional about trying to get them into the right spot and make sure that people quickly realize... this actually is a person who’s substantively worth including and listening to."
While you shouldn't be mean if you want to work at Jane Street, you don't have to be outgoing and collaborative to thrive either. Minsky said individual contributors who are "super good at their job and not built to be large scale leaders can still be enormously valuable and enormously well paid." Jane Street paid $1.4m on average </news/jane-street-s-1-4bn-pay-is-less-communist-than-2005-goldman-sachs> to its staff in 2024 and appeared to pay upwards of $2m </news/jane-street-versus-citadel-securities-pay> per head to staff last year.

item.title: Rothschild & Co. MD in Paris leaves for rival, American, boutique
item.description:
Rothschild & Co. is an illustrious boutique bank </news/boutique-investment-banks>. It was even the Ideal Boutique Bank Employer </news/best-place-to-work-boutique-bank> in our 2025 Ideal Employer report </ideal-employer> – but that doesn’t mean the people who work there will stay there forever.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Alexis Masson joined Evercore as a managing director (MD) in its M&A team earlier today, based in Paris. He joined the American firm from rival boutique Rothschild & Co., where he spent 5 years. During his time at Rothschild (and also Morgan Stanley before it), Masson lectured on financial modelling and later M&A at the prestigious Sciences Po </news/2020/10/french-bankers-working-in-universities>, his alma matter and one of France’s Grandes École </news/2023/02/best-grande-ecole-finance>.
Leaving a bank like Rothschild in Paris is a gamble, but Evercore might be a tempting out. The American boutique’s Paris office, which it planned to open </news/2020/06/evercores-paris-office-put-on-hold> in 2020, was delayed to 2023, when it launched with just 15 or so dealmakers. Bloomberg reported <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-09-09/evercore-launches-in-paris-as-dealmakers-navigate-french-turmoil> back in 2024 that there were some 20; it’s not clear how many there are now, but presumably more.
At the same time, not all gambles play out. According to market intelligence provider LSEG (formally Refinitiv), Evercore did very poorly in French M&A in 2025 – it lost almost two thirds of its market share based on deal size versus 2024 and slipped from 12th in 2024 in LSEG’s league table to 21st in 2025.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Technologists at UBS have good reason to feel nervous. Multistrategy hedge funds want a piece of your soul
item.description:
Investment banks </news/what-is-an-investment-bank>' IT systems are meant to be governed by the “Law of Five Nines <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Five_nines>”. That’s the standard of 99.999% uptime which is regarded as the highest levels of reliability that can be delivered by computer and telecoms systems. For systems that are “always on”, this translates to no more than five and a half minutes of outage every year; for a trading system that’s only in use during market opening hours, you might arguably stretch that to a couple of hours.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
However, in the real world, trading systems are just as likely to be subject to “Finagle’s Law of Dynamic Negatives <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Finagle%27s_law>”, which states that “if something can possibly go wrong, not only will it go wrong, but it will do so at the worst possible moment”. And this scientific principle appears to have been in action this week for UBS <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-17/ubs-faced-technology-outage-that-impacted-trading-business?srnd=phx-industries-finance>.
It doesn’t look like the outage they’ve experienced has been particularly far outside the operating envelope. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-17/ubs-faced-technology-outage-that-impacted-trading-business> reports that “some trading" at the bank was "halted" yesterday, which implies that most of it wasn’t. The bank reportedly identified the problem and deployed a fix within the day. If something like this had happened during a quiet summer afternoon, it would hardly have been noticed.
But, of course, markets are absolutely not quiet at the moment. Commodities, equities and rates are all experiencing volatility due to events in the Gulf, and some banks have already <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-17/deutsche-bank-ceo-warns-trading-revenue-may-decline-this-quarter> warned that revenues are being affected. There are few things more frightening than the rage of a trader who is stuck in a losing position because they can’t get their screen to work.
The outage comes at the worst possible time for UBS techies in career terms, as well. They already have things to worry about </news/first-ubs-s-head-of-technology-left-now-tech-people-are-being-cut>. Mike Dargan, the former COO and group chief technology officer, left last December. The "final phase" of <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-18/ubs-nears-full-credit-suisse-integration-by-finishing-it-project?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260318&utm_campaign=author_21003048> the integration of the Credit Suisse systems is underway and the bank plans to cut 3,000 technology jobs in order to realise the synergies.
There’s no evidence that any of these things were particularly related to the recent outages, except the general principle that happy ships always seem to have better luck. It will have been a particularly frustrating episode for UBS top management as well. When you’re engaged in a battle <https://www.ft.com/content/33c66cc4-6301-4643-92a7-8426e5575120> with the regulators over whether they’re being too tight and controlling, it’s embarrassing to have any slip-up of any kind, no matter how small.
So, there might be a bit of “blamestorming” going on, somewhere in UBS this week, as people look around to find who was standing closest to the errant server when it went down. Usually the most sensible thing to do in this sort of period is to find someone who is leaving the bank anyway, and ask them to do a solid last favour on their way out.
Elsewhere, the latest euphemism for hedge fund managers is “pod exhaustion <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-03-17/traders-are-ditching-giant-hedge-funds-to-set-their-own-terms>”. This describes the way that you feel if your investment style isn’t suited to the tight risk limits and centralised management of a multistrategy fund </news/big-multistrategy-hedge-funds>. As a reason for striking out and starting your own fund, it certainly sounds better than “losing money” or “being fired”.
The culture issues are real, though. Granted, big pod shops are able to make a persuasive case that they have the resources to look after their people (one recruiter even hired a night nurse for a trader with a newborn baby, so that he could get a few hours sleep and consider the job offer properly). But as well as “short term and repeatable” returns, they want your full commitment – if you have a social media presence or a research publishing business, like macro manager Alfonso Pecciatello, that’s likely to be a deal breaker.
That’s why some traders are turning down multistrat offers, and others are taking pay cuts in order to be their own boss. (Even the strongest performing pod shop managers are, increasingly, “spinning out” their own funds). The problem, though, is that it’s very difficult to make anything like as much money this way. As one multistrategy recruiter bluntly put it, “What are you doing with this Mickey Mouse little fund?”
Meanwhile …
AI evangelists have discovered that people who have a Bloomberg Terminal tend to get really cross at any suggestion that it might get taken away, and consequently don’t like being told that a vibe-coded startup will replace the whole system. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/bloomberg-terminal-perplexity-vibe-coding-e37a95f8?mod=mhp>)
Somewhat less emotionally, a markets professional explains how it’s not just the code and data that gives the Terminal its stickiness. (Rupak Ghose <https://rupakghose.substack.com/p/bloomberg-is-dead-long-live-bloomberg>)
The management consultancy equivalent of “pod exhaustion” is “AI brain-fry”. The secret to avoiding it is to limit the number of agents and tools that you’re meant to be keeping track of at any one time. (Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/ai-brain-fry-bcg-consulting-exhaustion-agents-work-2026-3>)
After the controversial collapse of his alternative investment firm, Mark Sowerby took solace in swimming very long distances in extremely cold and/or shark infested seas and nearly dying a lot. There’s a film out about the lessons he learned. (Guardian <https://www.theguardian.com/film/2026/mar/16/dont-be-prey-review-marathon-swimming-documentary>)
“The loudest voices in crypto are rarely the richest”. A journalist starts writing an article about the new digital zillionaires, but then finds that people are not quite as keen to talk about the new world when the bitcoin price is falling. Featuring Mike Novogratz, Cathie Wood and a VC who rates potential founders on a scale of “rizz and tiz” (charisma and autism). (Vanity Fair <https://www.vanityfair.com/news/story/cryptos-true-believers>)
“Great … six months, guys”? If the SEC goes through with its proposal to remove the obligation for quoted US firms to report quarterly, it may involve a step change in the need for investor relations professionals, lawyers and analysts, all of whose workflow has been organised around the seasonal rhythm of results announcements. (Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/quarterly-earnings-proposal-sec-accountants-lawyer-investor-relations-jobs-impact-2026-3>)
Why are insect farming startups going bankrupt? The answer is apparently a little more complex than “why do you think”. (Naked Capitalism <https://www.nakedcapitalism.com/2026/03/why-insect-farming-startups-are-going-bankrupt.html?utm_source=dlvr.it&utm_medium=twitter>)

item.title: Standard Chartered hired an MD in UBS' London investment bank and moved him to Singapore
item.description:
Standard Chartered </news/standard-chartered-asia> is planning to rapidly expand its private banking team </news/this-is-what-your-private-banking-salary-and-bonus-should-be-in-singapore> in Singapore, adding up to 50 relationship managers in the city <https://www.ft.com/content/716253b0-762b-4578-84f2-01354e71b2c4>for Chinese clients. Its investment bank in Singapore is getting some love too; the bank recently hired a UBS MD that spent the last decade in London.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Dan Folke Persson announced on LinkedIn yesterday that he's joining Standard Chartered as chief risk officer for its corporate and investment bank (CIB), and chief risk officer for Europe, the Americas, MENA and South Asia. He was most recently chief risk officer for UBS' investment bank but was also previously global head of market risk for the bank.
Persson also headed risk for UBS' non-core and legacy unit, charged with winding down thousands of applications as the bank integrated Credit Suisse's technology. The bank anticipates that it will have fully integrated Credit Suisse by the end of this year. </news/ubs-has-only-scratched-the-surface-of-credit-suisse-s-legacy-technology>
It's not Persson's first time being moved to Asia. He was moved from Europe to Hong Kong when he was promoted to MD, becoming global head of equities and EM market risk at UBS.
Standard Chartered has been hiring in the front office too. Jeremy Low <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/standard-chartered-hires-low-to-lead-oil-and-gas-m-a-0c55bee6> joined Standard Chartered as its global head of oil and gas M&A from Houlihan Lokey this week. He'll be staying in London </news/standard-chartered-q4-2025> for the role, however.

item.title: Hedge funds and market makers need data strategists as war heats up
item.description:
How can you achieve a trading advantage in the fog of war? How about satellite imagery of damage to oil refineries, data on tanker movements, or movements of troops? Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/hedge-funds-alternative-data-iran-war-2026-3>reported last week that hedge funds are turning to this kind of data amidst war in the Middle East. And there were already signs that data strategists and sourcing experts are on the move.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
In February 2025, Drew Lee, the VP of financial data at DE Shaw disappeared on gardening leave after 20 years with the fund. Last week, Lee announced that he's joined proprietary trading firm Tower Research as head of data strategy and and sourcing.
Lee is not the only senior person moving in the space. Evan Reich, the former head of data strategy and sourcing at Verition recently moved to intelligence firm BWG Global. Tony Berkman, a Two Sigma managing director who specialises in large empirical data sets, has been on gardening leave for the past year and is due to resurface.
Most funds already have large data sourcing teams and those that don't have been building them. Balyasny Asset Management for example spent 2025 adding to its data team under Jeremy Ridder Brunelli, the head of alt data and geospatial research who joined from UBS in 2022.
For those who want to get in at the bottom, Verition is currently hiring data sourcing associates in New York. The roles reportedly involve sourcing the data, negotiating its price, onboarding, ensuring it's legitimate (governance) and analysing how portfolio managers use it.
Alt data specialists work on data that delivers investible narratives in real time. Citadel has one of the largest teams within its data strategies group. Since November, this has been run by Jian Xu, a Citadel data scientise who launched the firm's "Alternative Data Observatory." Xu says this is now used by over 30% of Citadel's investment professionals.

item.title: "My boss at Morgan Stanley failed to notice that I was about to quit"
item.description:
Last year, I left Morgan Stanley. I was made redundant, but this was fine by me because I was about to quit anyway.
I spent multiple years at the bank and survived years of difficult managers. I worked in the sort of quantitative role that attracts people with a high IQ but a terrible EQ. They have devoted their lives to the job and once they become senior they call the shots in a way that makes them difficult to work for.
The same thing occurs at most banks, and it can be suffocating. I decided that I wanted to leave.
Long before I was laid off, I started to make preparations to go. I removed the trinkets and personal effects from my desk until there was nothing easily attributable to me, besides some dirty napkins.
Once my desk was devoid of personal effects, I began interviewing </news/quant-interview-questions-finance> elsewhere. People say that “interviewing is tough” and “there are so few openings.” It's true that we are in a “low fire, low hire” environment. But for someone with my skillset (niche coding skills, foreign languages, trading experience), the hiring market is far from dead.
I let it be known that I was looking for a role and I was swarmed by hungry recruiters. I didn't even have to send a CV. I quickly secured an offer from a top hedge fund and prepared to resign.
To my shock, though, three days before my resignation date, my managing director (MD) told me I was laid off. I received a severance package and several months' salary, even though I was about to leave the bank of my own accord and it would have cost them nothing at all!
Needless to say I accepted the payoff gladly, but to this date I'm astonished that it happened like this. I already had a foot out the door. My desk was clear. Somehow, my MD completely failed to notice. It's a sign that in quant roles at least, MDs are completely incapable of reading the room.
It worked out great for me. I now manage my own book at a top fund and don't have to report to these people.
Nathan Osborne is a pseudonym

item.title: Electronic market makers trading on Kalshi are small fish in a big pond
item.description:
In traditional financial markets, electronic market makers </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay#:~:text=Electronic%20Market%20Making,hedge%20their%20risk.>are juggernauts. Citadel Securities </news/citadel-securities-vs-jane-street>, for example, is involved in 25% of all trades in the US equities market just by itself. In the burgeoning asset class of prediction market event contracts, these firms are much less prominent.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Speaking on Stripe founder John Collison's Cheeky Pint podcast <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LsNmBcDCsq0>, Kalshi </news/tarek-mansour-kalshi-sam-bankman-fried-ftx> co-founder Luana Lopes Lara said that only 5% of bid matches on the platform are from "the big institutional market makers you'd think about." The other 95% comes from "over 2,000" smaller market makers, some of which are just individuals.
Kalshi's founders didn't say which institutions are market making on Kalshi. Susquehanna International Group has been a vocal fan of prediction markets, and Jump Trading quietly started making markets on the platform <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-11-20/jump-trading-quietly-joins-event-betting-craze-as-a-market-maker> late last year, and has been given an equity stake in the company for its services. Crypto investment bank Galaxy Digital was also reportedly in discussions <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-11-24/novogratz-s-galaxy-digital-experimenting-in-prediction-markets> to start a small market making unit for prediction markets. Other than that, market makers operating on Kalshi are doing so very quietly.
There's likely even less institutional participation on Polymarket </news/polymarket-shayne-coplan>, which has had less of an emphasis on regulation as Kalshi. One insider told us the market-making space there is "mostly just a bunch of cowboys." However, Jump Trading is thought to have a similar arrangement with Polymarket as it does with Kalshi.
There are multiple reasons that market makers don't want to join the train. Tarek Mansour, Kalshi co-founder, said on the podcast that "traditional Wall Street market makers are not geared up for" the breadth of markets available on Kalshi; "it's not like they can spin up a new desk to price politics or price culture in an hour." Lopes Lara said that long tail markets like "will One Direction have a reunion" (or Polymarket's famous 'Will Jesus Christ return' <https://polymarket.com/event/will-jesus-christ-return-before-2027> market) are particularly hard to price; she said Kalshi "actually [has] to incentivize market makers to come in.”
Many markets on Kalshi are traditionally taboo for electronic trading firms, </news/hft-pay-london> particularly sports contracts. Multiple major market makers have had sports trading teams </news/quants-sports-betting>, but it is seen as a 'lesser' market with higher risk that can harm your ability to bring in investors. One major electronic market maker denied having a sports trading team </news/quant-sports-betting-jobs>in 2024 despite multiple headhunters telling us the contrary. One of those headhunters said that the success of Kalshi and Polymarket hasn't changed much about their attitudes to sports trading.
While institutions aren't too hot on Kalshi yet, Kalshi is certainly interested in institutional talent. Peter Wagner, managing director at quant recruitment firm Affinity North said that Kalshi is "a new player in the market for elite talent" in his Q1 market report. Lopes Lara said in the podcast that the firm has just 120 employees at the moment but that may soon change.
