item.title: The best leveraged finance MDs earn the most now; directors not so much
item.description:
It's an interesting time to work in leveraged finance, and if you're a managing director (MD), it also appears to be a very lucrative one.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
A new compensation survey from search firm Prospect Rock Partners says MDs in leveraged finance earned the most for last year amidst high competition for experienced talent.
When salaries and bonuses </news/banker-bonuses> are combined, Prospect Rock says compensation for US-based leveraged finance MDs was $1.6m for 2025, versus an average of $1.1m across other areas of investment banking (including M&A and equity capital markets).
Below MD level, however, Prospect Rock found that compensation in leveraged finance was below other areas. Junior directors, for example, earned an average of $578k versus an average of $670k across investment banking teams as a whole.
Prospect Rock's full report is available here. <https://prospectrockpartners.com/>Sample sizes are relatively small and outliers may have skewed results. However, it's easy to see why banks might be willing to pay extra for top leveraged finance talent. - With now interest rates likely to stay higher for longer, experience is needed to navigate the market. Just ask the JPMorgan bankers <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-14/jpmorgan-makes-bold-push-to-offload-huge-lbo-debt-credit-weekly>who have $30bn in combined debt for Electronic Arts and Sealed Air Corp to market.
Prospect Rock managing partner Meridith Dennes says demand for experienced leveraged finance and restructuring professionals has picked up in 2026. "The dislocation in private credit markets has created real complexity for borrowers and lenders alike, and companies need experienced talent who can navigate that environment — whether that's structuring new deals or working through situations that need to be repositioned."

item.title: Employees at a London fintech paying "significant" bonuses are divided on its culture
item.description:
Checkout.com </news/checkout-guillaume-pousaz-work-from-home>, once the most valuable fintech in the UK in 2022, <https://www.theguardian.com/business/2022/jan/13/checkoutcom-becomes-uks-most-valuable-fintech-at-40bn> has emerged from a challenging period for the sector looking stronger than ever. In the payments firm's 2025 annual letter <https://www.checkout.com/blog/annual-letter-2025> it revealed that revenue was up over 30% for the second consecutive year. Insiders point to Checkout's unique hard-driving culture as a reason for its success.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
We've spoken to various current and former Checkout.com employees. They say one of the firm's idiosyncrasies is its "highly decentralised model where each team owns everything end-to-end, from infrastructure to product development." For example, when the firm migrated its cloud infrastructure </news/cloud-engineer-job-security-finance> from Snowflake to Google Cloud's BigQuery, each team handled its own migration independently.
Jenny Hadlow, COO of Checkout, told eFinancialCareers that the company is "departmental" rather than decentralized, with a focus on "incredibly modular" products. Checkout's products are designed to be mixed and matched rather than sold in bulk, so the firm gives its "product leaders the autonomy to build in a super fast-paced environment." She said that Checkout's teams push updates to their infrastructure and products on a daily basis.
There are benefits and drawbacks to Checkout's model. One technologist said building a product at Checkout is "attractive from a learning perspective and is exciting initially," but can become stressful and inefficient at scale. "This model may have contributed to burnout </news/finance/fintech-worse-than-banking-hours> in some teams," he said. Another employee said there's "nothing inherently wrong with it, but Checkout is very specific in how it wants to operate. I just could not get on with it."
It ties into one of Checkout's twelve operating principles, 'Be The Owner,' which Hadlow said is one of the most important things she looks for in prospective employees. Employees are expected to be willing to contribute in "every challenge and opportunity in what is a very complicated industry."
Checkout holds its employees to a high standard. One former employee said that "OKRs tended to be ambitious, and there was a strong managerial focus on delivering them." He said that engineers in particular weren't happy with that setup.
Checkout has taken a similar approach to Revolut </news/six-red-flags-that-revolut-hates-in-its-engineers> by developing an internal metrics-focused performance management tool. Checkout's tool, Fabric, had its first full year of use in 2025, and is designed to ensure Checkout employees adhere to the firm's operating principles.
We've heard varying reports on the level of turnover in the organization as a result of this performance driven culture. Some employees said they "didn't observe unusually high turnover" while others said they noticed "a lot... both voluntarily and involuntarily." Checkout's jobs data seems to suggest there have been departures. In its annual letter it said it posted 700 jobs last year, but only grew headcount by 100. However, those jobs are also thought to include internal promotions, pipeline hiring like internships, and listings that have carried over into 2026.
208,000 people applied for jobs at Checkout last year, meaning less than 0.4% of applicants get hired </news/checkout-fintech>. Hadlow said the firm has a "really strong belief" in its current talent pool, and is up front about its expectations in the application process. The firm takes a rigorous approach to hiring, with "hundreds of hiring managers across the company," and has instituted a 'bar raiser' stage in which Checkout's highest performing and longest serving employees come in at the end of the hiring process and assess candidates to make sure they align with the operating principles. This seems to be working, as Checkout has raised its net promoter score <https://www.qualtrics.com/en-gb/articles/customer-experience/net-promoter-score/> to 42, which it says is in the top quartile of technology businesses.
One female employee said she had an "awful" manager and described Checkout as "a bit of a boys club," a sentiment seen in multiple reviews on Glassdoor. Hadlow said this isn't reflective of her experience but that Checkout "listens everywhere" and "[doesn't] want to diminish those voices." She said the bar raiser program and Checkout's metric-focused culture are efforts to remove unconscious bias, and said that the company is currently 39% female. "We recognize that we need to keep pushing those boundaries; we're in an industry where that profile and skew has always been more heavy." </news/gender-pay-gap-investment-banking-goldman-sachs-jpmorgan#:~:text=The%20Gender%20Pay%20Gap%20In%20Fintech,but%20none%20did%20better%20than%2010%25.>
Employees say Checkout is "doing really well as a business." To retain its high-performers, they also say Checkout pays a lot of money. </news/top-london-fintechs> The ex-employee said "bonuses can be significant" if you hit your OKRs, and the female employee said Checkout pays "quite a bit above the industry standard for a lot of jobs as a way to try and convince people to stay." In one of its UK entities, Checkout paid an average of $110k to over 1,000 employees in "wages and salaries, including bonus and termination benefits" in 2024.

item.title: Citi's London DCM team has lost various people for various reasons
item.description:
Bonuses have been paid and people are moving on </news/ubs-s-new-head-of-high-yield-trading-is-leaving-now-that-it-has-an-even-newer-one>. At Citi, they are moving on from the London debt capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> (DCM) team that has already experienced plenty of shuffling.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Citi isn't commenting. Global Capital <https://www.globalcapital.com/article/2g488mdmtynfxu6bl181s/people-and-markets/do-not-pciti-loses-three-senior-bankers#:~:text=Paul%20Gibbs%20among%20those%20departing,infrastructure%2C%20transportation%20and%20private%20finance.>reported today that there have been three exits, but we understand it's more like five.
The departures include: Paul Gibbs, who was once the bank's head of EMEA DCM but who was shunted <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-08-26/citi-shuffles-emea-debt-capital-markets-team-with-jpmorgan-hire>into a role as the head of Citi's global infrastructure, transportation and private finance businesses last August; David Dubin, who was the global head of infrastructure but was moved down a rung when Gibbs got that job; Ivan Starcevic who was in Dubai and was promoted as head of CEEMEA strategic financing last June; Lucy Devlin in capital management; plus a director on the team.
Bonus payments aside, it's not clear why all five left now. Insiders suggest each individual may have had different reasons for leaving and that the exits were not coordinated.
Gibbs, for example, was displaced <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-08-26/citi-shuffles-emea-debt-capital-markets-team-with-jpmorgan-hire>when Citi made ex-JPMorgan banker Rob Cascarino co-head of EMEA DCM alongside existing head of leveraged finance Uday Malhotra last August. Dubin then had Gibbs as his boss, but is not understand to have left for that reason. Starcevic is thought to have a better job elsewhere. Devlin may simply have wanted a change after 20 years at Citi. None of the individuals mentioned have confirmed this or responded to a request to comment.
Citi's DCM team has undergone various other alterations in recent years. Tommaso Ponsele, </news/goldman-sachs-may-be-seducing-a-key-dcm-banker-at-citi> the head of European corporate DCM, moved to Goldman Sachs last May. Colm Rainey </news/citi-dcm-bankers-fired> head of corporate and financial institutions group debt capital markets for UK & Ireland and others left in 2024.
Cascarino appeared six months ago along with the multitude of other hires </news/now-citi-hired-jp-morgan-s-former-global-co-head-of-sponsors-private-side-sales>from JPMorgan. Yesterday Citi announced the arrival of Eric Farina from Morgan Stanley </news/work-from-home-hedge-fund> as co-head of infrastructure financing and capital solutions alongside Cascarino.

item.title: UBS's new head of high yield trading is leaving now that it has an even newer one
item.description:
Last April, UBS hired a new head of European high yield trading in the form of Bharat Gupta who joined from Deutsche Bank. Last week, UBS hired itself another head of European high yield trading in the form of Robert Laux </news/bank-of-america-s-european-high-yield-trading-team-sprung-a-leak>, who is joining from UBS.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
UBS isn't commenting, but we understand that with Laux arriving, Gupta has gone. Gupta didn't respond to a request to comment on where he's gone to.
UBS insiders say the Swiss bank is sprucing up its high yield trading team after a period of lacklustre profitability. Senior saleswoman Sarah Mobbs is also thought to be off.
Laux has worked for UBS before. He was there for 10 years and became the head of EMEA leveraged finance trading before leaving for BofA in 2022. It's not clear how much UBS has paid to bring Laux back again but insiders at the Swiss bank say his return is much needed. Since he left, UBS made various other brief hires - including Carlo Armillis </news/2023/06/natwest-trading>from NatWest, who came and then went again in the style of Gupta.
Shortly after Laux left, UBS also hired Shaun Barlow from Seaport global holdings as head of European finance trading and research. It's thought that Barlow will report to Laux upon his return.

item.title: Fintech Clear Street appears to be starting a prop trading arm
item.description:
Capital markets infrastructure fintech </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-fintech> Clear Street is no stranger to encroaching on crowded markets. It came on the scene as a technologically advanced competitor in the prime brokerage space, and has also branched out into investment banking. </news/2023/07/clear-street-new-york-fintech-jobs> Now, it seems to be building out a prop trading </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay#:~:text=Prop%20Trading%20Firms,its%20own%20funds.> arm under a veteran electronic trader.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Tripp Becket announced he was joining Clear Street in New York as an MD in proprietary trading. He joins from middle-market investment bank Cantor Fitzgerald, where he was a prop trading director. Becket has worked at multiple top algorithmic trading firms; he was previously a global head of statistical arbitrage at HFT firm Susquehanna, where he spent 20 years, and was a senior quant researcher </news/quant-jobs-finance> at market making firm Citadel Securities, where he spent just over one year.
Clear Street already provides trading and execution services to hedge funds and other institutions. Insiders alleged that, in Europe at least, Clear Street had some issues selling its services to institutions </news/clear-street-s-350-engineers-may-not-be-so-rich-after-all#:~:text=Some%20are%20suggesting%20that%20Clear%20Street%20should%20have%20chased%20institutional%20accounts%20more%20vigorously.%20%22Institutions%20and%20hedge%20funds%20are%20a%20better%20client%20base%2C%22%20says%20one.%C2%A0>, which resulted in cuts to a group of senior markets professionals last year. Prop trading, however, is traditionally when a firm trades using its own money; there doesn't appear to be an existing prop trading arm, suggesting this is a new initiative, but Clear Street declined to comment.
Becket may have been anticipating that Clear Street would go public before he joined. The fintech had filed for an IPO </news/after-brutal-job-cuts-clear-street-will-make-some-tech-people-very-rich>but eventually postponed the launch </news/clear-street-s-350-engineers-may-not-be-so-rich-after-all> after its price-per-share was cut by nearly a third.

item.title: Jefferies cut its London pay after profits rose 40%
item.description:
Jefferies, the American investment bank run by Richard Handler </news/2025-s-banking-interns-arrived-at-an-interesting-time-it-feels-surreal>, published results this week for its London-based subsidiary, Jefferies International Limited for the year ending November 2025. They were great, but its employees probably aren’t too happy about them.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
To start with, Jefferies’ net revenue reached £1bn ($1.3bn), an 11% increase on the period before. More impressively, its bottom-line income for the year reached £92m ($122m), a staggering 40% increase on the year before.
Despite that, Jefferies’ people in London might be disappointed. Average pay at the UK operation decreased between the two periods – from £368k ($489k) per head in November 2024 to £354k ($470k) in November 2025 – despite the strong performance.
The decline in pay was possibly due to hiring. The firm’s overall headcount increased by 8.2%, with an increase in both its front office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> staff (7.4% up) and administrative staff (9.7% up). Total compensation spend only increased by 4%, however, which wasn’t enough to make up the shortfall.
Jefferies’ huge increase in revenue was across the board. The firm’s revenue from trading commissions, investment banking fees, and research commissions was up by 28%, 27%, and 35%, respectively.

item.title: BlueCrest's chief risk officer turned up at a macro hedge fund
item.description:
Rupen Nagda is back. The former chief risk officer at BlueCrest left last year and has just resurfaced at Symmetry Investments in London.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Nagda joined Symmetry on March 17th according to a new filing with Companies House. He was last seen at BlueCrest in September 2025 after being appointed chief risk officer there in January 2020.
Nagda has presumably been on gardening leave. He's spent two decades working in markets roles and was previously a trader at Commerzbank and then head of rates market risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank> at BNP Paribas in London.
Symmetry Investments was founded by ex-Millennium portfolio managers Feng Guo and Michael Robinson in 2014. It runs systematic macro and fixed income strategies. For the year ending March 2025, Symmetry Investments UK LLP employed 28 partners in the UK, of whom the highest paid earned $55m. 133 other staff shared $62m; only 32 were in the investment team.
BlueCrest has lost other people too. Ben Souter </news/brevan-howard-s-technology-hiring-spree-came-to-blue-crest>, its head of front office technology and quant research, has gone to Brevan Howard.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Irascible hedge fund chief executive refused to work from home. Failed bankers' back-up career is having problems
item.description:
For many people in financial services, being given the option to work from home more frequently would be a fine thing. Just ask the junior bankers at UBS </news/ubs-five-days-in-office>, or staff at the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) </news/fca-return-to-office> in London. However, for a certain vintage of person, mandatory working from home is tantamount to excommunication and must be resisted at all costs.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Those people include Crispin Odey </news/crispin-odey>, the 67-year-old British hedge fund manager who ran Odey Asset Management and who liked to make an appearance in the office wearing brightly coloured shirts and ties. Odey is currently in court in the UK challenging the FCA's decision to ban him from working in financial services. Various of his foibles are being aired in the process.
One such foible was an alleged utter refusal to work from home when asked. Reporting from the court, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/odey-s-fund-executives-wanted-to-make-him-work-in-the-basement?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260316&utm_campaign=author_22884492>says that Tom Richards, who was on Odey's executive committee, said Odey wasn't keen to either work from home, take a sabbatical, or to work in the office basement to segregate him from everyone else while the firm investigated inappropriate conduct accusations against him. Odey really wanted to stay on the first floor, amidst the throng.
He's not the only chief executive to feel this way. David Solomon at Goldman Sachs famously came into the Goldman head office during COVID even when no one else was around. Jamie Dimon thinks working from home </news/jamie-dimon-work-from-home> is a road to dissolution. Ken Griffin reconvened Citadel's office in a Miami hotel when work from home threatened.
In Crispin Odey's case, the in-office insistence was linked to a refusal to be "punished" as the firm investigated the allegations. He was pretty forceful on this. When another executive visited Crispin Odey at home to discuss closing the firm, Odey was said to exhibit <https://www.thetimes.com/uk/law/article/crispin-odey-was-in-pure-rage-after-being-warned-over-behaviour-hdpwv856h?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqc8V8LHz4GOvFHPz_NXivik7ctH4zjS-z1TI4kWMDpDZove3o8chUXtZrkTcQ0%3D&gaa_ts=69b88340&gaa_sig=O0XLaMUVH2EgZgQOWuk3__mzfvgrJ3UYNHRlM2w2DucUiUf1lfHNCbvsA930HPu-F1c15mQQxiKKtmwgpMkRNw%3D%3D> "pure rage," changed colour, and made the table shake. Having such personas in the office might liven things up but is not ideal for team culture.
Separately, being an accountant is not cool. The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/4672e688-19b2-4fd9-9cd9-e18f50d6141d> reports that people can't be bothered to hang around to become a partner at Big Four firms any more. "A large chunk of my intake are failed bankers,” complains one junior auditor, who says he's surrounded by people who are "second tier."
The grumbling comes as accounting firms deploy AI to do junior work. On one hand, this deployment is expected to "help make the role even more exciting," in the words of senior auditors trying to sell the career. On the other, it means that fewer graduates are being hired and that those who are aren't very enthused about their futures.
It doesn't help that incumbents say junior accounting roles pay poorly and involve “tedious work” with “extremely long hours during busy season.” Deloitte <https://www.thetimes.com/article/604b8e9a-f2b2-4750-b752-7fce15f11572?shareToken=b1d1531d7b151634e894b27586b832e0> is attempting to combat the malaise by shortening its qualification period. 12 out of 14 ACA exams in the UK will be in future be taken in year one of Deloitte's graduate training programme instead of over three years while AI takes over juniors' repetitive tasks. Students rejected from banking jobs don't seem convinced that this will make audit jobs more appealing.
Meanwhile...
xAI is recruiting Wall Street bankers, portfolio managers, traders and credit analysts for its data annotation teams that train Grok. It particularly wants credit experts to teach financial modeling, leveraged loan syndication, distressed investing and niche bonds such as mortgage-backed securities and collateralized loan obligations. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/musk-s-xai-hiring-credit-experts-bankers-to-teach-grok-finance?srnd=phx-crypto>
John Zito, co-president of Apollo's asset-management arm, said a private-credit loan made to a generic small or midsize “Joe Software Company” might recover 20 to 40 cents on the dollar. He also queried the interest in private equity secondaries when private credit finances "80% of those portfolios." (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/top-apollo-executive-sounds-off-on-arrogance-in-private-markets-4f09b5cb?st=8q5xoB&reflink=article_copyURL_share>
A “substantial portion” of the private equity industry is already “stressed or distressed.” - “You’re not looking at a problem five years from now, you’re looking at a problem that exists today,” said Tony Yoseloff, managing partner and chief investment officer at credit hedge fund Davidson Kempner Capital Management. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/f77b2891-5d83-47a1-a328-1b526073a266>
The $42 billion Cliffwater Corporate Lending Fund has more than 3,600 individual holdings, including direct loans to middle-market corporate borrowers and ownership stakes in other private-credit funds. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/inside-a-42-billion-private-credit-black-box-more-black-boxes-e2523e8e?st=KsNMf5>
JPMorgan has begun offloading $30bn of debt related to Electronic Arts and Sealed Air Corp. It says investors already placed order for $19 billion of orders for debt related to EA. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/jpmorgan-kicks-off-5-75-billion-loan-sale-tied-to-ea-buyout>
Citi hired Eric Farina as global co-head of infrastructure financing and capital solutions from Morgan Stanley. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/odey-s-fund-executives-wanted-to-make-him-work-in-the-basement?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260316&utm_campaign=author_22884492>
Nomura is building a precious metals trading team in London and hired Aleksander Ganchev who worked for Standard Chartered. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/nomura-plans-precious-metals-trading-expansion-under-new-hire?taid=69b7cf8d75f02f0001613b18&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Stefan Bollinger, the ex-Goldman Sachs partner in white sneakers who now runs Julius Baer was paid $30m for his efforts last year, despite allegedly irritating some existing staff. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/julius-baer-ceo-pay-jumps-after-goldman-move-85f19236?mod=topic_news>
Singaporean data centre firm DayOne is filing for a $5bn IPO but it's in the US and all the banks working on it are America. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/asia-s-dayone-data-centers-is-said-to-near-confidential-filing-for-us-ipo>
Millennium pulled a $1bn investment from hedge fund Scopia after staff changes. It's not clear which changes though. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/millennium-pulls-1-billion-allocation-from-hedge-fund-scopia?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260316&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
If you're hit by a drone strike in Dubai don't take photos to share with your family at home as you will be arrested. (Daily Mail) <https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-15649801/Dubai-arrests-foreign-survivors-Iranian-drone-strike-sent-images-explosion-aftermath-loved-ones-prove-safe.html?ns_mchannel=rss&ns_campaign=1490&ito=social-twitter_mailonline>
Women experience pain more intensely than men. It's because of testosterone. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/health/wellness/pain-women-men-differences-research-testosterone-290928fe?mod=e2tw>
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. We will take reasonable steps to weed out anything that we consider to be offensive or inappropriate.
item.author: sbutcher@efinancialcareers.com (Sarah Butcher)

(Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/odey-s-fund-executives-wanted-to-make-him-work-in-the-basement?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260316&utm_campaign=author_22884492>

(Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/nomura-plans-precious-metals-trading-expansion-under-new-hire?taid=69b7cf8d75f02f0001613b18&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Stefan Bollinger, the ex-Goldman Sachs partner in white sneakers who now runs Julius Baer was paid $30m for his efforts last year, despite allegedly irritating some existing staff. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/julius-baer-ceo-pay-jumps-after-goldman-move-85f19236?mod=topic_news>
Singaporean data centre firm DayOne is filing for a $5bn IPO but it's in the US and all the banks working on it are America. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/asia-s-dayone-data-centers-is-said-to-near-confidential-filing-for-us-ipo>
Millennium pulled a $1bn investment from hedge fund Scopia after staff changes. It's not clear which changes though. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/millennium-pulls-1-billion-allocation-from-hedge-fund-scopia?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260316&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
If you're hit by a drone strike in Dubai don't take photos to share with your family at home as you will be arrested. (Daily Mail) <https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-15649801/Dubai-arrests-foreign-survivors-Iranian-drone-strike-sent-images-explosion-aftermath-loved-ones-prove-safe.html?ns_mchannel=rss&ns_campaign=1490&ito=social-twitter_mailonline>
Women experience pain more intensely than men. It's because of testosterone. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/health/wellness/pain-women-men-differences-research-testosterone-290928fe?mod=e2tw>

item.title: Bank of America's big Morgan Stanley rates trader slipped away after three short years
item.description:
Remember 2023? That was the year when Bank of America began its strategy of hiring as few people as possible </news/2023/03/driss-haj-khlifa-bank-of-america >and hoping that existing staff left of their own accords. It was also the year that BofA made an exception to this rule and recruited Driss Haj Khlifa </news/2023/03/driss-haj-khlifa-bank-of-america> from Morgan Stanley for its Paris rates desk.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Three years later, multiple sources say Haj Khlifa has gone. BofA declined to comment on his exit. It's not clear why he left.
Haj Khlifa is a euro swaps trader. Such traders have been in demand from hedge funds </news/escaped-nomura-rates-trader-joins-hedge-fund-amidst-bonus-rumblings> and from other banks. There are suggestions that Haj Khlifa might be joining Citi. At BofA, he reported to Paris-based Kal El-Wahab, the head of EMEA linear rates trading, who is still in his seat.

item.title: Goldman Sachs cut only one group of people in London after blockbuster year
item.description:
Goldman Sachs’ London-based business, Goldman Sachs International, published its report for 2025 earlier this month. It did great; revenue was up by 16% to $11.8bn. Somehow, the bank even managed to hire some people across the board. Well, mostly across the board.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
As the chart below shows. Investment banking </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> headcount at Goldman Sachs International was up 8.2%, equities sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> headcount up 1.1%, investment management headcount up 10.6%, and even support functions (such as compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance>) headcount was up by 9.8%.
There was one exception. The London operation's headcount in fixed income, currencies, and commodities (FICC) sales & trading declined by 6%, going from 694 heads to 655 of them. This was despite FICC revenue at GSI increasing by 13%, going from $3.2bn to $3.6bn.
The decline in FICC headcount was a curiosity, but not entirely surprising. Fixed income trading is going electronic, and traders are being replaced by electronic systems. More surprising was the fact that Goldman increased headcount in its other London divisions. - This is the first time that Goldman increased the number of bankers it employs in London since 2022, and the first time it increased its number of equities traders since 2020. Only support functions have steadily and inevitably crept up over the years, as the chart below shows.
Goldman's fired FICC traders probably aren’t happy with their situation, but whoever’s left at the firm probably is. Goldman Sachs International paid a huge average of $963k per head in its London office in 2025, up 21% on the $795k it paid on average in 2024. That was more than double the $399k Goldman Sachs paid on average globally, according to its Q4 results </news/goldman-sachs-q4-2025>.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, WhatsApp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. item.title: Individual bankers & hedge fund managers face huge losses on off-plan Dubai property investments
item.description:
Where did you invest recent years' bonuses? If it was barrels of oil, then congratulations. If it was property in Dubai, you are not alone.
Multiple senior traders in both Dubai and London say colleagues are reeling after investing previous bonuses in off-plan property in Dubai. As house prices in the Emirates fall, they are now facing a harsh reality.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
"It's not funny. People are people are being wiped out by this," says one Goldman Sachs trader, speaking off the record. "People are freaking out," confirms a senior rates trader in London. "They've all bought off-plan property which is going to be unsellable. It was going up in price, so there are people who bought over 10 units as an investment."
Off-plan property investing has been "Dubai's game for over a decade," says one senior trader who relocated to the Emirate from London. Off-plan investing <https://www.bhomes.com/en/blog/betterinformed/the-psychology-of-buying-off-plan-why-investors-are-willing-to-wait-years-for-roi> accounted for 60% of Dubai's property sales before the war in the Middle East and an entire industry sprung up to cater for the demand. Off plan purchases typically demand multiple payments: upfront, after meeting construction milestones and upon completion. Despite the change in regional circumstances, traders say they're still being asked to meet their commitments: "Developers are forcing contracts clauses and people are still forced to pay. No delay in payment is allowed."
The impact of the war on Dubai property prices is unclear. Pessimists are pointing to a 30%+ decline in Dubai's real estate index <https://www.hurriyetdailynews.com/dubai-real-estate-index-plunges-30-percent-amid-iran-war-219965> since the war began. Others insist the decline is more like 20%. Either way, it's a big change on 2025, when the value of real estate transactions <https://www.agbi.com/real-estate/2026/01/dubai-real-estate-market-grew-further-in-2025/>in Dubai rose by 20% on the expectation of continuously rising prices. Knight Frank <https://www.knightfrank.ae/site-assets/research/gated-reports/2025/destination-dubai-2025.pdf> says Dubai was the most popular emirate for property purchases by high net worth individuals last year. Many buyers were international and based in India, the UK, Saudi Arabia or Singapore.
One senior trader who relocated to Dubai says colleagues relocating to the emirate got greedy. "People who are new to Dubai saw this as a free money wheel," he says. "Prices went sky high after COVID and people bought into this with leverage. There were people running in and buying whole floors. If they can't make their first tranche of repayments they will be in trouble. At the least they will now lose their deposits."
Dubai is no stranger to property crashes. Property prices there rose by 70% between 2021 and 2025, <https://www.ft.com/content/84ea61c4-4c7c-48bc-bec0-d966a80a880e> but they fell by 50% in the two years after 2008. Since then, Dubai rulers have since passed regulations to ensure projects are better funded: "Building costs are now covered, so at least these properties will be finished and it won't be like 2008 again," says the trader.
Some finance professionals in Dubai are still interested in buying there and are hopeful of finding a bargain. "I haven't bought yet, but I was looking," says one hedge fund manager. "I'm interested in Emirate Hills, Palm Jumeirah or Al Barari. Prices are more stable for premium property. If I can get a 10%-15% discount, I'll be happy."
However, even a 10-15% discount may be insufficient if the war continues or an unstable ceasefire is reached. "The real question is whether this kills the dream of a tax-free, clean, safe paradise that offers everything in the world for a price," says the senior trader. "People are suddenly questioning whether it can ever be safe here, just 40 miles from Iran."
For the moment, many of the bombs have been intercepted. But the senior trader says much depends upon how long the war goes on for. "There are a lot of diverse expats here, from Africa, Asia and the Arab Spring Middle East, so people have a thicker skin than elsewhere, but if this goes on, the wealthy will move because of the inconvenience."
If that happens, the 30%+ losses on off-plan properties will be crystallised. If not, traders and hedge fund managers who've bought multiple highly levered properties might live to tell the tale. "I don't think Dubai property is over with," reflects the trader, ever hopeful.

item.title: The questions most candidates neglect in quant trading job interviews
item.description:
If you're looking for a job as a researcher </news/quant-jobs-finance> or engineer </news/software-engineer-citadel-securities> in quant trading, </news/hft-pay-london> you've likely practiced countless probability questions </news/quant-interview-questions-finance> and leetcode </news/how-much-leetcode-should-you-do-to-work-at-google-or-goldman-sachs>tasks. If you're not giving as much attention and care to second stage of your interviews, you may be setting yourself up for failure.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Peter Wagner, managing director of recruitment firm Affinity North, said in his Q1 market report that "once you pass the technical rounds, you’re not home free." In recent years, the non-technical, behavioural questions have "grown in importance" because "firms are more selective in this buyer’s market... you don’t want to make up your answers to behavioral questions on the spot."
We've seen at least 30 unique questions that hedge funds </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>and electronic trading firms </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> ask, based on submissions to Glassdoor in 2026. How do you prepare for such a wide array? Wagner said that you should "create a story bank of 6-8 experiences you can discuss and practice how to use those stories to demonstrate the desired characteristics." You'll need to practice these stories across multiple different questions to see how they fit, then test alternatives.
You'll also need to be aware of the culture of the firm you're interviewing with to know which questions to prepare for and which answers to strengthen. Hudson River Trading, for example, is thought to have a highly academic culture and is particularly interested in your choice of academic modules </news/hudson-river-trading-intern-pay#:~:text=%22high%20appreciation%20for%20students%20with%20a%20lot%20of%20ambition%3B%22%20ways%20of%20demonstrating%20that%20are%20%22taking%20all%20of%20the%20hardest%20courses%20on%20campus%22%20or%20%22div%5Bing%5D%20deep%20into%20personal%20projects.%22%C2%A0>, so incorporating that into your story when applying there might benefit you more than a traditional hedge fund.
Common Behavioural Questions in Hedge Funds and Trading Firms in 2026
Why do you want to work at [Fund/Firm]?
Why are you interested in [Fund/Firm]?
How did you first learn about [Fund/Firm]
Why are you interested in this role?
Tell me about yourself.
Tell me about your previous work experience
Tell me about a personal project you are proud of.
What is your greatest weakness?
Specific Behavioural Questions at Hedge Funds in 2026
Describe a challenging technical problem that you solved [Citadel]
Why do you think some companies fail to deploy AI solutions? [Point72]
Tell me about a time you made a decision with incomplete information. [Point72]
Describe how you resolved a conflict with your manager [Two Sigma]
How do you manage stress and tight deadlines? [Two Sigma]
What areas of technology interest you the most? [Qube Research]
What would you do if you forgot your lecture notes before teaching a class? [D.E. Shaw]
Specific Behavioural Questions at Electronic Trading Firms in 2026
Discuss your experience of building relationships, and overcoming challenges. [Jane Street]
What do you do for fun? [Jane Street]
Why does trading interest you? [Jane Street]
What is the coolest topic you learned in class? [Hudson River Trading]
Tell me about a time you contributed to a process [Citadel Securities]
Why did you choose to use [rare technology] in your previous internship? [Citadel Securities]
How do you prioritize your workday? [Optiver]
Tell me about a time you faced change. How did you confront it? [Optiver]
Tell me about a time you sought feedback for something. How did you use it?
How do you select an AI agent [Optiver]
Tell me an example of how you use data to make decisions in your everyday life. [Optiver]
How would you resolve a technical issue caused by a co-worker? [SIG]
Do you play games? If so, what kind? [SIG]
How do you keep up to date with markets? [SIG]

item.title: Brevan Howard's technology hiring spree came to BlueCrest
item.description:
Hedge fund </news/finance/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>Brevan Howard has lost a lot of its senior technology professionals </news/chris-willis-brevan-howard>. But it is also hiring and has now recruited a top front office technologist from BlueCrest.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Ben Souter, BlueCrest's former head of front office technology and quant research is understood to be joining Brevan Howard in an unspecified role that is thought to be similar to his previous job.
Brevan Howard declined to comment and Souter didn't respond to a request to comment on his arrival.
Souter spent over thirteen years at BlueCrest after arriving from JPMorgan, where he was head of interest rate derivative pricing and risk technology.
Brevan Howard has lost eight senior technologists, </news/hedge-fund-brevan-howard-parted-with-company-with-7-senior-technologists>including, most recently Chris Willis </news/chris-willis-brevan-howard>, its head of quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> analysis.
Many of the departures follow the arrival of Mike Sanders </news/brevan-howard-the-latest-hedge-fund-to-sign-a-cto-from-banking-this-time-from-jp-morgan> from JPMorgan as head of technology in 2024. Sanders has been hiring beyond Souter. Valdo Durrleman </news/valdo-durrleman-brevan-howard>, the former head of desk strats at Eisler joined in January. Tim Mace from Man Group joined as head of AI last November.
There are unconfirmed claims that Brevan Howard is paying handsomely for its senior hires. The fund is also saving money by building a technology hub in India </news/hedge-fund-india-hiring-brevan-howard> and making small cuts to its technology team in Abu Dhabi.

item.title: Hong Kong MD in Goldman Sachs' prized division leaves for venture capital
item.description:
Goldman Sachs' asset and wealth management </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-asset-management> division was the star of the show last year. CEO David Solomon said in the bank's Q4 earnings called it "extraordinary" and that the bank wants "more share and footprint," including in Asia. That might not be easy without one of its long serving wealth MDs in Hong Kong. </news/best-university-for-banking-jobs-hong-kong-singapore>
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Goldman's wealth division just lost Xinyi Feng, who announced via LinkedIn that he's joining the special situations group of Bain Capital (the venture capital </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-venture-capital>sister firm of consultancy Bain & Co) as an MD after 15 years at Goldman. He worked in the bank's growth equity and private equity team, focused on industrial and enterprise technology investments in Greater China.
Like many people in Goldman's asset management division, Feng started out as an investment banker </news/goldman-sachs-wealth-management-jobs#:~:text=Kristin%20Olsen%2C%20head,the%20same%20position.>. He spent a few years as an analyst at Morgan Stanley before joining Goldman Sachs, where he made MD in 2019. Unlike many people in the division, Feng is a semi-competitive ultramarathon runner <https://utmb.world/runner/412380.xinyi.feng> who has competed in five races in the last two years.
Bain Capital, meanwhile, has been growing its ranks in 2026. Earlier this month it acquired the wealth management arm of Australian investment firm Perpetual <https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/perpetual-sell-wealth-management-arm-bain-capital-350-million-upfront-2026-03-15/> for $350m up-front. The division has previously said that it hires just 25% of its people from investment banking </news/jobs-bain-capital> and the rest from consulting, making Feng an outlier.

item.title: Escaped Nomura rates trader joins hedge fund amidst bonus rumblings
item.description:
As we have observed here often </news/now-nomura-s-global-head-of-rates-and-fx-trading-resigned>, Nomura's rates business has lost several people in the past year. One has now turned up at a London hedge fund as those remaining at the Japanese bank say they fear for their bonuses.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
William Johnson was part of a raft of rates traders who left Nomura in 2025. Johnson, who spent 16 years at the Japanese bank has now turned up at hedge fund Alphadyne Asset Management in London.
Nomura's traders can be popular with hedge funds </news/2022/05/nomura-fx-jobs>because they are often more used to taking proprietary risk than traders at other banks who are steeped in market making. Nonetheless, Kilian Frensch, Nomura's former head of European rates swap trading has moved to UBS </news/ubs-s-shrunken-rates-team-is-going-for-a-paris-refresh>, where he's thought to be rebuilding the team.
Nomura may need some new rates traders this year and there are suggestions that senior figures from ANZ </news/nomura-is-thought-to-be-hiring-anz-s-senior-escapees-in-singapore>may appear there soon. However, there are also fears that this year's bonuses will be adversely impacted by war in the Middle East.
Nomura's year end is not until the end of this month and bonuses at the Japanese bank are not usually announced until May. This gives Nomura plenty of time to adjust payments downwards to take account of losses or uncertainty due to the war.

item.title: "I am a French banker. I too cannot stand the culture of banks in Paris"
item.description:
I am a French banker by nationality. But I do not work in Paris. I cannot. It is far too snobby and insular there.
I grew up in France, but not in Paris. My childhood was in the north - "the Liverpool of France." I have never been accepted by banks in Paris and yet I have had a successful career in London.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
When I was a student, I applied for jobs with the biggest banks in Paris but I wasn't even invited to interview. Instead, it was the branch of a bank in London that accepted me.
I know precisely why I was rejected. I did not go to the right school. In Paris, banking jobs are dominated by graduates of the Grandes Écoles </news/2023/02/best-grande-ecole-finance> and their top preparatory schools. If you don't have these on your CV, </news/the-absolutely-perfect-investment-banking-cv> you are no one. If you look at the French teams at the largest banks, you will see that they are comprised of these people.
It is all about "fit." I've had interviews </news/70-top-banking-interview-questions-and-how-to-answer-them> in Paris as an experienced banker, too, but have been told that my interviewers just didn't "feel it" when they met me. I know that I'm not the only French banker who's had this experience - I regularly meet other Paris escapees in London who have been shunned.
If you haven't been to the top schools but are still highly competent, it's therefore easier to get a job outside France. The Paris banking cliques are very closed. They have their own snobbish culture and they will do anything to protect it. It's like this a bit everywhere, but Paris is extreme.
Gabriel Gaumont is a pseudonym

item.title: Morning Coffee: 23-year-old 'banker' with six vests was secretly not working. 20 hour days in trading
item.description:
This month's photoshoot </news/goldman-sachs-junior-bankers-magazine-shoot> titled 'Meet the Finest Boys in Finance' <https://www.interviewmagazine.com/fashion/meet-the-finest-boys-in-finance>has prompted a cascade of warnings </news/finest-boys-in-finance-mason-clarke-clay-nelson-tommy-doherty> to young people in finance who think that appearing in a magazine wearing a Rolex and boasting about the number of vests your own is a good thing. Employers do not take kindly to such transgressions. However, it turns out that one of Boys was not employed in finance when the magazine was published anyway.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/demarre-johnson-finest-boys-finance-pwc-interview-magazine-2026-3?utm_source=Iterable&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=News%20Alert%20Bucket%201A%20-%20demarre-johnson-finest-boys-finance-pwc-interview-magazine-2026-3&insiderId=d8198f66-3051-484f-bdc4-3c5b70be622d>reports that 23-year-old Demarre Johnson, who told Interview Magazine that he owned six vests and a painting costing $1.4k, while working 55 hour weeks as a data, tech and AI analyst for PWC, is no longer employed by the Big Four </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-the-big-four>firm. Nor is he responding to messages. Johnson may simply be between jobs, but his disappearance casts aspersions on his new fame as a finance pin up.
It's tempting to think that Johnson was ditched by PWC after appearing in the photoshoot and then conducting follow-up interviews <https://www.businessinsider.com/wall-street-four-bankers-pwc-goldman-barclays-interview-magazine-viral-2026-3>, but this isn't so. PWC says he left in February, before his vests went public. In this context, Johnson's new fame looks like an exercise in pushing his Instagram <https://www.instagram.com/marre.1/> and TikTok <https://www.tiktok.com/@moneymarre_/video/7459874900560350510> channels and is a reminder that there are other ways to make money than data analysis right now.
Johnson isn't the only finance influencer with an occluded reality. As we reported last month, </news/juliet-mackay-notbadjuju> a Bank of America junior who runs a TikTok account “notbadjuju” <https://www.tiktok.com/@notbadjuju?lang=en> seems to work in an investment banking job, but was shifted into the middle office when Bank of America cut jobs last year.
Separately, one of the revelations of the Finest Boys in Finance was that working hours </news/working-hours-banks>in sales and trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> are usually gentle compared to those in investment banking. However, this is not so if you work in oil trading at this juncture.
Instead of the gentle 50-55 hour weeks of junior FX traders, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-13/-lose-1-million-in-2-seconds-market-chaos-rocks-trading-desks?srnd=phx-markets&sref=DK3y4h9m> reports that some oil traders have begun waking up at 6am and starting at their screens until 2am. The situation is particularly acute in Singapore as a result of Asian reliance on oil and gas imports from the Middle East. US traders are feeling it too. One observes that millions of dollars can be lost in seconds. He advises his traders to drink less so that they don't have to visit the bathroom: “You never take your eyes off the screen.”
Meanwhile...
Investment bankers are expected to look successful to their clients but otherwise project a studied modesty. Overt displays of wealth by juniors are discouraged. Juniors with designer briefcases or monogrammed shirts are mocked. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/150984eb-b67f-4c06-9afe-71fb94100833>
Hedge funds are using exotic hybrid options to trade cross-market gyrations. UBS strategists favor equity down / rates up hybrids and still tout the traditional stagflation payoff of equity down / gold up. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-15/hedge-funds-eye-exotic-options-to-play-huge-cross-asset-swings?srnd=homepage-uk>
BTIG wants to hire 100 investment bankers in New York and Miami beach. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-13/btig-plots-investment-banking-hiring-spree-under-new-owner?sref=DK3y4h9m>
Open AI and Anthropic want hedge fund talent. “AI firms are hiring top quants and data scientists to learn domain knowledge. These AI models have hit a limit on recursive feedback; learning from pre-training and training. So now they have to learn the domain knowledge to continue to evolve better.” (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/why-openai-and-anthropic-are-going-after-hedge-fund-talent-3082544e>
Suddenly, building lots of big glass buildings in the Gulf seems a bad idea. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-13/iran-war-trauma-of-middle-east-conflict-will-reshape-gulf-arab-states?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter?sref=hOduCZII>
Shanghai was once a leading financial centre but an exodus of wealthy Shanghainese after 1949 helped transfer that status to Hong Kong. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-13/iran-war-will-dubai-s-expat-hub-face-a-hong-kong-style-exodus?taid=69b6913e15e5590001e266a7&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Mamdani wants to slash New York state’s estate tax exemption threshold by almost 90%, from a more than $7 million limit to $750,000, and raise the top estate tax rate from 16% to 50%. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-13/mamdani-wants-new-york-estate-tax-threshold-cut-90-to-750-000?accessToken=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzb3VyY2UiOiJTdWJzY3JpYmVyR2lmdGVkQXJ0aWNsZSIsImlhdCI6MTc3MzQyMTc0MiwiZXhwIjoxNzc0MDI2NTQyLCJhcnRpY2xlSWQiOiJUQlVFTFhUOU5KTFMwMCIsImJjb25uZWN0SWQiOiJZTk9LTlhKMU4xQkVXVVJVNVZJWkJVNEdaSVU0WVpNVSJ9.yI1JE8YeB35nHgC399PEyr7BscjOaubw4rYjHekX-14>
33 year old Tomi Mikula makes $200k in revenue a month helping negotiate car purchases. He also works from home. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/business/autos/tomi-mikula-youtube-car-buying-negotiations-4a4c3d63?mod=hp_lead_pos9>
Leah Wood, 31, a model and designer and the daughter of Rolling Stone Ronnie Wood is married to Jack MacDonald, a banker specialising in entertainment deals who worked for Credit Suisse. She says Ronnie Wood has not given them much money. "My husband and I have had to work our arses off. Having kids and stuff, our mortgage to pay and, you know, trying to find X amount of money to fix the old stone wall." (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/life-style/celebrity/article/ronnie-woods-daughter-im-glad-dad-didnt-give-me-millions-mgk29f620>

item.title: "Banks hate mercenary behaviour. Good luck if you've been demanding higher pay"
item.description:
I've spent over two decades as a trader at major banks and have been a managing director for 10 years. I feel in my bones that we are due another clear-out. And when it comes, it will not necessarily be the loyalists like me that go.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
What I have learned through observation of repeated market cycles is that when cuts happen, it's the mercenaries who are targeted first.
These are the people who jump from role to role when the going is good, always trying to bid-up, always trying to be bid-back, always trying to get more and more for their own franchise.
This behaviour is remembered.
When times get tough, your behaviour will remembered and will backfire on you. Your mercenary behaviour will be repaid in kind.
Banks are staffed by humans. If you want to get ahead, you need to be capable but you also need to be loyal. You need to build credibility and stay in a place through good and bad times and then you will be trusted with bigger roles.
You can't force your progress. If you have, I fear that you are about to find this out in the bad times coming soon.

item.title: The Coffee Run: ‘The Finest Boys in Finance’ exposed the disconnect between Gen Z and Wall Street
item.description:
If you work in banking, it's highly likely that you've been shown (or shown someone) The Finest Boys in Finance. Clay Nelson, Mason Clarke and Tommy Doherty have immortalized themselves among their peers... albeit not the way they may have hoped.
It's not the first time that banks have clashed with their juniors, especially in a social media driven world where posting about working in a bank can get you thousands if not millions of views.
What did these boys to do rile everyone up, and why has their photoshoot become so infamous? Find out by watching the video below.

item.title: Hedge fund Qube Research's HRT hire was also pursued by DE Shaw
item.description:
As we reported earlier this week </news/hedge-fund-qube-research-hired-hudson-river-trading-s-head-of-options-market-making>, Qube Research and Technologies, </news/qrt-hedge-fund> the quantitative hedge fund with an interesting pay structure, </news/qube-qrt-pay>has strengthened its options business by hiring Beat Ammon from Hudson River Trading (HRT).
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Ammon appears to have been quite a catch: we understand that other places were pursuing him too. They are thought to have included the likes of DE Shaw.
Neither Ammon nor DE Shaw responded to a request to comment for this article. Ammon left HRT in 2024 and went on gardening leave. It's thought he was due to join DE Shaw this year, but opted for Qube instead. IMC may also have been in the hiring mix.
Ammon spent four years at HRT and was previously at Morgan Stanley (10 years) and Citadel (seven years). At HRT he was the head of options market making. At Qube he will be the head of research on options market making. Qube declined to comment.
Qube already has an options business, although it's thought to be relatively small. HRT is expanding its options market making activities after hiring Harsh Padia, the chief executive of HAP Trading late last year. Competition in the space is heating up: Citadel Securities <https://www.ft.com/content/abdff835-a072-4ee2-904a-1f8d1e9075ea>bought Morgan Stanley's options market making business last July.
In the circumstances, Ammon presumably thought QRT was his best bet as an employer. The fund doesn't divulge performance information but is thought to have done well so far this year. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-11/de-shaw-s-hedge-funds-gain-in-a-brutal-week-for-multistrat-peers> reported that DE Shaw was one of the only funds to make money last week.

item.title: Morgan Stanley's recent job cuts included various London credit traders
item.description:
Morgan Stanley is cutting jobs </news/morgan-stanley-goldman-sachs-job-cuts-2026>, again. The recent cuts reportedly involved "shifting business locations," but it seems they also involved cutting some European credit traders.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
We understand that various people have vanished from the US bank's credit desk in the past week. They include: Klaus Plattner, a senior CDS trader who joined from BNP Paribas in 2023; James Nowak, a senior corporate bond trader who joined from Citi the same year (and who may have resigned rather than being pushed); Aldo Olcese Pi, a credit salesman based in Madrid; and - it's thought - Florent Morizot on Pattner's team.
The CDS team, which was only small to begin with, is thought to be heavily depleted as a result.
Morgan Stanley declined to comment. A senior trader at another US bank said various members of the team have approached him in search of new jobs.
Morgan Stanley's global credit team is led by Rehan Latif, who been stamping his own authority on it. There were various departures </news/morgan-stanley-credit-traders-leaving> last year, including that of Herbie Filho </news/morgan-stanley-s-european-head-of-credit-trading-left-jain-global>, who left for Jain Global </news/as-jain-global-p-ms-take-headhunter-calls-bobby-jain-explains-why-they-need-patience>, where under appreciated 35 year-olds are popular. Filho is understood to have left Jain recently for health reasons.
Credit traders have left various banks in recent years as desks have been hit by electronification. </news/2020/12/electronification-of-credit-trading-jobs> "It seems Morgan Stanley is focusing on the algo business now," the rival trader suggested.

item.title: UBS's shrunken rates team is going for a Paris refresh
item.description:
UBS's London rates team has had a few issues, but now it's got a new man in charge and is freshening itself up. It wants some new people. In Paris.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The Swiss bank is advertising for two new junior rates traders, based in the Paris office. Sources at the bank say the roles are the initiative of Kilian Frensch, </news/kilian-frensch>the ex-head of European interest rate swap trading who arrived earlier this year.
Frensch's business at UBS is looking a bit diminished. Before he arrived, the bank lost nine people </news/kilian-frensch>from its London rates business, including senior traders like Mark Samarasinghe and George Dramitinos. There are thought to be very few revenue generators left as a result.
UBS isn't commenting but the Paris jobs are not understood to represent a shift out of London, where UBS employs Frensch, his line manager Jonas Klink </news/jonas-klink>and their ex-Credit Suisse boss, Shane O'Cuinn. If anything, they represent a consolidation of the European rates business in Paris instead of Frankfurt. The bank has historically had three European government bond traders and one swaps trader in the German city.
Sources at the bank say the Paris hiring hasn't gone well so far. Frensch is thought to have made an offer to one junior who was bought back by the US bank she currently works for.
There are also rumours that UBS's rates business may have lost money </news/turbulence-hits-banks-trading-desks-weeks-after-bonuses-were-paid> during the chaos created by the Middle Eastern war. This follows some allegedly generous bonuses to retain key remaining staff after the exodus last year.

item.title: The 25 universities Jane Street loves most for its quants & engineers
item.description:
What does it take to join Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street>, the electronic trading </news/hft-pay-london> and market making </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay#:~:text=Electronic%20Market%20Making,hedge%20their%20risk.> firm that paid its staff over $2m per head </news/jane-street-versus-citadel-securities-pay> last year? For its coveted trading and engineering roles, it overwhelmingly hires at graduate level; for its latest intern program, it hired from over 103 different colleges, but that doesn't stop it from having some favourites.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
According to LinkedIn profiles for current Jane Street staff, the most frequently recurring university among employees is MIT, by a significant margin; more than 100 of its current employees studied there. Its second and third favourite universities are in England: Cambridge and Oxford have at least 89 and 88 alumni working at the firm, respectively.
Below is a table containing the 13 most popular North American universities for Jane Street employees, the seven most popular in Europe, and the five most popular in APAC.
Not only is MIT the most popular for Jane Street employees in general, it's also the most popular for its coveted trading roles. It's not so closely followed by Harvard and Oxford. For its engineers, the top universities are a little more intriguing. Carnegie Mellon is the current top university for engineers, but Cambridge and the University of Waterloo are also very popular for these roles.
If you're more interested in an internship than a full-time role, examining ex-employees (the majority of which only interned there) can be more useful. MIT, of course, tops the list, but Harvard seems to produce more interns than any of the remaining schools. Waterloo again punches above its weight here; this is helped by the fact that it has a co-op program </news/best-school-for-quant-developer-jobs> in which students are mandated to complete six internships.
Not included in the list are Indian IITs </news/the-elite-graduates-earning-insane-salaries-in-electronic-trading>, which are known to be a huge source of talent for electronic trading firms in APAC. It seems most IIT graduates joining Jane Street tend to keep their profiles under wraps, but the firm has reportedly hired students from these schools on packages of up to $500k in their first year alone. </news/jane-street-student-salaries> For other schools in APAC, Hong Kong universities </news/best-university-for-banking-jobs-hong-kong-singapore> seem to be the most popular, but the National University of Singapore is a good alternative.
Jane Street has said in the past that it's not too hung up on its applicants studying a specific degree, but it's a safe bet that you'll need excellent STEM fundamentals. When we analyzed its 2025 internship cohort, </news/this-is-what-it-takes-to-get-a-64k-jane-street-internship> a third studied mathematics in some capacity and two thirds studied computer science (many studied both). This is likely because Jane Street staff say the modules and projects you choose are the most important part of your degree, </news/jane-street-employees-reveal-what-degrees-matter-when-applying-for-quant-jobs> and those subjects tend to have the most intellectually stimulating options. At MIT, for example, working in the AI lab CSAIL </news/jane-street-s-top-interns-in-2025-have-this-college-ai-lab-in-common>will help you to stand out.
Your specific school may not matter that much since Jane Street puts out feelers for top candidates well before then. Hilal Hussein, an MIT student who interned at Jane Street, said in a YouTube video <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uNTHLG3CPxk> last year that Jane Street and other quant firms were attending his high school mathematics tournaments.
Jane Street declined to comment.

Waterloo again punches above its weight here; this is helped by the fact that it has a co-op program </news/best-school-for-quant-developer-jobs> in which students are mandated to complete six internships.
Not included in the list are Indian IITs </news/the-elite-graduates-earning-insane-salaries-in-electronic-trading>, which are known to be a huge source of talent for electronic trading firms in APAC. It seems most IIT graduates joining Jane Street tend to keep their profiles under wraps, but the firm has reportedly hired students from these schools on packages of up to $500k in their first year alone. </news/jane-street-student-salaries> For other schools in APAC, Hong Kong universities </news/best-university-for-banking-jobs-hong-kong-singapore> seem to be the most popular, but the National University of Singapore is a good alternative.
Jane Street has said in the past that it's not too hung up on its applicants studying a specific degree, but it's a safe bet that you'll need excellent STEM fundamentals. When we analyzed its 2025 internship cohort, </news/this-is-what-it-takes-to-get-a-64k-jane-street-internship> a third studied mathematics in some capacity and two thirds studied computer science (many studied both). This is likely because Jane Street staff say the modules and projects you choose are the most important part of your degree, </news/jane-street-employees-reveal-what-degrees-matter-when-applying-for-quant-jobs> and those subjects tend to have the most intellectually stimulating options. At MIT, for example, working in the AI lab CSAIL </news/jane-street-s-top-interns-in-2025-have-this-college-ai-lab-in-common>will help you to stand out.
Your specific school may not matter that much since Jane Street puts out feelers for top candidates well before then. Hilal Hussein, an MIT student who interned at Jane Street, said in a YouTube video <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uNTHLG3CPxk> last year that Jane Street and other quant firms were attending his high school mathematics tournaments.
Jane Street declined to comment.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Hedge fund manager who massaged female employees may have miscalculated. Private equity firms upgrading own staff
item.description:
When he was running hedge fund Odey Asset Management, Robin Crispin William Odey had a well-earned reputation as an aristocratic maverick. "I have been my own man,” he informed Institutional Investor <https://www.institutionalinvestor.com/article/2bswuoksckdtyan7wda0w/culture/inside-the-mind-of-crispin-odey> five years ago, while noting that he is a historian and predicting that history will come down on his side.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
History has has not since been kind to Crispin Odey. In 2023, the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/e5d14398-e866-44b3-8ecb-4e6371167c6d> wrote a bombshell article claiming that Odey - previously described benignly by the paper as “a large puppy in a pinstripe suit,” had sexually assaulted 13 women who worked for him. Odey, who previously defended himself in British courts against an assault claim in 2021 and won, said the allegations were "rubbish." He is now pursuing the FT for £79m in damages <https://www.matrixlaw.co.uk/news/high-court-orders-joint-trials-of-cases-involving-crispin-odey/> in a trial that's due to take place in June and July this year.
Odey Asset Management closed in 2023 and was rendered dormant in January 2026, so Crispin Odey has plenty of time on his hands after selecting which pink shirt to wear. He's using it to reclaim his legacy and rewrite recent history. Alongside the FT libel case, Odey is pursuing the British Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) for holding a "hostile animus" against him and for unfairly banning him from working in financial services.
Odey's three week trial against the FCA began this week. Unfortunately, the script so far may not be entirely as Odey had envisaged. As the FT <https://www.ft.com/content/a38056ff-12eb-4e65-810e-e20594f6c88d> reported yesterday, the court has ruled that the sexual misconduct allegations against Crispin Odey can be aired in the FCA case because they're relevant to his fitness and propriety to work in financial services. Those same allegations will be aired again in his case against the FT in a few months.
Odey's public persona is therefore once again shrouded in comments like, "I think he is a sex pest I’m afraid," even as his lawyers attempt to direct attention to the FCA's alleged “pressure, prejudgment and prevarication” in dealing with their client.
Much like ex-Barclays CEO Jes Staley, </news/jes-staley-epstein> who attempted to take on the FCA and clear his name last year, but who failed (only for far more shocking allegations <https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/feb/05/jes-staley-barclays-rape-allegation-us-prosecutors-epstein>to subsequently emerge </news/jes-staley-bill-winters-jpmorgan> against him), Odey may yet decide that history would have been kinder had he remained on his country estate. At the very least, Odey appears to accept that he once gave a receptionist at Odey Asset Management a shoulder massage that somehow evolved into breast groping. A report from law firm Simmons & Simmons, prepared for the regulator, describes this incident, which Odey reportedly admitted to, <https://www.ft.com/content/a38056ff-12eb-4e65-810e-e20594f6c88d>but blamed on a dental sedative. The FCA's lawyer told the court <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/odey-challenges-uk-lifetime-ban-in-bid-to-salvage-reputation>that Odey admitted, “he did give quite a lot of shoulder massages,”
Unfortunately, Odey may not have had good role models or good advice. He previously described his father as “a wastrel from beginning to end” and his grandfather as a "formidable bully." Odey's wife and mother of his three children - fund manager Nichola Pease - has recently divorced him. It's easy to see why attempting to clear his name might seem appealing, but it will be a tough battle and a difficult year. However, his court cases turn out, public perception may remain that the formidable puppy has elements of the dirty old dog.
Separately, as private equity firms struggle to exit investments, some funds are upgrading their staff.
The FT <https://www.ft.com/content/247da046-afbe-4c3c-9ef3-02073cc61dd2> notes that Carlyle evicted both Marco De Benedetti and Jonathan Zafrani as co-heads of its European private equity fund and replaced them with the aptly named "Michael Wand" who is a TMT specialist.
The evictions happened in 2024, so Wand has had plenty of time to come up with new ideas. He wants to invest in "technology, healthcare, professional services and advanced industrials," and not consumer investments, which may not be the best thing in war time. Wand also wants to work more closely with portfolio companies and operations teams now that leverage is less potent than it used to be.
It's not clear what's happened to De Benedetti and Zafrani's favoured juniors, but it's a sign that private equity careers are less stable than before. If you don't have the magic, a new wand will waft you away.
Meanwhile...
Jon Thompson, a tech portfolio manager at Point72, generated hundreds of millions of dollars in investment gains in the first two months of the year. He did so by investing in hardware companies behind AI and shorting software stocks. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/the-ai-trade-thats-separating-wall-streets-winners-and-losers-6495cc5b?mod=panda_wsj_author_alert>
Nomura wants to hire for its FX and emerging markets trading teams in Asia. (Reuters) <https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/nomura-hires-fx-traders-asia-bet-high-volatility-drive-demand-2026-03-12/>
When private credit firms are in trouble, they can sell collateralized loan obligations, or CLOs, backed by bundles of corporate loans. The higher-yielding CLO bonds that private-credit funds primarily hold lost 4.1% in February, after gains of 1% in January and December. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/an-exodus-of-money-endangers-wall-streets-private-credit-craze-d0fbb8af>
Apollo Global is preparing to start reporting the net asset values of its credit funds on a monthly basis. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-11/apollo-plans-to-value-private-credit-daily-in-answer-to-critics>
Morgan Stanley and private credit lender Cliffwater have restricted withdrawals from private credit funds. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/22454850-a7da-4089-af17-a223fa11f31f>
Deutsche Bank says US commercial real estate remains a “key risk." (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-12/deutsche-bank-says-commercial-real-estate-remains-key-risk?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260312&utm_campaign=author_23851719>
HSBC has $23bn of exposure to the UAE and Qatar. (Reuters) <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/hsbc-stanchart-most-exposed-mideast-conflict-among-european-banks-jp-morgan-2026-03-12/>
Ex-investment banking analyst Tony Chin founded hedge fund Infini Capital in Hong Kong. Now he's been raided by authorities. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-13/hedge-fund-in-hong-kong-raid-took-big-bets-failed-goldman-test?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260313&utm_campaign=author_3492388>
The new dress code in the City of London. White trainers, navy trousers, a knit, sometimes a gilet thrown over a shirt, as if dressed for a weekend that never quite arrives. It looks casual but it isn’t relaxed. (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/life-style/fashion/article/city-boys-style-suits-work-qwj7cvhwj>
This is the deck being used by the US government to recruit bankers to its economic defense unit. (X) <https://x.com/HighyieldHarry/status/2032234552706605551>
