item.title: Very few equity researchers in banks ever exit to hedge funds or private equity firms
item.description:
In principle, equity researchers </news/research-job-investment-bank> are a pretty good fit for hedge funds </news/best-place-to-work-hedge-fund>, since the role has a lot of similarities to investment analysts. They could also bring their analytical skills to private markets with private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> firms. Despite this, less than 1% of equity researchers in banks ever make the switch in a given year.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
A study* <https://www.bwl.uni-mannheim.de/media/Lehrstuehle/bwl/Area_Finance/Finance_Area_Seminar/FSS_2026/Christoph_Paper.pdf> by the Chinese University of Hong Kong </news/best-university-for-banking-jobs-hong-kong-singapore> and other universities published last month looked at the impact equity researchers have on markets when they leave for the buy side. It analyzed 34,000 datapoints of equity research analyst job movements (or lack thereof) over a yearly period between 2007 and 2016.
The paper found that, on average, ~1.5% of equity researchers join the buy side in an average year, while 0.76% join hedge funds, PE firms or venture capital firms specifically.
This is partly because most equity researchers never left their jobs. 80% of the time, analysts remained at their firm from one year to the next. In days of yore, equity research roles could be some of the most stable careers </news/i-spent-30-years-in-equity-research-the-job-only-got-harder> in finance and elite-level researchers like Mike Mayo </news/2021/09/100000-banking-layoffs> at Wells Fargo or Mary Meeker </news/2003/01/how-much-am-i-worth-european-equity-analyst-international-city-based-firm#:~:text=This%20all%20changed,forever.%20And%20today%3F> at Morgan Stanley became somewhat synonymous with their banks. Meeker is a famous example of a researcher that eventually left banking, however.
When looking at individual instances of equity researchers leaving the sell side, hedge funds and PE firms make up a more notable 6%. It's not hard to understand why they would make the switch; the paper said these analysts get "more direct involvement in investment decisions, greater long-term compensation, and better work-life balance" on the buy side.
2016 was, of course, a long time ago now and the industry has become much harder to survive in. Over the last few years, the likes of HSBC, UBS and Stifel </news/it-s-ok-london-s-laid-off-equity-researchers-are-finding-new-jobs> have all made layoffs to their research divisions. The decline in popularity of IPOs has only hurt them, but the worst may be yet to come. In a recent paper, AI lab Anthropic said that investment analysts are the seventh most exposed role in the world to artificial intelligence. </news/ai-hedge-fund-jobs-vs-banking>
*When Public Information Goes Private: Analyst Careers and Market Efficiency

item.title: Morning Coffee: UBS bankers' controversial method of boosting bonuses. Non-banker celebrates appearance in "satirical" photoshoot
item.description:
UBS has published its annual report, <https://www.ubs.com/global/en/investor-relations/financial-information/annual-reporting.html> and with it, news of its bonuses </news/banker-bonuses>for the last year. It says that overall 2025 bonuses at the Swiss bank were based upon the very standard considerations of "financial performance" plus things like "risk management" and "strategic objectives," while individual bonuses were decided by "individual, team, business division and group performance." There is nothing unusual here.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-09/ubs-bonus-pool-rises-10-ermotti-pay-unchanged-at-19-million>notes that the overall bonus pool at UBS was up 10% for 2025. The average bonus awarded to the 88,812 UBS employees who benefitted from this bonus pool was $48k. The average bonus awarded to UBS's 755 elite "key risk takers" was $1.41m ($300k higher than last year). And CEO Sergio Ermotti himself received $19m.
None of this is bad. Barclays' average bonuses </news/barclays-bonuses-2026> for material risk takers for last year were $1m; HSBC's were $819k </news/hsbc-is-paying-senior-bankers-who-stay-1-4m-and-senior-bankers-who-go-500k>. UBS's senior bankers and traders can feel fortunate with their $1.4m.
Next year, though things might be a little different.
The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/2a1805a1-45ce-4811-9575-1dced18a2305> reports that UBS has discovered a way to juice bonuses for its wealth managers in 2026. However, it's an approach that comes at an awkward moment.
UBS has reportedly begun offering its bankers who sell private investments to wealthy clients a share of the performance fees those funds generate. So far, the agreement applies both to Carlyle's evergreen secondaries fund and to one of CVC's evergreen funds. In this way, UBS bankers are effectively in with a chance of earning carried interest. </news/what-is-carried-interest>
While this sounds like a fine thing for the UBS bankers who get it, it may be less good for UBS' clients. Questions are being raised about the wisdom of inviting individuals to invest in illiquid private credit </news/private-credit>funds whose value is being questioned following incidents like BlackRock's 100% write down </news/goldman-sachs-junior-bankers-magazine-shoot>of a loan to an Amazon aggregator. Questions are also being raised about the impartiality of the UBS bankers who are incentivised to sell clients investments in some funds where they receive carried interest as a result, but not others.
UBS says there is nothing to see here. External funds are recommended to clients, “based on our robust investment and due diligence process,” it tells the FT. “Fee arrangements are in line with market practices and are disclosed to clients,” it adds. Eyebrows are being raised nonetheless.
The practice may become more common. As private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity>and credit funds attempt to sell their investments to retail buyers, competition for access to those retail buyers has intensified. UBS's wealth managers may not be alone in receiving performance pay as an inducement to introduce their clients to funds with investments to offload.
Separately, following last week's viral photoshoot </news/goldman-sachs-junior-bankers-magazine-shoot>involving two junior Goldman Sachs bankers, one junior Barclays banker and one PWC guy who wasn't a banker at all, but who works in data analysis and AI, the PWC guy has been talking to the Wall Street Journal and to Business Insider about how it all came to pass.
Demarre Johnson of PWC tells the WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/style/fashion/the-ultimate-fashion-crime-for-finance-bros-dressing-better-than-the-boss-885c7f81?mod=hp_listb_pos1>he turned up at a steak house for the photo shoot and was given some very fancy outfits by a stylist. This was all part of the provocation, Johnson said. “To say we’re entry-level associates but also putting us in multi-thousand-dollar watches is very satirical, so I can see how it added flames to the fire.”
Johnson also spoke to Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/wall-street-four-bankers-pwc-goldman-barclays-interview-magazine-viral-2026-3>, where he seemed quite positive about the furore following the photoshoot. Johnson has a TikTok account where he's posted about the event and notes that he's "viral on twitter." - "Controversy sells," he declares. It's not clear whether the Goldman and Barclays boyz are as enthusiastic about their new notoriety.
Separately...
Hedge funds paid their bonuses and now people are moving around. Daniel Mazur, Saaket Mehta, Steven Jozkowski and Adam Weitzman, who worked for Citadel’s equities trading business Surveyor, are moving to Millennium. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-06/millennium-hires-four-citadel-stock-traders-in-post-bonus-churn?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260306&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
Ken Griffin says: "“We would be open to the possibility of selling a minority stake in Citadel at some point in the future.” He also says a privater equity firm offered to invest $5bn in Citadel Securities and that this will enable it to stay private for longer. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-11-21/ken-griffin-says-he-s-open-to-selling-minority-stake-in-citadel-mmetvdp1?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260306&utm_campaign=author_1707910>
XTX executive Alex Gerko injected £387m of equity into Cromulon Capital, his family office. It is named after Cromulons, a species of planet-sized beings that are shaped like giant human heads in Rick & Morty. Gerko is keeping his money in the UK, despite taxes. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-06/billionaire-trader-reveals-660-million-london-family-office-bet>
Lloyd Blankfein says he hasn't moved to Miami for tax reasons. “I’m being very generous to New York State by remaining a resident even though I don’t spend all my time there.” (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/business/companies-markets/article/goldman-sachs-boss-defends-bailouts-lsdd7gh6c>
Lloyd Blankfein on what to say when you're told off for interrupting in meetings. “I never held back my opinions. If I felt condescended to, which I often did, I would use humor to slash back at the offender. In a large meeting, I might talk over someone I disagreed with or ask too many questions too quickly. I was told I should listen to the answer before interrupting. I am listening, I said. I just hear faster than you talk.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-06/in-streetwise-lloyd-blankfein-defends-goldman-sachs?taid=69aab4e0404f690001cb0ff9&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Lloyd Blankfein says the thing that AI can’t really do is take risk. (YouTube) <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sgtun8y8Xes&list=PLe4PRejZgr0OJbRzA6nWybYiThLJd_ouz&index=1>
Some top end London homes are being sold at a loss. One four-bedroom flat in South Kensington, originally bought for £5.2m a decade ago, sold at the start of last year for £2.5m. (Telegraph) <https://www.telegraph.co.uk/money/property/buying-selling/one-in-five-million-pound-homes-selling-at-a-loss/>
“Dubai has become a tax haven for middle-class expats to build their lives, and safety is a big part of the allure for them. Sure, you can walk around with a Rolex on, but now there is the worry of what may be falling from the skies.” (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/world/middle-east/the-iran-war-is-hitting-gulf-markets-lifting-israel-and-shifting-risk-across-the-region-890d272e?mod=djemHeardEUH>
Operating profit at Michael Page Group fell 60% last year. (ProactiveInvestors) <https://www.proactiveinvestors.com/companies/news/1088447/provaris-energy-raises-1-3m-for-hydrogen-co-plans-icymi-1088447.html>
Nate Colley, a 19-year-old teenager who grew up on a Nova Scotia trailer park, is making $400k a month from a game he invented on the Roblox platform. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-06/roblox-s-teen-millionaires-are-disrupting-the-gaming-industry?taid=69aab73a138a0600011c8904&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>

item.title: Citi parted company with the nice woman leading its unpopular 'internal hit squad'
item.description:
A controversial female figure has left Citi.
Citi is not commenting but sources at the bank say that Yamini Patel, Citi's head of investigations for the UK, Europe and Russia, left the bank a few months ago.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Patel ran Citi's internal CSIS unit. Standing for 'Citi Security and Investigative Services,' the CSIS unit is an internal unit tasked with tackling wrongdoing at Citi. It was the subject of an article in the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/dddf2192-5550-4a22-976b-07d81021a5ea>earlier this week, in which Citi employees claimed that members of CSIS had interrogated them about internal complaints, and then used their testimony against them when they later took the bank to court.
“Citi’s CSIS operates as HR’s internal hit squad, highly trained to protect the firm at employees’ expense,” Citi MD Ardith Lindsey told the FT. Lindsey is suing Citi for sexism, harassment and assault while she worked there. The FT spoke to four current and former Citi employees who said they were summoned to CSIS after lodging whistleblowing complaints. Three said their interviews were subsequently used against them.
There is no indication of wrongdoing on the part of Patel, who colleagues say was both well liked at Citi and very good at her job. Nor is it clear whether the people complaining to the FT were interviewed by the European CSIS team. Patel didn't respond to a request to comment for this article. There are unconfirmed suggestions that her exit occurred as the bank cuts costs. If so, CSIS may be run by more inexperienced employees at Citi in the future.
Patel spent 12 years at Citi and was hired from Barclays. Citi's CSIS team globally is run by New York-based Robert Clarke, a former member of the City of London police.
Speaking to the FT, Citi said: "Our Security and Investigative Services team consists of highly experienced professionals whose responsibilities include supporting Citi disaster response efforts, protection for colleagues around the world and looking into certain concerns about colleague behaviour along a clearly laid-out set of guidelines.”
The bank added that it takes its responsibility for looking into conduct concerns seriously. "These situations are inherently challenging for those involved and we approach them with professionalism, integrity and empathy.”

item.title: Citi banking MDs moving to the fertile plains of Deutsche Bank may be wary now
item.description:
If you're an investment banking managing director (MD) who's feeling unhappy with the new regime </news/citi-vis-raghavan> at Citi, you might feel like joining your old regime at Deutsche Bank. But the recent fate of one who made that journey four years ago should give you cause to pause.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Ed McBride spent 12 years working in consumer banking for Citi, where he led coverage of the consumer, leisure and real estate sectors in the UK, before leaving for reasons that were unclear in 2021. McBride then joined Deutsche Bank, where he reported to Daniel Ross, the former Barclays banker who became Deutsche's co-head of banking around the same time.
Ross himself left Deutsche Bank and joined Rothschild in December 2024 </news/daniel-ross-rothschild> and now Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/amp/articles/deutsche-bank-dealmaker-mcbride-departs-in-latest-uk-exit-91416eaf> reports that McBride is also out. It's not clear why and it's not clear where he's going. Neither McBride nor Deutsche Bank responded to requests to comment for this article. Multiple sources tell us McBride actually left a month ago, seemingly before bonuses were announced or paid.
Deutsche Bank's investment banking and capital markets (IBCM) division is run by Alison Harding Jones, </news/alison-harding-jones-deutsche-bank>the former head of M&A at Citi. Citi bankers looking for exits have been inclined to give her a call. After hiring 115 bankers in 2023 and 125 bankers in 2024, Deutsche Bank said <https://www.thebanker.com/content/ffef77f3-73b3-41dc-bffd-b9ec5f73bf27> in November that it wants another 60 bankers in 2026.
However, Citi MDs eyeing a new role with Harding Jones might want to move cautiously. Despite its new senior bankers, Deutsche Bank has underperformed: M&A revenues there rose by only 1% last year, compared to double digit increases at most banks and a 53% rise at Citi. This is partly down to coverage: Deutsche is strongest in sectors </news/deutsche-bank-bankers> where deals were few in 2025; it wants to ameliorate this with new recruits </news/deutsche-bank-gently-trimmed-45-people-in-q4-while-hiking-pay>"industrials, FIG, healthcare and tech" in 2026.
As Deutsche adds yet more MDs, some insiders are complaining that it already has too many senior bankers chasing too few deals. "It's too crowded on the top," says one.
The DB consumer team which shed McBride has at least five MDs in London. They include Siddharth Malik, the co-head of consumer banking who joined from BofA in 2023; Robert Plowman, who joined from Citi in 2023; Clemence Kaniak, who was promoted last year; Robert Ohnsorge, who covers the luxury sector; and Pablo Bowen, whom Deutsche hired as an MD from BofA (where he was only a director) last October. As we reported last May </news/deutsche-bank-s-london-consumer-bankers-keep-leaving-for-jp-morgan>, the top-heavy nature of the team has been encouraging overworked juniors to leave.
With this in mind, Deutsche's analysts and associates may welcome McBride's exit. Gillian Wilson, another MD on the consumer team, also left last June and has since resurfaced at PJT.
Other MDs thinking of joining Deutsche from Citi and other banks should probably take note. Deutsche needs to show a return on its hiring investments. If it's not forthcoming - even in this newly difficult market - patience may wear thin.

item.title: Tremendously well-paying Canadian pension fund loses an MD to Goldman Sachs in London
item.description:
The Canadian Pension Plan pays a pretty penny </news/canadian-pension-plan-pay>. Plenty private capital professionals, both in private equity and private credit, plan for a career there; some, however, are just as happy to leave back to banking.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Adrien Aubague joined Goldman Sachs in London as a managing director this week, working in its European real estate credit team. He joined the bank from CPP Investments, the investment arm of the Canadian Pension Plan.
Aubgaue spent 11 years with CPP Investments in total, across London and Hong Kong, and from analyst all the way up to principal. In a social media message, Aubgaue said that he started in a team that was an “extension” of the real estate equity business, and ended up being global real assets credit platform.
The Canadian pension system’s various stewards are very well paid. Leaving them might be an odd choice – and especially given that, despite the private credit industry’s mild ongoing crisis, real estate will likely be unaffected <https://www.bisnow.com/national/news/capital-markets/ai-spreads-panic-around-private-credit-but-real-estate-debt-should-barely-wince-133531>.

item.title: Top Abu Dhabi quant declares he feels "fortunate" to be there now
item.description:
For all the upheaval in the Middle East, one person there has no worries. Marcos Lopez de Prado, the head of quantitative R&D at the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority (ADIA) </news/adia-pay>, says he feels "fortunate" to be there now and that the UAE's leadership is thoughtful and well-prepared.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
De Prado is the global head of quantitative R&D in ADIA's "Team Q" and a former head of quantitative research at Tudor. He moved to Abu Dhabi in 2022 as a founding member of the ADIA Lab and has helped build the team from 50 to 150 people.
Writing on LinkedIn, De Prado said his thankfulness comes from seeing how "calmly and effectively things continue to function here," as "daily life goes on with only minimal disruption," despite "a very uncertain regional environment." This is, "clearly the result of years of careful preparation and thoughtful leadership," he enthused.
One person died and seven were injured in a strike on Abu Dhabi's Zayed airport <https://news.sky.com/story/one-dead-and-others-injured-at-abu-dhabi-and-dubai-airports-as-missile-threat-grounds-flights-13513380>on Sunday. Yesterday, six people were reportedly hit by debris from falling drones that had been shot down. There are reports today that British people in Abu Dhabi have been issued with a warning to take shelter. <https://www.express.co.uk/news/world/2178767/abu-dhabi-explosion-live-uae-dubai> These have not been confirmed. Earlier this week, the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/136d9ad6-f427-4bea-bbb2-5df8fb5decec> reported that authorities in Dubai were threatening to take legal action against people posting fake claims about bomb strikes.
Lopez de Prado's uplifting post inspired various colleagues to express similar sentiments. "I concur. Well said!" said Alexander Lipton, ADIA's global head of quantitative research and development. "Completely agree," said Daniel Nehren, the former head of global cash equities e-trading at Citi in London, who moved to become the global head of quant research and development at ADIA in 2022. Bernd Scherer, ADIA's head of portfolio implementation, complimented Lopez de Prado and declared, "Abu Dhabi is safe. Arguing any different is a sign of distorted probability assessment."
It's not clear whether war in the Middle East will affect ADIA's investment strategy. Writing yesterday, the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/ab7d597d-5e72-4cbf-8d3b-53815695d68f> quoted an unnamed Gulf official who said the war could encourage Gulf countries to review overseas investments as they increase defence spending and face declining income from tourism and energy sales.
With Abu Dhabi under fire, it's thought that ADIA employees are working from home. A spokesperson for ADIA declined to comment on the guidance the fund has provided to its staff.
Speaking off the record, one ADIA quant told us "the show must go on" at ADIA. Another insider accused Lopez de Prado of emitting "misleading signals" and "kissing the ring."
"Well said and thank you for this post," said Saeed Al Mansouri, ADIA's deputy director, writing beneath Lopez de Prado's thoughts.
As we observed last year, </news/adia-pay>working for ADIA can be lucrative. Even technologists at Team Q are on $500k tax free salaries. Top quants there are thought to earn salaries alone of $3m or more. Pay grades were accidentally revealed when someone left a sheet in the ADIA printing room. </news/adia-quant-salaries>

item.title: Anthropic suggests AI might be worse for hedge fund employees than bankers
item.description:
Banks are still relying on their trusty analysts to build powerpoints and financial models, but will that change as AI becomes more capable? </news/ai-is-not-going-to-get-rid-of-18-hour-days-for-investment-bank-analysts> Anthropic, </news/ai-jobs-for-bankers> developers of the AI chatbot Claude, seems to think the rumors of the death of financial services jobs are greatly exaggerated. Some of them, at least...
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
In a report on the labour market <https://cdn.sanity.io/files/4zrzovbb/website/dc7bcd0224644fce97cecb7f9e68dcd8434b35f1.pdf> published by Anthropic this week, the AI lab looked at the theoretical exposure of various industries to AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job> and compared it to the observed exposure it had seen by users of Claude. 'Business and finance' jobs are, theoretically, some of the most exposed jobs, but Anthropic's perceived impact is less than a third of what's actually possible.
Source: Anthropic
Indeed, Anthropic suggests pretty much all industries are using AI less than they theoretically can. One of the biggest disparities between theory and practice is for management jobs, which presumably overlaps with senior banking jobs somewhat.
Not all jobs in finance are safe. Investment analysts </news/what-is-a-hedge-fund-analyst-a-day-in-the-life-of-an-equities-analyst-at-citadel> had a perceived exposure of 57.2%, making it the seventh most exposed role to AI that Anthropic assessed. This might suggest that hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> staff are more exposed than bankers, especially as major funds build out their own proprietary AI systems </news/hedge-funds-new-hiring-problem-too-many-ai-people-not-enough-everything-else> that do a lot of the grunt work like analyzing regulatory filings. Equity researchers </news/research-job-investment-bank> in banks may have similar cause for concern.
Technology jobs in finance </news/how-to-get-a-technology-job-bank> are also at risk; Anthropic observed that computer programmers had an observed exposure of 74.5%. Banking engineers may have seen this coming; as their employers brought out new AI development tools last year, it became popular to measure their output in 'developer hours reduced'. </news/developers-are-doubling-their-code-output-with-ai-is-that-a-bad-thing>
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: WhatsApp: http://wa.me/442079977910 <http://wa.me/442079977910> (+44 20 7997 7910), Telegram: @AlexMcMurray, Signal: @AlexMcMurrayEFC.88 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Goldman Sachs' & Barclays' juniors desperate bid to impress women goes awry. BlackRock's bad loan
item.description:
Ladies, do you like a man who works for Goldman Sachs? How about a man who works for Goldman Sachs, works out five or six days a week, plays the piano and likes to dance? If so, we can recommend Clay Nelson, a 25-year-old associate on Goldman's New York delta one desk. You can find him on Hinge.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
If not Clay, how about 24-year-old-Mason Clark, who currently has a girlfriend but who looks good pouting on a velvet chaise, works out six days a week, and says mediocrity is his idea of hell?
Alternatively, we can suggest Tommy Doherty Junior, a sultry 25-year-old FX trader at Barclays who wears a Rolex, works out four or five times a week and likes a cold plunge. You can find Tommy on Raya, the members only dating app for celebrities and influencers. If you're not on Raya, swing by the Hudson Hound, an old fashioned Irish pub on Manhattan's Hudson Street. He's often there Thursday nights.
If the junior bankers above sound appealing, would they still be so if they weren't actually working in banking any more? This is the risk after Clay, Mason and Tommy appeared in a photoshoot for Interview Magazine, titled 'Meet the Finest Boys in Finance <https://www.interviewmagazine.com/fashion/meet-the-finest-boys-in-finance>.'
What may have seemed like a great idea when Interview Magazine approached the three young bankers on LinkedIn, has turned out very definitely not to be. Clay and Tommy may get more attention on dating apps following their "baby faced" profiles in Interview, and Clay may find solace in new non-mediocre friends, but their employers are not impressed. A spokesman for Goldman Sachs told Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-06/goldman-junior-banker-fashion-shoot-sets-off-blame-game-at-firm>the firm didn't approve their appearances. Barclays declined to comment on Doherty's comments, but he seemed to realise the error of his ways and the latest iteration of the article online has deleted his name.
It's not clear what the three young bankers' intentions were they agreed to the request from a random journalist. Meeting women may have been subsidiary to appearing in a highly styled photo shoot while flashing a Rolex. Either way, the exercise has badly backfired. As banking influencers </news/bankers-influencers-consulting> on TikTok and elsewhere are finding out, banks don't much like their juniors posting about their lives on social media, let alone appearing in a photoshoot, bragging that they've spent $3k on a jacket. It may be a lesson painfully learned.
Separately, Goldman Sachs' CEO David Solomon says not to worry too much about private credit markets. “We’re watching very closely to see if there’s been a little bit too much aggression, frothiness,” said Solomon yesterday. <https://seekingalpha.com/news/4561061-goldman-sachs-ceo-david-solomon-watching-for-frothiness-in-private-credit-market?mailingid=44501964&messageid=2900&position=rta_news_bankr_hysacopy_fullspelling_main_0_title&serial=44501964.3745&source=email_2900&utm_campaign=rta-stock-news&utm_content=link-1&utm_source=seeking_alpha&utm_term=44501964.3745>“While there have been a bunch of idiosyncratic events where there have been problems, the broad portfolios are performing reasonably well.”
One of those idiosyncratic events has occurred at BlackRock, which Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-05/blackrock-slashes-another-private-loan-value-from-100-to-zero?taid=69a978cbf7cd120001a051b4&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter> notes has slashed a loan to zero, three months after marking it at 100 cents on the dollar.
The loan is small and worth a mere $25m, but value is not the point. As Bloomberg notes, such a sudden and dramatic markdown highlights the lag between the valuation of illiquid loans and the true state of the companies receiving them (in this case, an Amazon aggregator).
Solomon is hopefully heeding the words of his predecessor. Ex-Goldman Sachs CEO Lloyd Blankfein told the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/6f711748-f6ea-4788-a89a-db743c63f137>this week that Goldman avoided large losses in the last financial crisis by "very aggressively marking to market" and making its people sell things just to see if they could. Regularly marking to market isn't the intention with illiquid private credit loans, but hard headed realism might help. As BlackRock's 100% mark down shows, thing can change in a period of months.
Meanwhile....
Market Financial Solutions, </news/apollo-loss-mfs> the mortgage company that's collapsed into insolvency, leaving banks with hundreds of millions in losses, threw staff parties costing hundreds of thousands of pounds. Last Christmas, there was a party at the five-star Peninsula Hotel where workers watched a pair of dancers perform inside a giant glass dome. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-05/mfs-ceo-spent-on-artwork-parties-indian-rapper-before-collapse?accessToken=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzb3VyY2UiOiJTdWJzY3JpYmVyR2lmdGVkQXJ0aWNsZSIsImlhdCI6MTc3MjcxNjIyOSwiZXhwIjoxNzczMzIxMDI5LCJhcnRpY2xlSWQiOiJUQkMxSVBUOTZPU00wMCIsImJjb25uZWN0SWQiOiIwMTY3RUNERjE0QUE0QUYzQjU0ODhCQThDNjBCQkY5NCJ9.mQy3Rj3ecCNRfoITb8RyDqoGtwcsxKs4QCbCtSQPuoA>
Asif Goodall, the head of markets for EMEA at Mizuho and an ex-portfolio manager at Polus Capital Management, says he reviewed an investment in MFS in 2019 and turned it down. "The portfolio managers identified that some loans were being moved between SPVs. When queried, they were told the assets were being “re‑bridged”. This had not been disclosed upfront and naturally raised further questions around asset valuations and liquidity." (LinkedIn) <https://www.linkedin.com/posts/asif-godall_the-recent-news-about-the-collapse-of-lender-share-7435276731684626432-S2CQ?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAC_n9ABKGyf486Jq5_52e4WCygx5h9EouQ>
If you receive a message from a colleague on WhatsApp saying "What's up?", don't answer it. It's a Phishing attempt by your employer. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/banks-run-fire-drills-as-whatsapp-ban-continues-to-vex-compliance-teams-018c3d50>
Significant risk transfers are all the rage. Banks issued $41bn of SRTs in 2025, up from $29bn in 2024 and they're up another 20% this year. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/80bfb23c-315f-4b61-b966-aa04c81de69a>
It's not easy being a macro trader now. “I’m not even sure Trump knows what Trump thinks, let alone a macro fund.” (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/macro-hedge-funds-struggle-to-find-diversified-trades-amid-middle-east-conflict-39fa89f9>
It's not easy selling a leveraged loan now. “If you miss a week — as we may have just done — that can mean the difference between executing what you want to execute and not executing. We’re currently in a time where hitting that market window has become that much more important...If you’re not ready to launch in 24 hours. you’re not ready.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-05/jpmorgan-s-3-500-person-levfin-event-jolted-by-war-reality-check?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260305&utm_campaign=author_20461763>
JPMorgan was the sole financer of the $55bn Electronic Arts Inc leveraged buyout last year. It's since syndicated the loan. Next week, it and other banks will attempt to sell a $10.5bn offering to the US market and €4bn to Europe. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-05/ea-buyout-banks-eye-4-billion-10-5-billion-split-in-debt-sale?taid=69a95a64cd818f0001220b42&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Taproot Management, the hedge fund aiming to deliver multistrategy returns without expensive portfolio managers, has lost money. (Business Insider) <https://www.businessinsider.com/taproot-management-hedge-fund-performance-2026-3>
The cost of living in the UAE now involves a private jet to fly in and out of the UK so as not to exceed the number of residency days that will trigger a UK tax bill. Spending more than 183 days in a country typically results in becoming tax resident in that country. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/80bfb23c-315f-4b61-b966-aa04c81de69a>
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: How was your bonus for 2025? We’d like to know
item.description:
Have you been told your bonus for 2025 yet? How was it, if so?
Over 2,500 people responded to our Salary & Bonus Survey for 2024-2025 last year across the front, middle, and back offices <https://efinancialcareers.com/news/finance/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking>. When we have collated this year's compensation data we will produce and share our 2025-2026 Salary & Bonus Report. We will also write about our findings here from March.

item.title: The Lehman Brothers traders leading hedge fund Squarepoint's new market maker
item.description:
The electronic market making </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay#:~:text=Electronic%20Market%20Making,hedge%20their%20risk.> space may look very different in a few years' time. New entrants backed by major firms are appearing. They include STG Securities, a spinout of hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>Squarepoint that will have a similar relationship to the fund that Citadel Securities has to Citadel </news/2022/10/how-to-get-a-job-at-citadel>. It's being led by very tenured investment bank traders. </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-04/squarepoint-affiliate-starts-market-making-arm-to-rival-citadel?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260304&utm_campaign=author_19908303> reported yesterday that STG securities is led by Kirill Gellman, an ex-Lehman Brothers </news/investment-bank-associates-today-earn-less-than-the-weakest-2007-lehman-brothers-associates> trader who made the jump to Barclays after its bankruptcy. He ran an automated options market making business at the UK bank up until 2020, when the unit was sold to market making firm GTS. His team appears to have been sold to STG in 2025; Gellman has been registered with the STG on FINRA since last October. STG's FINRA profile says the market maker's head of trading will be Eric Levine, another Lehman refugee at Barclays. Alok Mukherjee, who joined Lehman Brothers </news/basis-trade-hedge-funds> in 2007, is also registered with STG. Other Lehman alums linked to the unit include Alex Shleimovich, head of volatility market making technology at Squarepoint. It's a reunion of sorts; Squarepoint initially spun out of Lehman Brothers' prop trading arm.
STG is not just Lehman alums though; other FINRA registered personnel have come from Morgan Stanley, hedge fund Walleye and Two Sigma.
Competing with the likes of Citadel Securities and Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street>is a tall order. Electronic market makers generate revenue by quoting buyers one price, sellers another, and pocketing the difference; The profits from individual trades are small, so these firms often need to operate on a massive scale. Citadel Securities, for example, is involved in ~25% <https://www.citadelsecurities.com/what-we-do/equities/#:~:text=~35%25,volume%20executed3>of all US equities trades. This usually requires a firm to have a lot of capital at its disposal; Bloomberg reports that Squarepoint's AUM comes mostly from its principals and employees, rather than external investors, which might benefit STG as it grows.
As STG competes with the likes of Citadel Securities in the market making space, another new firm is competing with its execution services business. Optimal Market Technologies <https://www.thetradenews.com/optiver-and-virtu-backed-optimal-market-technologies-options-execution-platform-launches/>, backed by Optiver and Virtu Financial, is building an options execution platform under the leadership of Brian Donnelly, an ex-UBS MD and founder of its electronic volatility trading division. The firmis expected to launch the platform this quarter.

item.title: Hedge fund Brevan Howard suffered the departure of its head of quant analysis
item.description:
Brevan Howard, the multistrategy hedge fund </news/big-multistrategy-hedge-funds>that's been hiking graduate pay </news/hedge-fund-brevan-howard-hiked-graduate-pay-from-90k-to-150k> and hiring-in inflation traders </news/citi-s-top-inflation-trader-is-joining-brevan-howard-goldman-s-is-a-man-of-mystery>has also been losing technologists </news/hedge-fund-brevan-howard-parted-with-company-with-7-senior-technologists> and now quants. </news/quant-jobs-finance>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Chris Willis, the head of quantitative analysis at Brevan Howard, is understood to the latest to go away. Multiple sources say that Willis resigned this week. Brevan Howard declined to comment.
Willis has worked for Brevan Howard since 2012, when he joined as quant analyst. He spent five years at Coremont, the Brevan Howard spin-out which provides portfolio managers with portfolio management software, data and managed services. Brevan Howard insiders say that Willis was a key figure in the unit.
However, Brevan Howard has focused on bringing its technology back in-house since 2023. Willis returned to work for Brevan during that same year.
As we reported last September, Brevan Howard has lost at least seven </news/hedge-fund-brevan-howard-parted-with-company-with-7-senior-technologists> of its most senior technology professionals in recent years. Now, Willis has left too.
Brevan Howard hired Mike Sanders </news/brevan-howard-the-latest-hedge-fund-to-sign-a-cto-from-banking-this-time-from-jp-morgan> from JPMorgan as its head of technology in 2024. Willis, who didn't respond to a request to comment for this article, is understood to have reported to Sanders. Sanders has hired some of his own people. They include Tim Mace from Man Group, who joined as head of AI last November; Joey Allcock, from Goldman Sachs as COO; Valdo Durrleman </news/valdo-durrleman-brevan-howard>, the former head of desk strats at deceased hedge fund Eisler, who joined in January.
Brevan Howard has various technologists among its staff in Abu Dhabi. It's also building a technology hub in India. </news/hedge-fund-india-hiring-brevan-howard>As the Indian hub grows, the Abu Dhabi team has been gently trimmed. </news/brevan-howard-abu-dhabi-jobs>

item.title: As banking clamps down on influencers, consulting pays its interns to post on LinkedIn
item.description:
If you work in banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> and have an active social media presence, you may have noticed a crackdown on 'finfluencers' in recent years. If you're still intent on broadcasting yourself to the world, you may want to consider a switch to consulting. </news/working-for-mckinsey-bain-and-boston-consulting-vs-goldman-jpm-and-morgan-stanley>
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Insiders claim that Big Four </news/working-for-the-big-four-pwc-deloitte-ey-kpmg> firm PwC has offered to pay interns </news/internship-investment-bank> to post positive things about their experience on LinkedIn. The offer was allegedly to its interns in its most recent summer cohort that received return offers, and was at a rate of £10 per post. PwC did not respond to a request for comment.
Not everyone wants to be an influencer on LinkedIn, and £10 might not be enough to tempt them (or to buy lunch for that matter), but you can also post for the thrill of it on TikTok. Consultants are busy there. For example, Claudette Mae, an influencer with over 20k TikTok followers revealed in a post this week her full-title and company (engagement manager at Oliver Wyman), as well as her bonus. She previously spent over four years in JPMorgan's payments team; though her account existed before joining, she made very few posts until after she left, none of which mentioned the bank.
Banks tend to be less ecstatic about posting on social media. Rachel Wiseman, formerly a TMT banking analyst at Goldman Sachs, said in a recent TikTok that the bank has been discouraging analysts from making day-in-the-life content and has been keeping an eye on its staff that moonlight as influencers since mid-2024. "Unfortunately, if you want to work in finance and have a long term career, it's generally frowned upon to have a social media presence," she said. Goldman Sachs declined to comment.
Different banks seem to have different policies; Bank of America employee Juliet McKay </news/juliet-mackay-notbadjuju> has had various viral day-in-the-life videos which she still publishes, although she no longer works within its investment banking division. Dilan Abeya </news/citi-hired-jp-morgan-s-instagram-influencer-and-equities-trader>, a former sales trader across various banks, said on a podcast last year <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-bIzInDWjvM&t=1994s> that one former employer held "six global conference calls about [his] Instagram account" once it became too popular, discussing whether he should delete it.
You'll notice that most influencers who make content about banking either hide their name/bank, or they'll only make content after they've left the industry. One RBC associate </news/junior-banker-at-rbc-complains-of-3-hours-sleep-on-tik-tok-attracts-attention-of-hr> who did the former got millions of views on videos complaining about her lack of sleep. After the bank's HR was made aware of the account, it was made private. Jonathan Tristan </news/ex-investment-banker-that-worked-16-hour-days-i-m-happy-i-did-it-because-i-m-on-the-other-side>, formerly of Deutsche Bank, has done the latter. He managed to grow a 250k+ follower base discussing finance careers on TikTok, having left the industry in 2021.
You might gain more freedom once you get more senior. Ex-Goldman Sachs MD Rebecca Anderton-Davies </news/2023/04/goldman-sachs-work-life-balance> amassed almost 90k Instagram followers while working at the bank, although she hasn't posted since 2023. Graham 'G-Dog' Ambrose, another Goldman MD, has amassed over 20,000 Instagram followers as a 56-year-old fitness influencer. </news/graham-ambrose-goldman-sachs-fitness>
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>

Consultants are busy there. For example, Claudette Mae, an influencer with over 20k TikTok followers revealed in a post this week her full-title and company (engagement manager at Oliver Wyman), as well as her bonus. She previously spent over four years in JPMorgan's payments team; though her account existed before joining, she made very few posts until after she left, none of which mentioned the bank.
Banks tend to be less ecstatic about posting on social media. Rachel Wiseman, formerly a TMT banking analyst at Goldman Sachs, said in a recent TikTok that the bank has been discouraging analysts from making day-in-the-life content and has been keeping an eye on its staff that moonlight as influencers since mid-2024. "Unfortunately, if you want to work in finance and have a long term career, it's generally frowned upon to have a social media presence," she said. Goldman Sachs declined to comment.
Different banks seem to have different policies; Bank of America employee Juliet McKay </news/juliet-mackay-notbadjuju> has had various viral day-in-the-life videos which she still publishes, although she no longer works within its investment banking division. Dilan Abeya </news/citi-hired-jp-morgan-s-instagram-influencer-and-equities-trader>, a former sales trader across various banks, said on a podcast last year <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-bIzInDWjvM&t=1994s> that one former employer held "six global conference calls about [his] Instagram account" once it became too popular, discussing whether he should delete it.
You'll notice that most influencers who make content about banking either hide their name/bank, or they'll only make content after they've left the industry. One RBC associate </news/junior-banker-at-rbc-complains-of-3-hours-sleep-on-tik-tok-attracts-attention-of-hr> who did the former got millions of views on videos complaining about her lack of sleep. After the bank's HR was made aware of the account, it was made private. Jonathan Tristan </news/ex-investment-banker-that-worked-16-hour-days-i-m-happy-i-did-it-because-i-m-on-the-other-side>, formerly of Deutsche Bank, has done the latter. He managed to grow a 250k+ follower base discussing finance careers on TikTok, having left the industry in 2021.
You might gain more freedom once you get more senior. Ex-Goldman Sachs MD Rebecca Anderton-Davies </news/2023/04/goldman-sachs-work-life-balance> amassed almost 90k Instagram followers while working at the bank, although she hasn't posted since 2023. Graham 'G-Dog' Ambrose, another Goldman MD, has amassed over 20,000 Instagram followers as a 56-year-old fitness influencer. </news/graham-ambrose-goldman-sachs-fitness>

item.title: Morning Coffee: Morgan Stanley is cutting jobs, Goldman Sachs will probably be next. The secret police at Citi
item.description:
It's always been the case that investment banks </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> cut jobs in the final quarter of the year - maybe even directly before bonuses </news/hsbc-fired-more-bankers-before-bonuses-it-s-harsh-and-inhumane#:~:text=It%20is%20now%20a%20ruthless,it%20announces%20bonuses%20for%202025.&text=Bloomberg%20reported%20the%20cuts%20yesterday.> in the case of HSBC - but trimming in the opening quarters of the year is also a thing. Morgan Stanley </news/morgan-stanley-job-cuts-2025>and Goldman Sachs </news/as-goldman-sachs-fires-underperforming-v-ps-time-in-office-is-a-criteria> did it in 2025 and now we understand that Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs will do it again in 2026.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/morgan-stanley-lays-off-2-500-employees-across-all-divisions-5e6d4b25?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqeymXTlOVc6KIPu5f9SDGf1hQvLx6xnynfxqOyqPGC80jJsAmM18MhIAkX_hnE%3D&gaa_ts=69a92705&gaa_sig=oJGNJsZqdzHdlPYCcGLFWILKqyNTrZD3OLZjZazzkPU2ZSv6aXdyMyujSl1H9GIsEC-gX5YSzcwXrKkbJ8iMpQ%3D%3D> reports that Morgan Stanley is cutting 2,500 people across investment banking and trading, wealth management and investment management. Many of the cuts already happened last Wednesday. They were partly the result of 'shifting business location,' which typically means middle and back office jobs being moved to low cost regions overseas.
Goldman Sachs' coming job cuts have yet to be reported, but insiders at the firm tell us they're coming too, with cuts likely in the next few months. Goldman Sachs declined to comment.
Last year, Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs and Bank of America </news/morgan-stanley-cutting-jobs>all announced job cuts in March. Each found themselves with an excess of people, in part due to existing employees' unwillingness to resign. This year, insiders at Goldman Sachs tell us the "churn" has once again been poor, despite some engineering and operations staff receiving poor bonuses in an effort to encourage them on their way. </news/everything-we-know-about-2026-bonuses-by-bank>
After an outstanding 2025, banks were initially ebullient </news/cant-get-an-m-and-a-job>about 2026. Since last Saturday, though, things have changed. America and Israel's war with Iran risks delaying M&A deals and IPOs, increasing inflation and postponing interest rate cuts, and creating the sort of unforeseen volatility that contributes to trading losses (and maybe gains). This is being combined with the rising tide of AI and reported write downs and withdrawals in private credit. As ex-Goldman Sachs CEO Lloyd Blankfein </news/lloyd-blankfein-goldman-sachs>quipped, "the horses are whinnying in the corral" for the next financial crisis.
Hopefully that instinct is wrong. Either way, these small rounds will not be the only cuts we see this year.
Separately, the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/dddf2192-5550-4a22-976b-07d81021a5ea> reports Citi has got a unit Citi Security and Investigative Services (CSIS), which is deployed to investigate accounts of employee wrongdoing.
While this sounds good in theory, in fact insiders at the bank tell the FT that CSIS seems more intent on using its internal police force against its own employees who report wrongdoing than against the perpetrators. Multiple employees interviewed by the CSIS tell the FT that their interviews were subsequently used by the bank to defend itself in legal cases. They include Ardith Lindsey, </news/citi-equities-insiders-on-the-real-problem-london-management-poor-promotions> the Citi MD who claims to have been subject to sexism, harassment and assault there.
Citi told the FT that its CSIS team, "consists of highly experienced professionals whose responsibilities include supporting Citi disaster response efforts, protection for colleagues around the world and looking into certain concerns about colleague behaviour along a clearly laid-out set of guidelines.”
Meanwhile...
Barclays is owed $700m by collapsed mortgage lender Market Financial Solutions Ltd. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-04/barclays-owed-about-500-million-by-collapsed-uk-lender-mfs>
The war in the Middle East has upended popular trades at the start of 2026. Asian and European equities and have suffered losses, as has gold. Wall Street indices and the US dollar are buoyant. “The US will be in a better position to cushion any blow from sharply rising energy prices...Asia, Europe . . . they are all net energy importers”. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/2c6529e0-41cb-4c2e-957d-04fb9641d807>
Banks are having second thoughts about expanding in the Middle East. “The main thing we want to see is stability. The worst thing for us is to be in places where we don’t have visibility over the next two to three years and where there could be massive change.” (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/amp/articles/a-rude-awakening-how-the-iran-conflict-could-dent-banks-middle-east-growth-plans-3792c7db>
Companies are worried about IPOs. “For IPOs to come to market, it’s really going to be a question of sector exposure...It’s also going to be a matter of whether you have a deal where the foundations have been laid so you can be in and out of the market very quickly.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-02/middle-east-conflict-pressures-ipo-candidates-to-get-deals-done?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=linkedin>
An affliliate of Squarepoint is starting a new electronic-trading unit called STG Securities, which will operate a market maker alongside a hedge fund business. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-04/squarepoint-affiliate-starts-market-making-arm-to-rival-citadel?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260304&utm_campaign=author_19908303>
Marc Rowan at Apollo doesn't like Jeffrey Epstein. “I didn’t like him for my own reasons, he wasted my time. Even from the grave, he’s wasting my time.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-03/apollo-s-rowan-says-epstein-wasting-my-time-from-the-grave?srnd=phx-industries-finance>
Abderdeen Asset Management cut Bloomberg terminals and other vendor products to save £150m in costs. We were buying too much and we weren’t buying smart enough. We’ve looked at contracts, reset them and tightened them up.” (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/fewer-bloomberg-terminals-and-vendor-contracts-how-aberdeen-cut-180m-in-costs-5aedaa4f?mod=homepage>
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: Hong Kong Global Talent Summit Week – meeting the challenges of a changing market
item.description:
The world economy and the global talent market are in a paradoxical position at the start of 2026. On one hand, the possibilities opened up by artificial intelligence and the pace of technological change make the future of work even more uncertain than usual. But on the other hand, realising those same possibilities will require investment and skilled human capital. Talent acquisition specialists have the job of navigating this uncertainty, while every professional worker needs to think about how to adapt to the new reality.
In this new environment, Hong Kong strives to reinforce its position in the front rank of economic centres, regionally and globally. The city has always been a key hub for Asia, benefiting from proximity to the Chinese economy coupled with a common law legal system and a diverse English-speaking business community. Last year, Hong Kong jumped from ninth to fourth <https://www.info.gov.hk/gia/general/202509/09/P2025090800827.htm> in the International Institute for Management Development’s World Talent ranking, making it the number one location in Asia in terms of competitiveness for top global talent. The financial centre was the global leader for IPO capital raised, and as part of the Shenzhen-Hong Kong-Guangzhou cluster, topped the World Intellectual Property Organisation’s innovation rankings.
Hong Kong has always been the place for international talent to fast-track a career. There are more regional offices and subsidiaries of international companies in the city than any other in Asia, and an active expat community. Anyone hoping to develop truly global executive experience will find that there are unparalleled opportunities in Hong Kong, as the fast growth of the local and regional market coupled with an entrepreneurial culture mean that promotions and personal development happen at a faster pace.
In order to support global professionals considering relocation, the Hong Kong government set up Hong Kong Talent Engage (HKTE) in 2023. This office is tasked with highlighting Hong Kong’s role and advantages as an international talent hub and the country's gateway for talent, providing a single central point of contact for all the needs of high level international professionals. As well as promotions and job fairs, HKTE provides ongoing advice and support for lifestyle and career needs.
Global Talent Summit Week
This sets the stage for the Global Talent Summit Week, a flagship series of events, organised by the Labour and Welfare Bureau and HKTE with the aim of showcasing Hong Kong as a destination for top global talent. It is centred on the International Talent Forum on the 18th and 19th of March, along with the CareerConnect Expo taking place on the same dates, surrounded by nine “satellite” events over the course of the week focused on specific themes and sectors. As Felix Chan, the Director of HKTE, puts it, this week is a “signature event, to send a very strong signal to the world”.
The Director of HKTE, Felix Chan.
The International Talent Forum will take place for an invited live audience and streamed online in real time. Forum speakers will include business leaders, along with panelists representing the technology, finance, education and other key sectors, with a keynote address from Nobel Prize-winning economist Professor Christopher Pissarides. The forum aims to provide thought leadership and inspire forward-looking perspectives, as experts on technology, education and talent from around the region and the globe discuss the current environment and its opportunities.
At the same time, the CareerConnect Expo, also taking place in the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre will comprise five different themed zones to support professional advancement. As well as live showcases and networking opportunities with exhibitors, the Expo will give access to HKTE’s one-stop support services to help global professionals to integrate and advance in the city. There will also be opportunities to connect with Hong Kong’s universities for global talent interested in taking advantage of the world-class education system. A special Technology Zone focuses on the cross-regional opportunities in Hong Kong’s innovation and technology ecosystem.
The week is filled out by a large number of satellite events organised by bodies including ACCA, Cyberport and Hong Kong Talents Group. These events highlight specific opportunities and sectors, while allowing global talent professionals to network and discuss the changing trends, challenges and opportunities for the future.
Heading for Hong Kong
The CareerConnect Expo is open to the public, along with nine satellite events, creating a great opportunity for global talent in other centres to consider making a visit in person. As Felix Chan, says, “It is about making the connection. By coming here into Hong Kong, people can see for themselves what Hong Kong is – the great environment here, the new world-class Kaitak stadium, the promenade along the Victoria Harbour, and also make friends and connections… at the macro level and at the personal level, I would aim to make use of the GTS Week to showcase Hong Kong and to help those to make the decision to come to the city.”
For people who do not have the ability to make the trip on this occasion, the International Talent Forum will be broadcast online, and HKTE organises regular virtual events throughout the year. However, for global professionals who are seriously considering a career move, there is hardly a better time to explore Hong Kong.
Details of the Global Talent Summit Week are available on its dedicated website at https://gts.hkengage.gov.hk/en <https://gts.hkengage.gov.hk/en>. Now is the time to register at https://gts.hkengage.gov.hk/register/attendee/public?lang=en <https://gts.hkengage.gov.hk/register/attendee/public?lang=en> and get ready to make the first steps on a career journey to the most exciting centre for global talent.
item.author: editor@efinancialcareers.com (Hong Kong Talent Engage)

item.title: Why a target school really matters when applying to banking internships
item.description:
If you're interested in a career in banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank>, you'll have no doubt heard that there are 'target schools </news/the-top-100-universities-globally-for-careers-in-banking-and-finance>' which banks love to hire from. What does that mean, really?
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Ben Chon, a former JPMorgan investment bank analyst runs an educational YouTube channel and advises students on getting careers in banking. In a video published yesterday <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sZz_wOSroD4>, Chon revealed that US target schools will have "a team of 5 to ten bankers that lead recruiting" at most major banks. Those teams are allocated a certain number of spots for internship superdays </news/2016/07/make-the-most-of-an-investment-banking-superday> that they're allowed to fill exclusively with candidates from their school. "If you do go to a target school, the fastest, most efficient way to get an offer is by speaking with those bankers leading recruiting," he said.
Chon, who left JPMorgan in 2018, worked on one of those recruiting teams for UC Berkeley in California. He said that recruitment teams are very invested in their candidates, and want to send out their "best gladiators to battle hoping they [will] demolish the competition."
Which schools are targets? It depends on the bank </news/2020/08/best-universities-for-banking-jobs>; Chon said that some banks build recruiting pipelines with non-target schools </news/2023/04/good-banking-universities> that their senior bankers originated from. Some schools, however, you can expect to be a universal target. Speaking in an AMA on The Bubble <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/t/ama-goldman-sachs-investment-banking-analyst-available-to-answer-your-questions-on-getting-the-job/5139/11>, our new anonymous forum, an ex-Goldman Sachs </news/2016/03/the-best-universities-for-getting-a-job-at-goldman-sachs-in-the-u-s-europe-and-asia-within-ma-trading-risk-and-ops> banker said that the five best target schools in the US are Harvard, Princeton, NYU Stern, Duke University and the University of Chicago.
The process is slightly different in Europe and the UK. On-premise campus recruitment teams are less prevalent but banks will host more student events at target schools where a lot of the leg work for recruitment takes place. Trackr suggests </news/target-universities-finance-uk> that the best UK target schools are Imperial, Oxford, Cambridge, LSE, UCL and Warwick. Bocconi is your best option in mainland Europe.
Getting an interview is much harder at a non-target school. In the US, Chon said applicants from these schools are "grouped into one really large pool of candidates." The amount of available superday spots will vary from bank to bank, but the acceptance rate for non-target applicants will be significantly lower. In Europe, Trackr's summer internship report <https://the-trackr.com/blog/summer-internship-season-report-2025/> last year said that the average target school applicant received 0.87 offers while the average non-target applicant received just 0.47 offers.
The ex-Goldman banker said that applicants from non-target schools "need as much of a leg up as possible." One of the best places to start is by joining your school's finance society. "It’s a great network and oftentimes that’s the place where banks will pick from," he said. This is equally true at target schools; since recruiting leads are often juniors, befriending them while they're still students could give you an edge.
Whether you go to a target or not, there are things anyone can do to improve their chances. Chon said that the best applicants "sent hundreds of emails to set up chats" with alumni of their school working in banks, and had in-person conversations with up to 50 of them. Chon said that candidates who impressed him most over coffee chats would always make it to first round interviews "as long as they also had really strong resumes." If this all doesn't work, the Goldman banker said that a "[target school] MBA </news/best-mba-for-finance-banking>is your best bet."

item.title: Macro trader's post-Australian hedge fund odyssey continues in London
item.description:
Brent Cook has travelled far. Having worked for Citadel as the London-based head of macro trading in the dark days of 2005, he went to banks (Deutsche Bank, ANZ, Macquarie) and spent five years in Australia. Cook returned to London </news/2021/08/brent-cook-nomura> to work for Nomura in 2021, had his head turned by the unfortunate Eisler Capital </news/eisler-capital>in 2022 and fled to Schonfeld in 2023. Now he's moved on again.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Cook joined hedge fund Fulcrum Asset Management on Monday. He's understood to be a macro portfolio manager there, managing a combination of external and Fulcrum assets.
Fulcrum was founded in 2004 by another veteran of the financial crisis and beyond: Gavyn Davies the former chief economist of Goldman Sachs. It runs a combination of discretionary and quantitative strategies, coupled with "alternative solutions."
Cook isn't the only person at Fulcrum receiving money from outside investors. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-12-12/millennium-backs-two-hedge-fund-managers-with-over-3-billion> reported in December that Millennium management had given $2.3bn to Fulcrum partner Fawaz Chaudhry to manage in a separately managed account focused on a long short equity strategy.
As markets reprice inflation expectations in light of the unexpected war, experienced traders like Cook may be popular. Macro strategies can be volatile, though, so trading on a smaller platform like Fulcrum may make more sense than working for the sorts of large multistrategy funds </news/big-multistrategy-hedge-funds>which don't always tolerate large drawdowns.

item.title: Hedge fund Millennium hired an ex-Goldman Sachs strat MD from Citadel
item.description:
Hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>Citadel </news/citadel-hedge-fund> likes to hire people from Goldman Sachs. So does hedge fund Millennium. </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium>It's natural, then, that someone who left Goldman Sachs for the former hedge fund may be sought after by the latter. One Goldman quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> who did just that has emerged at Millennium in New York.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Samuel Krasnik worked for Citadel as a quant research </news/what-is-a-quant-researcher-hedge-fund> lead for three years at Goldman Sachs. He has been out of the market for two years on garden leave, but this month joined rival fund Millennium as a quant researcher. Krasnik spent 12 years at Goldman Sachs, joining in 2009 and making MD in 2017.
At Goldman, Krasnik was a quant strat, its famous, highly commercial take on quantitative developers. Specifically, Krasnik led the FAST (franchise analytics strategy and technology) team </news/2017/03/the-nine-top-technology-teams-at-goldman-sachs#:~:text=2.%20The%20FAST%20analytics%20team%C2%A0> within global markets. Under Krasnik, the team developed data and analytics tools for its front-office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> traders to use, often involving AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job> techniques like natural language processing.
Krasnik isn't the only recent arrival from Goldman Sachs. The FT reported </news/goldman-sachs-partners-leaving> late last week that the fund had hired partner and co-head of equities Erdit Hoxha.
Citadel and Millennium seem to like Goldman Sachs alumni for slightly different reasons. Citadel founder Ken Griffin previously praised Goldman's staff </news/2023/10/citadel-goldman-sachs-114>for their communication skills and said he tried to emulate that aspect of its culture at Citadel. Millennium insiders previously told the FT </news/2023/11/goldman-sachs-to-hedge-funds-millennium-and-citadel> that Millennium founder Izzy Englander "likes the concept of committees... [Goldman people] tend to respect hierarchies, and they are used to co-heads and committees.”
Millennium declined to comment.

item.title: HSBC's MD promotions are no longer chaotic, but still not simple
item.description:
HSBC promoted some managing directors (MDs). Sadly, we do not have the full list of the 53 new MDs across the bank, but we do know the names of several in the debt capital markets (DCM business).
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
HSBC isn't commenting but sources say the MD promotions include: Alex Wheal, a London DCM banker; Gabriel Sibony, the head of US private placements; Chonghan Goh, the Singapore-based head of debt capital markets for South East Asia; and Daniel Porto, the New York-based head of Latin American corporate and structured debt origination.
All have toiled long and hard for HSBC: Wheal joined nearly 17 years ago; Sibony joined nearly 13 years ago; Goh has spent his whole career there; Porto arrived in 2016.
The new MDs will join the ranks of HSBC's material risk takers, who earned an average of $1.4m for last year. </news/hsbc-is-paying-senior-bankers-who-stay-1-4m-and-senior-bankers-who-go-500k> HSBC usually promotes its new MDs in March, but in 2025 it delayed promotions until June </news/hsbc-promoted-some-managing-directors-but-there-was-confusion>after ruthlessly cutting non-DCM </news/hscb-closure-europe-us-job-cuts>bankers in Europe and the US as it restructured the bank and pivoted to Asia and the Middle East.
Last year, insiders told us HSBC's MD promotions involved confusion as people who'd been told they were promoted in February were not promoted after all in June. This year, we are not aware of similar chaos, but multiple sources say the DCM team is mired in disgruntlement after bonuses were announced.
DCM bonuses were strong </news/hey-hsbc-s-us-bonuses-were-really-great-actually>at HSBC for last year, but insiders say not all DCM bankers there were treated equally. HSBC's syndicate bankers were allegedly paid more handsomely than its origination bankers who in turn claim that they're undervalued and have been unfairly </news/hsbc-s-dcm-people-are-slipping-away-before-bonuses-are-paid>removed from their jobs. "We're always run by bankers from syndicate," grumbles one origination insider.
Adam Bothamley, the bank's latest global head of DCM, spent 19 years in syndicate before moving into a leadership position in 2019. Even if Bothamley's bonuses were not the best in origination, bankers in the team can at least console themselves that they benefitted in his promotions.

item.title: Citadel Securities staff in London are appreciated by banks and trading firms
item.description:
Staff at electronic market maker </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay#:~:text=Electronic%20Market%20Making,hedge%20their%20risk.> Citadel Securities aren't just appreciated by contemporaries in the algorithmic trading space. Hedge funds </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>want a piece too.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Millennium, </news/millennium-hedge-fund-pay-london> for example, hired Citadel Securities portfolio manager Omar Gzouli as a senior PM this month. He spent two and a half years at the trading firm, joining initially from Chris Rokos' hedge fund, Rokos Capital Management, where he was a partner and portfolio manager. Gzouli was also an MD at Barclays, working in equity derivatives.
Meanwhile, London-based electronic trading firm G-Research </news/g-research-pay> hired Citadel Securities quant researcher Janmartin Jahn. He spent far longer at Citadel Securities, seven and a half years, and was EMEA head of its systematic internalizer business. <https://www.thetradenews.com/citadel-securities-registers-as-systematic-internaliser/> Like Gzouli, Jahn had a banking background; he was a VP in systematic equities market making at Bank of America, and a quant analyst in Citi's equities algorithmic trading team.
The pair leave Citadel Securities after what was a great year for the firm. It was on track for its largest ever yearly revenues <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-12-01/citadel-securities-trading-revenue-jumps-9-amid-competition> in 2025, and was on track to pay an average of $2m </news/citadel-securities-pay-2025> globally to its employees. In London, the year prior, it paid ~$1.1m. </news/hft-pay-london>
